A hypervisor cannot manage hardware resources ever
explanation: A hypervisor creates and manages virtual machines, allowing multiple operating systems to run on the same physical hardware.

text: What is the key advantage of time-sharing over batch processing?
options: Batch processing provides faster interactive response
Time-sharing eliminates the need for any memory
Time-sharing provides interactive response to users
Time-sharing consumes less CPU than batch does here
explanation: Time-sharing provides interactive response to multiple users by rapidly switching the CPU among processes.

text: What role does the DMA controller play in an operating system?
options: It compiles source code into machine language
It schedules processes for execution on processors
It manages user authentication and access control
It transfers data between memory and I/O directly
explanation: The DMA controller transfers data directly between I/O devices and memory without CPU intervention, improving performance.

text: Which statement about system programs is correct?
options: They replace the need for an operating system here
They provide convenient environment for development
System programs only include text editor programs
System programs are the same as the OS kernel
explanation: System programs provide a convenient environment for program development and execution, including utilities and tools.

text: What is the trap instruction used for in operating systems?
options: To physically trap faulty hardware in the system
To prevent users from logging into the system
To switch from user mode to kernel mode safely
To catch and eliminate computer virus threats
explanation: A trap is used to switch from user mode to kernel mode when a user program needs to invoke an OS service via a system call.

text: What is the difference between policy and mechanism in OS design?
options: They are interchangeable terms with the same meaning
Policy refers to hardware; mechanism to software here
Mechanism decides what; policy decides how it is done
Policy decides what to do; mechanism decides how
explanation: Policy determines what should be done while mechanism determines how it is done in OS design.

text: What happens during a context switch in an operating system?
options: The computer restarts and reloads the entire system
All running processes are terminated and then removed
The OS saves one process state and loads another
The kernel is replaced with a newer version of code
explanation: During a context switch, the OS saves the state of the current process and loads the saved state of another process.

text: What is the purpose of the OS system call interface?
options: It controls voltage supply to all peripheral devices
It connects the monitor cable directly to CPU chip
It manages the physical layout of the motherboard
It provides a layer between user programs and kernel
explanation: The system call interface provides a layer between user programs and the kernel for requesting OS services.

text: In a hybrid kernel architecture, which services typically remain in kernel space?
options: Only user applications remain inside the kernel space
Networking stacks exclusively run in the kernel space
All device drivers and file systems run in user space
Core IPC and some drivers stay in kernel for speed
explanation: Hybrid kernels keep core services like IPC and performance-critical drivers in kernel space while moving others to user space.

text: What security vulnerability does the Meltdown attack exploit in CPUs?
options: It exploits buffer overflow in user application code
It exploits speculative execution to read kernel data
It exploits faulty device drivers in the OS subsystem
It exploits weak encryption in network protocol sets
explanation: Meltdown exploits speculative execution in modern CPUs to allow user-space programs to read kernel memory.

text: What is the key design trade-off in exokernel operating systems?
options: Exokernels sacrifice all security for maximum speed
They trade app portability for resource efficiency now
Exokernels trade memory capacity for CPU performance
They sacrifice networking for faster disk I/O throughput
explanation: Exokernels expose hardware resources directly to applications, trading portability for maximum resource efficiency.

text: How does a Type-1 hypervisor differ from a Type-2 hypervisor?
options: Type-2 runs on bare metal while Type-1 needs a host
Type-1 runs directly on bare metal without host OS
Type-1 only supports Linux; Type-2 supports all OSes
Both types require a host operating system to function
explanation: A Type-1 hypervisor runs directly on hardware without a host OS, while Type-2 runs on top of a host OS.

text: What problem does the UEFI boot process solve over legacy BIOS?
options: UEFI only supports smaller disk sizes than BIOS can
UEFI is slower but more compatible with older hardware
UEFI eliminates the need for any operating system now
UEFI removes the 2TB disk limit and adds secure boot
explanation: UEFI overcomes BIOS limitations including the 2TB disk size limit and adds Secure Boot for security.

text: In capability-based OS design, what does a capability represent?
options: A type of scheduling algorithm for real-time tasks
A measure of CPU processing speed in megahertz
A token granting specific access rights to objects
A hardware register for storing interrupt addresses
explanation: A capability is an unforgeable token that grants the holder specific access rights to a particular object.

text: What challenge does the OS face in managing NUMA architectures?
options: Memory access times vary by processor-memory distance
All memory accesses take the same time in NUMA setups
NUMA systems cannot support more than one processor
NUMA eliminates the need for cache memory in all CPUs
explanation: In NUMA, memory access times vary depending on whether a processor accesses local or remote memory.

text: What is the fundamental principle behind separating mechanism and policy?
options: It ensures the OS can never be modified or updated
It prevents any user from customizing the OS at all
It allows changing decisions without changing the code
It requires all policies to be hard-coded in hardware
explanation: Separating mechanism from policy allows changing decisions without modifying the underlying mechanism code.

text: What is a unikernel and how does it differ from a traditional OS?
options: A unikernel bundles app and OS in one address space
A unikernel is a multi-user general-purpose OS now
A unikernel requires a full OS installation underneath
A unikernel runs many applications simultaneously here
explanation: A unikernel bundles the application with only the needed OS library components in a single address space.

text: Why is the halting problem relevant to operating system design?
options: It guarantees all programs terminate in finite time span
It proves the OS can always detect all infinite loops
It shows no algorithm can decide if programs will halt
It applies only to database management systems today
explanation: The halting problem proves no general algorithm can determine whether an arbitrary program will halt or run forever.

text: What is a monolithic kernel operating system?
options: An OS where all services run in kernel space
An OS that only runs a single application task
An OS designed for mobile devices exclusively
An OS with no kernel component present at all
explanation: A monolithic kernel runs all operating system services in kernel space as a single large program.

text: Which of the following is an example of a system call category?
options: Process control
Web browsing
Graphics rendering
Text formatting
explanation: Process control is one of the main system call categories including operations like creating and terminating processes.

text: What is the purpose of an API in an operating system?
options: It manages physical hardware components alone
It replaces the kernel entirely in modern OS
It provides functions for programmers to use
It handles only network communication protocol
explanation: An API provides a set of functions that programmers can use to interact with the OS without knowing system call details.

text: What does the command interpreter do in an operating system?
options: It controls hardware interrupt processing now
It compiles all programs into machine code
It manages virtual memory page tables only
It reads and executes user commands directly
explanation: The command interpreter reads user commands and executes them either directly or by invoking system programs.

text: Which layer is at the bottom in a layered operating system design?
options: File system layer
Hardware layer
Application layer
User interface layer
explanation: In a layered OS design, the hardware layer is at the bottom with successive layers built on top.

text: What is a system program in the context of an operating system?
options: A utility program bundled with the OS itself
A virus that attacks the operating system now
A program that only runs during system boot
A diagnostic tool built into the CPU hardware
explanation: System programs are utility programs bundled with the OS that provide a convenient environment for development.

text: What type of system call is used to create a new process?
options: Communication call
File management call
Device management call
Process control call
explanation: Creating a new process uses a process control system call, such as fork() in Unix.

text: What is the function of a linker in program execution?
options: It translates source code to assembly code
It handles input and output device operation
It manages memory allocation during runtime
It combines object modules into executable
explanation: A linker combines multiple object modules and libraries into a single executable file.

text: Which of the following is a communication system call?
options: open() call
exit() call
pipe() call
fork() call
explanation: pipe() is a communication system call creating a channel between processes.

text: What is the role of a loader in program execution?
options: It manages the network connections system
It designs the user interface for programs
It writes source code for the programmer
It loads executable files into main memory
explanation: A loader loads an executable file from secondary storage into main memory for execution.

text: What is the main advantage of a layered OS structure?
options: It eliminates the need for any system call interfaces
It provides the fastest possible system performance now
It requires less memory than all other architectures
Each layer can be debugged and verified independently
explanation: The main advantage is modularity: each layer can be debugged independently since it only uses lower layer functions.

text: How do loadable kernel modules benefit the operating system?
options: They allow extending kernel functionality at runtime easily
They eliminate all system calls from the OS call interface
They require the entire kernel to be recompiled each time
They replace the need for any device drivers completely now
explanation: LKMs allow the kernel to be extended at runtime without recompilation, enabling dynamic loading of drivers.

text: What is the role of the system call interface in OS architecture?
options: It only manages file system operations and nothing else
It replaces the kernel in all microkernel architectures
It serves as boundary between user programs and the OS
It provides direct hardware access to user-level programs
explanation: The system call interface serves as the boundary between user programs and the OS kernel.

text: What is a key disadvantage of the simple monolithic structure?
options: It cannot access any hardware devices at all currently
It is very slow due to many excessive mode switches
It does not support any form of multitasking operation
It is hard to maintain because parts are tightly coupled
explanation: Monolithic structure makes maintenance difficult because all components are tightly coupled together.

text: How does message passing work in a microkernel OS design?
options: Services exchange messages through microkernel's IPC
Services call each other like regular function calls
All communication happens directly in shared memory
Services communicate through shared global variables
explanation: In a microkernel, user-space services communicate by exchanging messages through the IPC mechanism.

text: What is the parameter table approach for passing system call arguments?
options: It limits system calls to only three parameters total
It stores system call results on the hard disk drive
Parameters are stored in a table; address passed in register
It eliminates the need for any CPU registers entirely
explanation: When many parameters are needed, they are stored in a memory table and the address is passed in a register.

text: What advantage does a microkernel have over a monolithic kernel?
options: Microkernels have more code running inside kernel space
Microkernels always have better raw system performance
Microkernels are easier to extend and port to new hardware
Microkernels eliminate the need for device driver support
explanation: Microkernels are easier to extend and port because most services run in user space.

text: What is the difference between static and dynamic linking?
options: Dynamic libraries must be recompiled with each application
Static libraries are linked at compile time into binary
Static libraries are always smaller than dynamic libraries
Dynamic libraries are slower but use more total disk space
explanation: Statically linked libraries are combined into the executable at compile time; dynamic libraries are loaded at runtime.

text: Why is debugging a layered OS more straightforward than others?
options: Because layered systems do not use system calls at all
Because errors in one layer are isolated from other ones
Because all layers share the exact same address space
Because layered systems have no kernel component in them
explanation: In a layered design each layer only interacts with adjacent layers, so errors are isolated within layers.

text: What role does the POSIX standard play in operating system design?
options: It describes physical network topologies for computers
It specifies a standard OS interface for portability use
It defines a mandatory hardware specification for PCs
It is a language used specifically to write OS kernels
explanation: POSIX defines standard OS interfaces that enable software portability across different Unix-like systems.

text: In the L4 microkernel family, what design decision improves IPC performance?
options: L4 uses asynchronous message passing exclusively always
L4 batches all IPC messages and processes them in bulk
L4 implements synchronous IPC with register-based transfer
L4 eliminates IPC entirely and uses shared memory only
explanation: L4 uses synchronous IPC with register-based message transfer to minimize communication overhead.

text: What is the key challenge in defining layers for a layered OS?
options: Finding hardware that supports the layered architecture
Determining correct layer ordering with dependencies here
Making sure each layer has the exact same code size now
Ensuring every layer can communicate with all other ones
explanation: The key challenge is determining the correct ordering since each layer can only use services of lower layers.

text: How does the Mach microkernel handle memory management?
options: It requires only hardware-specific memory management
It manages all memory entirely within the microkernel
It does not support virtual memory in any form at all
It uses external pagers in user space for page handling
explanation: Mach delegates page-fault handling to user-space external pagers for flexible memory management.

text: What is a system generation (SYSGEN) process in OS deployment?
options: It automatically writes new operating system source code
It generates documentation for the operating system APIs
It configures the OS for specific hardware at installation
It creates backup copies of the OS kernel on other disk
explanation: SYSGEN configures and customizes an OS for specific hardware during installation.

text: Why do modern OS designs favor hybrid kernel over pure microkernel?
options: Pure microkernels provide better performance in all cases
Hybrid kernels balance modularity with better performance
Pure microkernels cannot support any device drivers now
Hybrid kernels are simpler to implement than microkernels
explanation: Hybrid kernels keep performance-critical services in kernel space while maintaining modularity.

text: What is the role of the Hardware Abstraction Layer in Windows NT?
options: HAL manages user interface themes and visual appearance
HAL compiles C++ code into native machine instructions
HAL hides hardware differences from upper OS layers now
HAL provides a game rendering engine for DirectX apps
explanation: The HAL hides hardware-specific details from the rest of the OS, enabling hardware-independent code.

text: How does the Android architecture differ from standard Linux?
options: Android adds middleware like ART runtime over Linux kernel
Android does not use any Linux components in its design
Android replaces the Linux kernel with a custom one here
Android uses Linux only for networking and nothing else
explanation: Android adds middleware layers including ART runtime over the Linux kernel, differing from standard distributions.

text: What problem does eBPF solve in modern Linux kernels?
options: It replaces the TCP/IP stack with a new protocol stack
It allows safe programmable extensions in the kernel now
It provides a graphical desktop environment for Linux
It manages Bluetooth device connections in Linux only
explanation: eBPF allows running sandboxed programs within the kernel without modifying kernel source code.

text: What is the significance of MINIX 3 in operating system research?
options: MINIX 3 eliminated the need for file systems completely
MINIX 3 proved monolithic kernels are always superior
MINIX 3 demonstrated a reliable self-healing microkernel
MINIX 3 was the first OS to support graphical interfaces
explanation: MINIX 3 demonstrated that a microkernel OS can achieve high reliability through self-healing components.

text: How do C library wrappers simplify system call invocation?
options: They bypass the kernel entirely for all OS operations
They provide function interface hiding assembly details
They convert system calls into network protocol packets
They eliminate the need for privilege mode transitions
explanation: C library wrappers provide function interfaces that hide assembly-level details of system call invocation.

text: What is a process in an operating system?
options: A program in execution in main memory
A type of network protocol standard
A hardware component inside the CPU
A file stored on the hard disk drive
explanation: A process is a program in execution including code, current activity, stack, data section, and heap.

text: Which of the following is a valid process state?
options: Formatting
Installing
Waiting
Compiling
explanation: Waiting is a valid process state where a process waits for some event like I/O completion.

text: What does the Process Control Block contain?
options: All user passwords and credentials
Only the program source code text
Information about a specific process
The entire operating system kernel
explanation: The PCB contains information about a specific process including state, program counter, and registers.

text: What system call creates a new process in Unix?
options: exec()
fork()
kill()
wait()
explanation: The fork() system call creates a new child process that is a copy of the parent in Unix/Linux.

text: What is a parent process in operating system terminology?
options: The first process created during boot only
A process with the highest priority in queue
A process that created one or more children
A process managing all hardware device drivers
explanation: A parent process is one that has created one or more child processes using system calls like fork().

text: What happens when a process moves from running to waiting state?
options: The process begins on a different CPU core
The process is permanently terminated now
The process waits for an event like I/O
The process moves back to the ready queue
explanation: A process moves to waiting state when it needs to wait for an event such as I/O completion.

text: What is the ready queue in process management?
options: Processes that are ready and waiting for CPU
A list of processes waiting to load from disk
Blocked processes waiting for user keyboard input
A queue of completed processes to be deleted
explanation: The ready queue contains all processes in memory that are ready and waiting for CPU time.

text: What is the purpose of the process scheduler?
options: To handle network packet routing operations
To manage file system directory structures now
To select a process from ready queue for CPU
To compile programs into executable files
explanation: The process scheduler selects an available process from the ready queue and allocates the CPU.

text: What is process termination in operating systems?
options: Pausing a process temporarily for later
Ending a process and releasing its resources
Changing the priority of a running process
Moving a process to another computer node
explanation: Process termination ends a process and releases all its allocated resources back to the system.

text: What is an orphan process in Unix/Linux systems?
options: A process consuming no memory or CPU at all
A process whose parent has terminated first
A process running only during system shutdown
A process that has no executable code to run
explanation: An orphan process is one whose parent has terminated; it is typically adopted by init (PID 1).

text: What differs between the short-term and long-term scheduler?
options: Short-term selects from ready queue; long-term from disk
Long-term runs more frequently than short-term scheduler
Short-term controls the degree of multiprogramming level
They are the same scheduler with two different OS names
explanation: Short-term selects from the ready queue for CPU; long-term selects from disk to load into memory.

text: What is a zombie process and why does it occur?
options: A process created but never actually started running
A terminated process whose parent has not called wait
A process paused by the operating system permanently
A process that runs forever consuming all resources
explanation: A zombie is a terminated process whose parent has not yet called wait() to read its exit status.

text: How does exec() differ from fork() in Unix systems?
options: Neither affects the process structure in any way here
exec() creates a new process; fork() replaces image
Both create new processes in exactly identical fashion
fork() creates new process; exec() replaces the image
explanation: fork() creates a new child process; exec() replaces the current process image with a new program.

text: What is inter-process communication (IPC) used for?
options: Connecting a computer to external network devices
Compiling multiple source files at the same time here
Managing CPU scheduling algorithms exclusively now
Allowing processes to exchange data and synchronize
explanation: IPC allows processes to exchange data and synchronize using shared memory, message passing, or pipes.

text: How do shared memory and message passing IPC differ?
options: Neither mechanism allows processes to exchange any data
Both mechanisms work identically in all systems always
Message passing uses shared region; shared uses kernel
Shared memory uses common region; messages use kernel
explanation: Shared memory uses a common memory region; message passing exchanges messages through the kernel.

text: What is the purpose of the medium-term scheduler?
options: It schedules I/O operations for device driver code
It manages the file system directory tree structure
It assigns priorities to all newly created processes
It swaps processes between main memory and the disk
explanation: The medium-term scheduler swaps processes between memory and disk to control multiprogramming.

text: How does a pipe work as an IPC mechanism in Unix?
options: It provides unidirectional byte stream between procs
It only works between processes on different machines
It replaces the need for any process synchronization
It provides bidirectional communication always here
explanation: A Unix pipe provides a unidirectional byte stream where one process writes and another reads.

text: What is a process address space composed of?
options: Exclusively the operating system kernel code only
Only the stack and no other memory segment data
Text, data, heap, and stack segments combined
Only the program code and nothing else at all
explanation: A process address space is composed of text, data, heap, and stack segments.

text: What is cascading termination in process management?
options: Restarting a process multiple times before stopping
Terminating all children when parent process exits
Gradually slowing down a process before termination
Saving the process state to disk before termination
explanation: Cascading termination terminates all child processes when their parent process is terminated.

text: What is the role of the dispatcher in process management?
options: It gives CPU control to the process the scheduler chose
It manages file system allocation tables on the disk
It handles all network communication between hosts
It creates new processes from executable binaries
explanation: The dispatcher gives CPU control to the process selected by the short-term scheduler.

text: How does copy-on-write optimize the fork() system call?
options: It eliminates the need for virtual memory addressing
It copies the entire address space at fork instantly
It prevents child processes from writing any memory
It shares pages until a write then copies that page
explanation: COW shares memory pages after fork() and only copies when either process writes, reducing overhead.

text: What is vfork() and how does it differ from fork()?
options: vfork() creates multiple child processes at same time
vfork() bypasses the kernel for process creation now
vfork() is identical to fork() in all design aspects
vfork() shares address space and suspends the parent
explanation: vfork() shares the parent's address space and suspends the parent until the child calls exec() or exit().

text: How do process groups and sessions work in Unix systems?
options: Sessions and process groups are the same exact concept
Every process belongs to exactly one group and session
Process groups exist only for root user processes now
Process groups are only used in Windows and not Unix
explanation: Every process belongs to a group, and groups are organized into sessions for job control.

text: How do preemptive and cooperative multitasking differ?
options: Preemptive lets OS forcibly take CPU from a process
Cooperative lets the OS forcibly interrupt any process
Both approaches give unlimited CPU time to processes
Preemptive requires processes to yield voluntarily now
explanation: In preemptive multitasking the OS can forcibly take the CPU from a running process via timer interrupt.

text: What is the purpose of the clone() system call in Linux?
options: It converts a user process into a kernel-level process
It merges two existing processes into a single process
It creates a thread or process with fine-grained sharing
It only duplicates files on disk, not any processes
explanation: clone() creates a new process or thread with fine-grained control over which resources are shared.

text: How does Chrome use process architecture for security?
options: Chrome uses separate processes per tab for isolation
Chrome runs all tabs in one single process for speed
Chrome does not use processes; it uses threads only
Chrome relies only on the OS kernel for tab isolation
explanation: Chrome uses a multi-process architecture with separate sandboxed processes per tab for isolation.

text: What is the role of the init process (PID 1) in Unix?
options: It only manages network interfaces during boot time
It handles only file system mounting and nothing else
It is temporary and exits after boot process completes
It is ancestor of all processes and adopts all orphans
explanation: init (PID 1) is the first user-space process, ancestor of all processes, and adopts orphan processes.

text: What challenges arise with process migration in distributed systems?
options: Only the process ID needs to move between machines now
Process migration is trivial with no challenges at all
Open file handles and network state must be transferred
Process migration eliminates the need for scheduling here
explanation: Process migration must handle transferring state, file handles, and network connections while maintaining consistency.

text: How does process creation differ between Windows and Unix?
options: Windows maintains a strict parent-child tree like Unix
Neither system tracks relationships between processes now
Unix has parent-child trees; Windows uses handle tokens
Both systems have identical process creation mechanisms
explanation: Unix maintains a process tree; Windows uses handles without maintaining a strict parent-child tree.

text: What is process checkpointing in fault-tolerant systems?
options: It saves process state periodically for failure recovery
It permanently deletes process state to free memory
It checks if a process has any source code syntax errors
It counts the number of processes running on the system
explanation: Checkpointing periodically saves process state to stable storage for recovery after failures.

text: What is CPU scheduling in an operating system?
options: Overclocking the CPU for better processing performance
Installing new processors into the computer motherboard
Deciding which process gets the CPU next for execution
Measuring the temperature of the CPU chip continuously
explanation: CPU scheduling decides which process in the ready queue gets the CPU next.

text: What does FCFS stand for in CPU scheduling?
options: First Cache First Store
Fast CPU Flexible Scheduling
Fully Controlled File System
First Come First Served
explanation: FCFS stands for First Come First Served, executing processes in arrival order.

text: What is turnaround time in CPU scheduling?
options: Time to switch between two processes
Total time from submission to completion
The speed at which the CPU clock runs
The time to restart the computer
explanation: Turnaround time is the total time from submission to completion including waiting and execution.

text: What is waiting time in CPU scheduling?
options: Time needed to compile a program from source
Time required to boot the operating system
Time a process spends in the ready queue
Time taken to save a file to the disk drive
explanation: Waiting time is the total time a process spends waiting in the ready queue.

text: What is the time quantum in Round Robin scheduling?
options: The time required for a context switch now
The clock speed of the processor chip now
The total time to complete all processes
A fixed time slice given to each process
explanation: The time quantum is the fixed amount of CPU time allocated to each process in Round Robin.

text: Which scheduling algorithm selects the shortest job first?
options: First Come First Served
Round Robin scheduling
Shortest Job First
Priority scheduling only
explanation: Shortest Job First selects the process with the smallest CPU burst time next.

text: What is throughput in CPU scheduling metrics?
options: The speed of data transfer to the disk drive
Number of processes completed per time unit
The temperature of CPU during operation
The size of the ready queue at a given moment
explanation: Throughput is the number of processes completed per unit of time.

text: What is a preemptive scheduling algorithm?
options: One where OS can take CPU from a process
One that only works with a single process
One that requires manual user intervention
One that never interrupts a running process
explanation: A preemptive algorithm allows the OS to take the CPU from a running process.

text: What is a CPU burst in process execution?
options: A period of continuous CPU computation time
A malfunction causing the CPU to overheat now
A type of interrupt from external hardware now
A period when the CPU is powered off safely
explanation: A CPU burst is a period of continuous CPU computation between I/O operations.

text: In priority scheduling, which process gets the CPU first?
options: The process with lowest priority value
The process with highest priority value
The process arriving most recently now
The process with longest burst time
explanation: The process with the highest priority value is selected for CPU execution first.

text: What is the convoy effect in FCFS scheduling?
options: All processes complete at exactly the same time
Short processes wait behind a long CPU-bound one
The CPU is idle while waiting for process arrival
Processes are sorted by priority before execution
explanation: The convoy effect occurs when short processes wait behind a long CPU-bound process in FCFS.

text: How does the Shortest Remaining Time First algorithm work?
options: It preempts if a new process has shorter remaining time
It always runs the longest process first for throughput
It is identical to non-preemptive SJF in every way
It uses Round Robin with variable time quantum sizes
explanation: SRTF preempts the current process when a new one arrives with shorter remaining CPU burst.

text: What problem does aging solve in priority scheduling?
options: It prevents context switching between processes now
It increases the time quantum in Round Robin schemes
It solves starvation for low-priority waiting processes
It eliminates the need for any scheduling algorithm
explanation: Aging gradually increases waiting process priority, solving starvation for low-priority processes.

text: What happens with a very small time quantum in Round Robin?
options: It reduces context switches significantly overall
It increases context switch overhead and degrades speed
It eliminates the need for a ready queue in the system
It converts Round Robin into FCFS scheduling behavior
explanation: A very small quantum causes excessive context switches, degrading system performance.

text: How does multilevel queue scheduling organize processes?
options: Each process gets its own dedicated scheduling queue
Processes are divided into classes with separate queues
All processes share a single common ready queue only
Processes are randomly assigned to available CPU cores
explanation: Multilevel queue scheduling partitions the ready queue into separate queues with different policies.

text: What is dispatch latency in CPU scheduling?
options: Time to transfer data from main memory to the CPU
Time to stop one process and start running another
Time for a process to complete its entire execution
Time between submitting a job and its first output
explanation: Dispatch latency is the time to stop one process and start another, including context switching.

text: What is response time and why is it important?
options: It counts total number of context switches performed
It is time from submission to first response produced
It is the total time to complete the process entirely
It measures amount of memory a process consumes now
explanation: Response time is from submission to first response, critical for interactive system responsiveness.

text: How does SJF achieve optimal average waiting time?
options: By using a large time quantum to minimize switching
By assigning equal CPU time to every queued process
By running the longest jobs first to clear the queue
By placing shortest jobs first reducing total wait time
explanation: SJF is optimal because executing shorter jobs first minimizes cumulative waiting time.

text: What distinguishes multilevel feedback queue from multilevel queue?
options: Multilevel queues allow movement but feedback does not
Feedback queues allow processes to move between queues
Both scheduling algorithms are identical in all behavior
Feedback queues do not allow preemption of processes
explanation: Multilevel feedback queue allows processes to move between queues based on behavior.

text: What is the main disadvantage of Shortest Job First?
options: It needs CPU burst time which is hard to predict ahead
It cannot work with more than two processes at a time
It always has the worst average waiting time possible
It causes excessive context switching between processes
explanation: SJF requires knowing the next CPU burst length in advance, which must be estimated.

text: How does the Completely Fair Scheduler in Linux work?
options: CFS maintains virtual runtime for fair CPU allocation
CFS uses lottery scheduling for random CPU assignment
CFS uses strict priority queues with fixed time slices
CFS implements simple Round Robin with a fixed quantum
explanation: Linux CFS uses a red-black tree keyed by virtual runtime, scheduling the process with the lowest vruntime.

text: What is the exponential averaging formula for burst prediction?
options: Next burst is always set to a fixed constant value here
Next burst equals the exact length of the previous burst
tau(n+1) = alpha*t(n) + (1-alpha)*tau(n) using history
Prediction uses only the average of all prior burst times
explanation: Exponential averaging uses tau(n+1) = alpha*t(n) + (1-alpha)*tau(n) balancing recent and historical data.

text: What is priority inversion and how can it be solved?
options: Low-priority blocks high-priority; use priority inheritance
High-priority tasks always run first without any exception
Priority inversion occurs only in single-core CPU systems
It is solved by removing all priorities from the system
explanation: Priority inversion is when a low-priority task blocks a high-priority one; solved by priority inheritance.

text: How does the O(1) scheduler differ from CFS in Linux?
options: O(1) is fairer than CFS for interactive desktop workloads
O(1) uses active/expired arrays; CFS uses red-black tree
Both schedulers are identical in design and implementation
CFS uses fixed time slices while O(1) uses virtual runtime
explanation: O(1) uses two priority arrays; CFS uses a red-black tree ordered by virtual runtime.

text: What is gang scheduling in multiprocessor systems?
options: It only works with single-threaded processes on one core
It co-schedules related threads simultaneously on CPUs
It randomly assigns processes to processors without order
It schedules one process at a time across all CPUs now
explanation: Gang scheduling co-schedules all threads of a process simultaneously on different processors.

text: How does processor affinity affect multiprocessor scheduling?
options: It has no effect on scheduling in multiprocessor systems
It forces processes to migrate between CPUs constantly
It keeps a process on the same CPU to exploit warm cache
It assigns all processes to the same single CPU core here
explanation: Processor affinity keeps a process on the same CPU to take advantage of cache data.

text: What is global vs. partitioned scheduling in multicore systems?
options: Global and partitioned scheduling are the exact same thing
Global scheduling assigns each process to a fixed core
Partitioned scheduling uses a single queue for all cores
Global uses one shared queue; partitioned uses per-core
explanation: Global uses a single shared queue allowing migration; partitioned uses per-core queues.

text: What is rate-monotonic scheduling for real-time systems?
options: It uses Round Robin for all periodic real-time task sets
It assigns the same fixed priority to every task equally
It assigns priorities randomly to all real-time task sets
It assigns priorities inversely proportional to periods
explanation: Rate-monotonic assigns fixed priorities inversely proportional to periods; shorter period = higher priority.

text: How does Earliest Deadline First schedule real-time tasks?
options: It only works with non-periodic tasks in batch systems
It dynamically prioritizes tasks with nearest deadlines
It schedules the task with the latest deadline first
It ignores deadlines and uses burst time for scheduling
explanation: EDF dynamically prioritizes tasks with the nearest deadlines first; optimal for uniprocessor systems.

text: What is the schedulability test for rate-monotonic scheduling?
options: All tasks must have priority higher than the OS kernel
Each task must have the same exact period and burst time
CPU utilization must be below 100 percent for all tasks
Sum of Ci/Ti must be at most n*(2^(1/n)-1) for guarantee
explanation: The sufficient condition is that total utilization sum(Ci/Ti) must not exceed n*(2^(1/n)-1).

text: What is a thread in operating system terminology?
options: A network connection between two machines
A type of file stored on the hard disk
A lightweight execution unit in a process
A separate physical CPU core in system
explanation: A thread is a lightweight unit of execution within a process sharing the process's resources.

text: What do threads within the same process share?
options: Nothing at all between them
Individual stack memory only
Code, data, and file resources
Only the process identifier
explanation: Threads share code section, data section, and OS resources while each has its own stack and registers.

text: What is multithreading in operating systems?
options: Running multiple threads within a single process
Running multiple operating systems simultaneously
Using multiple monitors for display output only
Connecting multiple keyboards to one computer
explanation: Multithreading supports multiple threads of execution within a single process.

text: Which of the following is a benefit of using threads?
options: Threads prevent all forms of parallel execution
Thread creation is faster than process creation
Threads consume more resources than processes
Threads restrict programs to a single CPU core
explanation: Thread creation is faster than process creation because threads share resources.

text: What is a user-level thread managed by?
options: The operating system kernel directly
A user-level thread library only
The network interface card alone
The hardware CPU scheduler chip
explanation: User-level threads are managed by a user-level thread library without kernel involvement.

text: What is a kernel-level thread?
options: A thread that manages hardware device interrupts
A thread that only runs during system boot time
A thread managed and scheduled by the kernel
A thread that exists only in embedded OS systems
explanation: A kernel-level thread is managed directly by the OS kernel for scheduling on processors.

text: What does each thread have that is unique to itself?
options: Its own copy of all open file handles now
Its own operating system kernel instance
Its own stack and program counter value
Its own copy of entire process memory
explanation: Each thread has its own stack and program counter while sharing code, data, and files.

text: What is the main advantage of multithreading in a web server?
options: It prevents any client from accessing the server
It reduces the server network bandwidth usage
It eliminates the need for any network protocol
It handles multiple client requests concurrently
explanation: Multithreading allows a web server to handle multiple client requests concurrently.

text: What is the Pthreads API?
options: A POSIX standard for thread management
A network protocol for file transferring
A graphics library for rendering images
A database query language for data access
explanation: Pthreads is a POSIX standard API for thread creation, synchronization, and management.

text: What happens when one thread in a process calls exit()?
options: Other threads continue running without any impact
Only that specific thread is terminated safely
The entire process and all its threads terminate
The thread is moved to a different process now
explanation: Calling exit() terminates the entire process and all its threads.

text: What is the many-to-one threading model?
options: Many user threads map to one kernel thread
Each user thread maps to one kernel thread
Many kernel threads map to one user thread
Multiple kernel threads share one CPU core
explanation: Many-to-one maps many user-level threads to a single kernel thread.

text: What is the one-to-one threading model's main advantage?
options: It eliminates the need for any synchronization at all
It uses less memory than all other threading models
It allows true concurrency on multiprocessor systems
It does not need any operating system kernel support
explanation: One-to-one enables true parallelism since the kernel can schedule threads on different cores.

text: What is the purpose of thread pools in multithreaded systems?
options: To pre-create threads and reuse them for new requests
To prevent any thread from being created in a system
To merge all threads into a single execution context
To permanently store thread objects on the hard disk
explanation: Thread pools pre-create threads that wait for work, avoiding creation overhead.

text: What is thread-local storage (TLS) used for?
options: Storing thread data on a remote network file server
Sharing data between all threads in a process
Encrypting data that threads access in shared memory
Giving each thread its own copy of specific data
explanation: TLS gives each thread its own copy of data that would otherwise be shared.

text: How does the many-to-many threading model work?
options: It eliminates both user and kernel threads entirely
It maps one user thread to many kernel threads here
It maps many kernel threads to one user thread now
It maps many user threads to many kernel threads
explanation: Many-to-many multiplexes many user threads onto a smaller or equal number of kernel threads.

text: What is the signal handling challenge in multithreaded programs?
options: Signals are only relevant to single-threaded programs
Deciding which thread should receive and handle signal
Signals cannot be generated in multithreaded programs
All signals are handled automatically by the OS kernel
explanation: The OS must decide which thread should receive a signal in multithreaded programs.

text: What is the difference between implicit and explicit threading?
options: Both approaches are identical in their implementation
Implicit threading needs manual thread creation code
Explicit threading is handled automatically by system
Implicit threading is done by compilers and libraries
explanation: Implicit threading shifts thread management to compilers and runtime libraries like OpenMP.

text: What happens during deferred thread cancellation?
options: Thread cancellation is impossible in deferred mode here
The target thread is terminated immediately on request
Thread checks for cancellation at cancellation points
The entire process is terminated along with all threads
explanation: In deferred cancellation, the thread checks at cancellation points for safe cleanup.

text: Why is fork() complex in multithreaded programs?
options: fork() terminates the calling thread before the child
It must decide whether to duplicate all or one thread
fork() cannot be called from multithreaded programs
fork() always duplicates all threads without exception
explanation: fork() must decide whether to duplicate all threads or only the calling thread.

text: What is OpenMP and how does it support threading?
options: OpenMP is a hardware spec for multicore CPUs only
OpenMP is a network protocol for distributed systems
OpenMP provides compiler directives for parallel code
OpenMP is an operating system for embedded controllers
explanation: OpenMP uses compiler directives to identify parallel regions, enabling implicit threading.

text: What is a lightweight process (LWP) in many-to-many threading?
options: An LWP is a file storing thread state on the hard disk
An LWP is intermediate between user and kernel threads
An LWP is a network packet for inter-thread messaging
An LWP is a physical CPU core in the processor chip
explanation: An LWP is an intermediate structure between user-level and kernel-level threading.

text: What is the scheduler activation mechanism in threading?
options: Kernel notifies user library via upcalls about events
It is a simple timer interrupt for thread scheduling
It replaces the CPU scheduler with a user-space one
It prevents communication between kernel and threads
explanation: Scheduler activations allow the kernel to notify user-level thread libraries about events via upcalls.

text: How does Windows thread scheduling differ from Linux?
options: Both systems only schedule processes, never any threads
Windows schedules processes; Linux schedules threads
Windows uses Round Robin; Linux uses SJF exclusively
Windows schedules threads; Linux schedules tasks via CFS
explanation: Windows directly schedules threads; Linux schedules tasks using the Completely Fair Scheduler.

text: What is the thundering herd problem in threaded servers?
options: A single thread monopolizes the CPU preventing others
Threads run too slowly due to excessive synchronization
Too many threads cause total memory exhaustion now
Multiple threads wake for one event but one handles it
explanation: Multiple threads are woken for a single event but only one can handle it, wasting resources.

text: What is the purpose of the futex mechanism in Linux?
options: It provides a graphics rendering pipeline for threads
It controls CPU frequency scaling for threaded workload
It manages file transfers between threads over network
It implements fast user-space locking with kernel fallback
explanation: A futex handles uncontended locking in user space and only involves the kernel when blocking is needed.

text: How does hyperthreading affect OS thread scheduling?
options: It eliminates the need for any software threading at all
It reduces the number of CPUs visible to the OS kernel
It has no effect on how the OS schedules threads at all
OS sees additional logical CPUs and schedules on them now
explanation: Hyperthreading presents additional logical CPUs to the OS for scheduling threads.

text: What are the challenges of implementing user-level threads?
options: User-level threads are always slower than kernel threads
User-level threads cannot be created by any application
User-level threads require special hardware support only
A blocking syscall blocks all threads in the process now
explanation: A blocking system call blocks the entire process since the kernel is unaware of user threads.

text: What is the two-level model in thread implementation?
options: It combines many-to-many with one-to-one mapping
It requires two separate kernels for thread management
It uses exactly two threads per process, no more here
It maps all threads to exactly two kernel-level threads
explanation: The two-level model combines many-to-many with the ability to bind user threads to kernel threads.

text: How does Grand Central Dispatch handle threading on macOS?
options: GCD only supports single-threaded application execution
GCD requires programmers to manually create threads
GCD uses blocks and dispatch queues managed by runtime
GCD is a hardware threading mechanism in Apple chips
explanation: GCD uses blocks on dispatch queues with a runtime-managed thread pool.

text: What is a fiber and how does it differ from a thread?
options: Fibers are identical to threads in every way possible
Fibers are hardware threads built into processor cores
Fibers are cooperatively scheduled user-level threads
Fibers are kernel-level threads with preemptive scheduling
explanation: Fibers are cooperatively scheduled user-level threads where the fiber must explicitly yield control.

text: What is a race condition in concurrent programming?
options: A technique for sorting data in parallel
A way to synchronize clocks between PCs
When output depends on timing of threads
A method to speed up program execution
explanation: A race condition occurs when program behavior depends on the relative timing of thread execution.

text: What is mutual exclusion in process synchronization?
options: Allowing multiple processes to access resources
Preventing any process from executing on CPU
Deleting shared resources to prevent any access
Ensuring only one process in critical section now
explanation: Mutual exclusion ensures only one process can be inside its critical section at any given time.

text: What is a critical section in concurrent programming?
options: The first section of code executed by program
The most important function in program code
A section of code needing no synchronization
A code segment accessing shared resources here
explanation: A critical section accesses shared resources and must be executed atomically.

text: What is a semaphore in operating systems?
options: A synchronization variable with atomic ops
A memory allocation technique for processes
A scheduling algorithm for real-time tasks
A type of hardware interrupt signal line
explanation: A semaphore uses wait and signal atomic operations to control access to shared resources.

text: What is the purpose of a mutex lock?
options: To handle network packet routing faster
To speed up single-thread execution
To ensure mutual exclusion in critical section
To manage memory allocation for processes
explanation: A mutex ensures only one thread can enter a critical section at a time.

text: What does the wait() operation do on a semaphore?
options: It creates a new semaphore with given value
It decrements value and blocks if negative
It increments the semaphore value by one
It destroys the semaphore variable for good
explanation: wait() decrements the semaphore value and blocks the caller if the result is negative.

text: What does signal() do on a semaphore?
options: It increments value and may wake a process
It deletes the semaphore from the system
It resets semaphore to its initial value now
It decrements semaphore value by one unit
explanation: signal() increments the value and may wake a waiting process.

text: What is a binary semaphore?
options: A semaphore requiring two processes only
A semaphore restricted to zero or one
A semaphore holding any integer value
A semaphore for binary search algorithms
explanation: A binary semaphore has value restricted to 0 or 1, similar to a mutex lock.

text: What is busy waiting in the context of synchronization?
options: A thread sleeps until explicitly woken up
A thread exits when a resource is locked
A thread continuously checks a condition
A thread creates more threads to help out
explanation: Busy waiting continuously loops checking a condition, wasting CPU cycles.

text: What is a counting semaphore used for?
options: Measuring CPU clock speed in gigahertz
Counting total processes in the OS system
Controlling access to finite resource instances
Counting the number of files on the disk
explanation: A counting semaphore controls access to a resource with a finite number of instances.

text: What are the three requirements for a correct critical section solution?
options: Speed, memory efficiency, and low power consumption
Simplicity, readability, and backward compatibility now
Atomicity, consistency, isolation, and full durability
Mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting time
explanation: Mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting are the three requirements.

text: What is the producer-consumer problem in synchronization?
options: A technique for optimizing database query performance
Coordinating producer adding and consumer removing
A problem about consumer electronics device drivers
A problem about manufacturing physical hardware
explanation: It involves coordinating a producer adding items and a consumer removing items from a shared buffer.

text: How does Peterson's solution achieve mutual exclusion?
options: It uses a centralized server to manage all locks
It uses two shared variables: turn and flag array
It uses hardware test-and-set atomic instructions
It relies on disabling all interrupts on processor
explanation: Peterson's solution uses a turn variable and flag array for software-only mutual exclusion.

text: How do spinlocks differ from blocking locks?
options: Both types always put waiting threads to sleep now
Spinlocks sleep; blocking locks use busy waiting
Both types always use busy waiting to check locks
Spinlocks busy-wait; blocking locks put threads asleep
explanation: Spinlocks busy-wait in a loop; blocking locks put the thread to sleep until available.

text: What is a monitor in concurrent programming?
options: A display screen for viewing program output
A network device monitoring all traffic on LAN
A high-level synchronization construct with mutex
A tool for monitoring CPU temperature and speed
explanation: A monitor encapsulates shared data with built-in mutual exclusion and condition variables.

text: What is the purpose of condition variables in monitors?
options: They store values of conditional expressions only
They replace the need for any mutual exclusion now
They count threads inside the monitor at any time
They let threads wait for specific conditions safely
explanation: Condition variables let threads wait until a specific condition is met using wait/signal.

text: What is the readers-writers problem in synchronization?
options: A problem about reading user input from a keyboard
Allowing concurrent reads but exclusive writes to data
A technique for writing data to multiple disk drives
A problem about reading and writing files in order
explanation: It allows multiple concurrent readers but requires exclusive access for writers.

text: What is the test-and-set instruction used for?
options: Testing network and setting bandwidth limits on it
Setting CPU clock speed after testing the hardware
Testing programs and setting breakpoints in them
Atomically testing and modifying a word for locks
explanation: Test-and-set atomically reads and sets a memory word, used to implement locks.

text: What is the compare-and-swap (CAS) instruction?
options: A sorting algorithm comparing and swapping values
A technique to compare files and swap if different
A method to swap two processes in scheduling queue
Atomic instruction conditionally updating memory word
explanation: CAS atomically compares a memory location and swaps if equal to expected value.

text: What is a barrier synchronization mechanism?
options: A way to permanently block a thread from running
A point where all threads must arrive before any go
A physical barrier between two system processors
A mechanism preventing threads from being created
explanation: A barrier is a point where all threads must arrive before any can proceed past it.

text: What is the dining philosophers problem and its significance?
options: It models network routing algorithms in large data centers
It is about optimizing database query execution plans now
It models resource allocation leading to potential deadlock
It describes scheduling of philosophers teaching classes
explanation: It models concurrent resource acquisition that can lead to deadlock and synchronization challenges.

text: How does Read-Copy-Update (RCU) achieve lock-free reads?
options: RCU prevents any writes from occurring while reading
RCU uses spinlocks internally for all read operations
RCU locks all readers while writer updates the data
Readers access old data while writers prepare new copy
explanation: RCU lets readers access old data while writers create a copy, updating the pointer atomically.

text: What is the ABA problem in lock-free data structures?
options: ABA is an algorithm for balanced binary tree building
It describes automatic backup and archival of data
It refers to alphabetical ordering of lock acquisition
Value changes from A to B to A, hiding the updates
explanation: ABA occurs when a value changes A->B->A, making CAS succeed incorrectly and missing updates.

text: What is a memory barrier instruction used for?
options: It ensures memory operations complete in proper order
It prevents the CPU from accessing some memory regions
It doubles available memory by compressing all data now
It creates a physical barrier in RAM to separate data
explanation: Memory barriers enforce ordering of memory operations, preventing reordering across the barrier.

text: What differs between Mesa and Hoare monitor semantics?
options: Hoare uses signal-and-continue; Mesa signal-and-wait
Mesa requires busy waiting; Hoare uses blocking only
Mesa and Hoare semantics are completely identical here
Mesa uses signal-and-continue; Hoare signal-and-wait
explanation: Mesa: signaler continues; Hoare: signaler yields immediately to the waiting thread.

text: How does transactional memory simplify concurrent programming?
options: It only works with single-threaded programs in practice
It requires more locks than traditional synchronization
It eliminates all shared memory between threads fully
It lets code blocks execute atomically without locks now
explanation: Transactional memory allows code blocks to execute atomically, handling conflicts automatically.

text: What is the priority ceiling protocol and how does it work?
options: Each mutex gets ceiling equal to highest priority user now
It only lets highest priority thread acquire locks at all
It lowers all thread priorities to zero to stop conflicts
It eliminates all priorities making every thread equal here
explanation: Each mutex is assigned a ceiling equal to the highest priority thread that may lock it, preventing deadlock.

text: What is lock-free vs. wait-free in concurrent algorithms?
options: Wait-free ensures system progress but not per-thread
Lock-free ensures system progress; wait-free per-thread
Lock-free and wait-free algorithms are exactly the same
Lock-free uses hidden locks but hides them from users
explanation: Lock-free guarantees some thread makes progress; wait-free guarantees every thread completes in bounded steps.

text: What does the sleeping barber problem model?
options: It models network packet queuing in router memory now
It describes file system journaling for crash recovery
A scheduling algorithm for barber shop appointments
It models bounded-buffer producer-consumer with sleep
explanation: It models a producer-consumer with a finite waiting room where the barber sleeps when idle.

text: How does Linux handle preemption within critical sections?
options: Linux disables preemption using preemption counters now
Linux never allows any kernel-level preemption at all
Linux always allows preemption inside critical sections
Linux uses hardware locks to prevent all preemption
explanation: Linux uses a preemption counter; preemption is disabled during critical sections and re-enabled when the counter reaches zero.

text: What is a deadlock in an operating system?
options: When processes wait for each other forever
When memory is fully available to all tasks
When the CPU overheats and shuts down
When a program runs faster than expected
explanation: A deadlock is when two or more processes wait indefinitely for resources held by each other.

text: How many conditions must hold simultaneously for deadlock?
options: One
Four
Two
Three
explanation: Four conditions must hold: mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait.

text: What is the mutual exclusion condition for deadlock?
options: Processes can preempt resources from other ones
Resources can be shared by multiple processes
All resources are always available to every process
At least one resource must be held non-sharably
explanation: Mutual exclusion means at least one resource must be held in a non-sharable mode.

text: What is the hold and wait condition for deadlock?
options: Processes never hold more than one resource at a time
Processes wait without holding any resources at all
Processes hold resources while waiting for others
Processes release resources before requesting new ones
explanation: Hold and wait means a process holds resources while waiting to acquire additional ones.

text: What is a resource allocation graph used for?
options: Designing the layout of network infrastructure
Measuring CPU utilization over time periods
Drawing flowcharts for program logic design
Visualizing allocation and detecting deadlock
explanation: A resource allocation graph visualizes resource allocation and detects potential deadlocks.

text: What is the circular wait condition for deadlock?
options: Processes wait for resources in random order
A circular chain where each waits for the next
Processes wait in a linear queue for resources
All processes wait for the same single resource
explanation: Circular wait means a circular chain of processes each waiting for a resource held by the next.

text: What is the no preemption condition for deadlock?
options: Only the OS kernel can hold resources permanently
Resources cannot be preempted from holders now
Resources can be forcibly taken from processes
All resources are preemptible at any time freely
explanation: No preemption means resources cannot be forcibly taken from a holding process.

text: What is deadlock prevention as a strategy?
options: Recovering by terminating all running processes
Ignoring deadlocks and hoping they never occur
Detecting deadlocks after they already occurred
Ensuring at least one deadlock condition fails
explanation: Deadlock prevention ensures at least one of the four necessary conditions can never hold.

text: What is starvation in resource allocation?
options: A process runs out of memory and crashes fast
A process is denied needed resources indefinitely
A process completes too quickly and wastes resources
A process gets all resources it needs quickly
explanation: Starvation occurs when a process is indefinitely denied the resources it needs.

text: What is a safe state in deadlock avoidance?
options: A state where resources can be allocated avoiding deadlock
A state where all resources are fully allocated already
A state where the system has no processes running
A state where CPU temperature is within safe limits
explanation: A safe state means resources can be allocated to avoid deadlock with a safe execution sequence.

text: How does the Banker's algorithm determine if a state is safe?
options: It finds a safe sequence where all processes complete
It only checks if CPU utilization is above fifty percent
It checks if total resources exceed process count
It verifies no process has any resource allocated now
explanation: Banker's algorithm simulates allocation to find a safe sequence where all processes can complete.

text: How can the hold-and-wait condition be prevented?
options: By removing mutual exclusion from all shared resources
By giving every process all existing system resources
By requiring processes to request all resources at once
By allowing processes to request at any time freely
explanation: Requiring processes to request all resources before starting prevents hold-and-wait.

text: How do deadlock prevention and avoidance differ?
options: Prevention ignores deadlocks; avoidance terminates all tasks
Prevention detects deadlocks; avoidance recovers from them
Prevention constrains requests; avoidance checks each allocation
They are exactly the same strategy with different names now
explanation: Prevention constrains requests; avoidance dynamically checks each allocation for safety.

text: How does total ordering on resources prevent circular wait?
options: It allows processes to request resources in any order
It makes all resources shareable between all processes
It eliminates the need for any resource allocation logic
Processes must request resources in increasing order now
explanation: Total ordering requires resources be requested in increasing order, preventing circular chains.

text: What is deadlock detection using a wait-for graph?
options: A graph showing CPU utilization over time periods
A diagram of physical network connections between PCs
A chart showing memory usage of each running process
A variant with only processes; a cycle means deadlock
explanation: A wait-for graph has only process nodes; a cycle indicates deadlock.

text: What are the recovery options after detecting a deadlock?
options: Only restarting the entire computer system works now
Process termination or resource preemption can be used
Deadlocks always resolve themselves automatically here
Adding more physical memory always resolves all deadlock
explanation: Recovery uses process termination or resource preemption after deadlock detection.

text: What is the ostrich algorithm for handling deadlocks?
options: A sophisticated algorithm detecting all deadlocks fast
An algorithm preventing deadlock with resource ordering
It ignores the problem assuming deadlocks rarely occur
A technique recovering from deadlocks automatically now
explanation: The ostrich algorithm ignores deadlocks assuming they are rare and not worth the overhead.

text: What is a livelock and how does it differ from deadlock?
options: Livelock means all processes are blocked and inactive
In livelock processes are active but make no progress
Livelock only occurs in single-threaded applications
Livelock is identical to deadlock in every way here
explanation: In livelock, processes are active but continuously change states without making progress.

text: What does the Banker's algorithm need from processes?
options: The total disk space used by each process on system
Only the current CPU usage of each active process
Maximum resource needs declared by each process first
Only the priority level assigned to each process now
explanation: Banker's algorithm requires each process to declare maximum resource needs in advance.

text: Why might deadlock prevention lead to low resource utilization?
options: Because conservative restrictions leave resources idle now
Because it allocates all resources to one process always
Because it detects deadlocks only after they have formed
Because it allows processes to hold resources indefinitely
explanation: Conservative restrictions in prevention can leave resources idle, reducing utilization.

text: What is the time complexity of the Banker's algorithm?
options: O(n*n*m) for determining safe sequence each time here
O(n) linear time complexity for each resource request
O(log n) logarithmic time for each allocation decision
O(1) constant time regardless of process or resources
explanation: Banker's algorithm has O(n^2 * m) complexity, scanning n processes checking m resource types.

text: How does the Linux kernel handle deadlock in practice?
options: Linux uses lockdep to detect potential deadlock patterns
Linux ignores deadlocks and restarts the system if stuck
Linux prevents all four deadlock conditions at all times
Linux implements full Banker's algorithm at runtime
explanation: Linux uses lockdep, a lock dependency validator that detects potential deadlock patterns.

text: What is two-phase locking's relationship to deadlock?
options: Two-phase locking completely eliminates all deadlocks
Two-phase locking causes deadlock in every single case
Two-phase locking ensures serializability allows deadlock
Two-phase locking has no relationship to deadlock at all
explanation: Two-phase locking ensures serializability but does not prevent deadlocks.

text: What is a knot in a resource allocation graph?
options: A knot is simply another name for any edge in graph
A knot means the graph has no cycles present at all
A cycle where no outside process can break the cycle
A knot indicates that the system is in a safe state
explanation: A knot is a cycle where no process outside it can provide resources to break it.

text: How does wound-wait differ from wait-die for databases?
options: Wait-die: older waits or dies; wound-wait wounds younger
Both schemes are identical and behave the same always
Wound-wait: older wounds younger; wait-die: older waits
Wound-wait preempts younger; wait-die makes older wait
explanation: In wound-wait older preempts younger; in wait-die older waits but younger is rolled back.

text: How are victim selection strategies used in deadlock recovery?
options: Cost-based selection minimizes rollback overhead impact
Victim selection only applies to file system operations
All victims are always the newest process in the system
Victim selection is random and cannot be optimized now
explanation: Cost-based victim selection minimizes overall rollback overhead based on multiple factors.

text: How can deadlock occur in distributed systems?
options: Processes on different nodes can form distributed cycles
Deadlock cannot occur across multiple distributed nodes
Only single-node deadlocks are possible in any system
Distributed systems automatically prevent all deadlocks
explanation: Processes on different nodes can form wait-for cycles spanning multiple machines.

text: What is the phantom deadlock problem in distributed detection?
options: It describes deadlocks that only occur during system startup
It is a deadlock that is invisible to all detection methods
It occurs when a detected deadlock does not actually exist
It refers to deadlocks in virtual machine environments only
explanation: A phantom deadlock is a false detection due to delays in propagating state information.

text: What is hierarchical deadlock prevention using lock ordering?
options: It assigns random order to locks at runtime dynamically
It groups locks into levels and enforces strict ordering
It eliminates all locks from the system to prevent issues
It uses only one global lock for the entire system here
explanation: Hierarchical lock ordering enforces acquiring locks in strictly increasing level order.

text: How does the edge-chasing algorithm detect distributed deadlocks?
options: It uses a centralized server to collect all wait-for info
It prevents deadlock rather than detecting it after fact
It broadcasts all lock info to every node all the time
It propagates probe messages along wait-for edges across
explanation: Edge-chasing propagates probe messages along wait-for edges; if a probe returns to initiator, deadlock exists.

text: What is the main purpose of memory management?
options: To permanently store data like a hard drive
To increase the physical size of RAM chips
To manage network bandwidth between hosts
To allocate and track memory for processes
explanation: Memory management allocates and tracks memory for running processes.

text: What is virtual memory in an operating system?
options: A type of cache memory inside the CPU chip
A technique giving illusion of larger memory
Physical RAM installed in the computer
A backup copy of files stored on the disk
explanation: Virtual memory gives the illusion of a larger memory space than physically available.

text: What is a page in the context of virtual memory?
options: A type of hardware interrupt signal to CPU
A section of the operating system kernel
A unit of data stored on the hard disk now
A fixed-size block of virtual memory space
explanation: A page is a fixed-size block of virtual memory that maps to physical frames.

text: What is a page table used for?
options: Displaying a table of contents for docs
Storing user passwords and credentials now
Mapping virtual addresses to physical ones
Scheduling CPU time for running processes
explanation: A page table maps virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers.

text: What is a page fault in virtual memory systems?
options: A syntax error in the running program code
An access to a page not in main memory now
A hardware malfunction in memory module
A failure of CPU to execute an instruction
explanation: A page fault occurs when accessing a page not currently loaded in physical memory.

text: What is a frame in physical memory?
options: A fixed-size block of physical RAM memory
A unit of data transmitted over a network
A type of CPU register storing addresses
A decorative border around monitor screen
explanation: A frame is a fixed-size block of physical memory corresponding to a page.

text: What is swapping in memory management?
options: Exchanging CPU cores between processes
Replacing old hardware with new hardware
Switching between different user accounts
Moving process between memory and disk
explanation: Swapping moves processes between main memory and disk to free up memory.

text: What is internal fragmentation in memory?
options: Memory corrupted due to hardware failure
File fragments scattered across the disk
Free memory between allocated blocks here
Wasted space inside an allocated partition
explanation: Internal fragmentation is wasted space inside an allocated partition that is larger than needed.

text: What is external fragmentation in memory?
options: A process using more than available memory
Memory corruption from external hardware
Free memory scattered in small pieces now
Memory allocated to already exited processes
explanation: External fragmentation is free memory scattered in small non-contiguous blocks.

text: What is the logical address space of a process?
options: Address of the process control block here
Set of all addresses generated by process
Total amount of RAM installed in computer
Physical location of process on the disk
explanation: The logical address space is the set of all addresses generated by the CPU for a process.

text: How does the LRU page replacement algorithm work?
options: It replaces the page not used for the longest time
It randomly selects any page for replacement now
It replaces the page used furthest in the future
It replaces pages in the order they were loaded in
explanation: LRU replaces the page not used for the longest period, approximating optimal replacement.

text: What is the Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)?
options: A register storing the base address of process
A buffer for storing data being written to disk
A queue for processes waiting for memory alloc
A fast cache for recent page table entries only
explanation: The TLB is a fast hardware cache for recently used page table entries.

text: What is Belady's anomaly in page replacement?
options: Page faults are constant regardless of the frame count
More frames always reduce the number of page faults
Belady's anomaly only occurs with the optimal algorithm
More frames can increase page faults with FIFO scheme
explanation: More frames can paradoxically increase page faults with FIFO replacement.

text: How does segmentation differ from paging?
options: Neither technique uses any address translation now
Both use exactly the same fixed-size memory blocks
Segmentation uses fixed-size; paging uses variable
Paging uses fixed-size; segmentation uses variable
explanation: Paging uses fixed-size blocks; segmentation uses variable-size logical segments.

text: What is demand paging in virtual memory?
options: Loading all pages into memory at startup
Requiring users to manually load needed pages
Pre-loading all pages from disk before start
Loading pages only when they are accessed now
explanation: Demand paging loads pages only when accessed, reducing initial load time.

text: What is the working set model in virtual memory?
options: Pages a process actively uses in time window
Set of all pages stored on the hard disk now
Total physical memory available in computer
Total number of processes in ready queue
explanation: The working set is the set of pages actively used by a process in a recent time window.

text: What is thrashing in virtual memory systems?
options: A process running very fast and finishing quickly
The process of defragmenting virtual memory space
A technique to improve memory utilization efficiency
Excessive page faults causing most time spent swapping
explanation: Thrashing is when a process spends more time paging than executing.
