text: How does the FIFO page replacement algorithm work?
options: It replaces the page with fewest access counts
It replaces pages by their physical address now
It replaces the most recently loaded page here
It replaces the oldest page that was loaded in
explanation: FIFO replaces the oldest page loaded into memory regardless of usage.

text: What is the dirty bit used for in paging?
options: It marks pages containing errors or corrupted data
It indicates whether a page was modified in RAM
It shows whether a page is currently locked in RAM
It counts the number of times a page was accessed
explanation: The dirty bit indicates whether a page has been modified and needs to be written back.

text: What is a multi-level page table and why is it used?
options: A page table stored on multiple different disk drives
A hierarchical page table reducing memory overhead
A page table storing entries for multiple processes
A table mapping pages to multiple physical frames
explanation: Multi-level page tables reduce memory overhead by avoiding unused page table entries.

text: How does the inverted page table reduce memory usage?
options: It eliminates the need for any page table entirely
It uses one entry per virtual page for each process
It compresses page table entries to half their size
It uses one entry per physical frame not per page
explanation: An inverted page table has one entry per physical frame instead of per virtual page.

text: What is the clock (second-chance) page replacement algorithm?
options: It gives each page a second chance via reference bit
It always replaces the page with highest frame number
It schedules page replacements at fixed clock intervals
It replaces pages based on the actual wall clock time
explanation: The clock algorithm uses a reference bit giving pages a second chance before replacement.

text: What is copy-on-write in virtual memory management?
options: Copying entire memory space when a process is created
Sharing pages until write occurs then making private copy
Writing all memory contents to disk after every instruction
Preventing any process from writing to memory at all
explanation: COW shares pages until a write occurs, then creates a private copy for the writer.

text: How does ASLR improve security?
options: It disables virtual memory to improve system performance
It encrypts all data stored in process address space
It randomizes code and data locations to prevent exploits
It prevents all processes from accessing shared memory
explanation: ASLR randomizes memory layout to make exploit target addresses unpredictable.

text: What is the optimal page replacement algorithm's limitation?
options: It does not support virtual memory in any implementation
It causes more page faults than any other algorithm
It can only work with a single page frame in system
It requires future knowledge of page references to work
explanation: The optimal algorithm requires knowing future page references, which is impossible in practice.

text: How does huge page support improve TLB performance?
options: Huge pages reduce TLB size to improve lookup speed
Huge pages reduce TLB miss frequency with fewer entries
Huge pages eliminate the need for any TLB hardware now
Huge pages make TLB slower but more accurate in lookup
explanation: Huge pages reduce TLB entries needed, decreasing miss rates and improving translation.

text: What is the page-buffering algorithm used for?
options: Buffering network data in page-sized blocks only
Compressing pages to fit more data into memory now
Preventing page faults by locking all pages in RAM
Keeping a pool of free frames to speed replacement
explanation: Page buffering maintains free frames so pages can be loaded immediately during faults.

text: What is memory-mapped I/O and how does it work?
options: It stores all I/O data exclusively in the CPU registers
It maps physical memory addresses to network addresses
It maps I/O device registers into memory address space
It eliminates the need for any I/O devices in system
explanation: Memory-mapped I/O maps device registers into the memory address space for access.

text: What is kernel same-page merging (KSM) in Linux?
options: It merges all kernel modules into a single binary
It identifies and merges identical pages across procs
It prevents any page sharing between virtual machines
It splits each page into two smaller pages for speed
explanation: KSM merges identical pages across processes with copy-on-write, saving memory.

text: How does the buddy system manage memory allocation?
options: It assigns memory randomly without any structure here
It pairs every process with a buddy for backup now
It splits memory into power-of-two blocks recursively
It allocates memory in fixed blocks without splitting
explanation: The buddy system splits memory into power-of-two blocks and coalesces buddies when freed.

text: What is a file in operating system terminology?
options: A network protocol for data transfer
A physical storage device like a disk
A type of CPU instruction for processing
A named collection of related data items
explanation: A file is a named collection of related information stored on secondary storage.

text: What is a directory in a file system?
options: A hardware device for reading media
A physical partition on the hard disk
A structure containing info about files
A file type that cannot be modified now
explanation: A directory contains entries about files including names, attributes, and locations.

text: What is a file extension used for?
options: To indicate the type or format of a file
To increase the file size beyond limits
To encrypt the file contents securely now
To compress files for saving disk space
explanation: A file extension indicates the type or format of a file like .txt or .jpg.

text: What is the root directory in a file system?
options: Where all temporary files are stored
A directory only for administrators here
A directory storing the OS kernel files
The topmost directory in the hierarchy
explanation: The root directory is the topmost directory from which all others branch out.

text: What is a file's access permission used for?
options: Determining the file size on the disk
Setting the creation date of the entry
Controlling who can read, write, execute
Choosing which app opens the file type
explanation: Access permissions control which users can read, write, or execute a file.

text: What is sequential file access?
options: Writing files in reverse order from the end
Accessing files from multiple disks at once
Accessing any record directly by position
Reading records one after another in order
explanation: Sequential access reads records one after another in linear order.

text: What is the purpose of file system mounting?
options: Creating a backup copy of the file system
Making a file system accessible at a location
Physically installing new hard drive hardware
Removing a file system from the OS entirely
explanation: Mounting makes a file system accessible at a specific mount point in the directory tree.

text: What does the open() system call do for files?
options: It permanently deletes a file from system
It creates a brand new empty file on disk
It prepares a file for reading or writing
It changes the permissions of a file now
explanation: open() prepares a file for reading or writing and returns a file descriptor.

text: What is an absolute path in a file system?
options: A path containing only the file name itself
A path relative to current working directory
A complete path starting from root directory
A path that points to a deleted file now
explanation: An absolute path specifies the complete location starting from the root directory.

text: What is a symbolic link in a file system?
options: A file containing a path to another file
A physical copy of a file in another place
A special file storing encrypted disk data
A temporary file created during system boot
explanation: A symbolic link is a file containing a path pointing to another file or directory.

text: How does indexed allocation manage file disk blocks?
options: Blocks are linked using pointers in each block
An index block contains pointers to data blocks
Blocks are allocated randomly without tracking
All blocks are stored consecutively on disk
explanation: Indexed allocation uses an index block with pointers to all file data blocks.

text: What differs between a hard link and a symbolic link?
options: Hard links work across file systems but soft do not
Hard link points to inode; soft link points to path
Both hard and symbolic links are exactly identical
Symbolic links point to inodes; hard links to paths
explanation: A hard link points to the inode; a symbolic link contains a pathname to the target.

text: What is the inode structure in Unix file systems?
options: A structure storing file metadata and block pointers
A network node handling file transfer protocols
A hardware component managing disk read operations
A process monitoring file system integrity at boot
explanation: An inode stores file metadata and pointers to disk blocks containing the file data.

text: How does the FAT file system organize file allocation?
options: Using a file allocation table chaining disk blocks
Using B-tree indexes for each file on the disk now
Using extent-based allocation with balanced trees
Using inodes and indirect blocks like Unix systems
explanation: FAT uses a table where entries chain disk blocks together for each file.

text: What is journaling in modern file systems?
options: Recording user activity logs for security audit
Writing metadata changes to a log before commit
Storing journal entries for file content searching
Maintaining daily backup schedule of all files now
explanation: Journaling writes metadata changes to a log before applying them for crash recovery.

text: What is the free-space management bitmap method?
options: A method to compress free space for more storage
A technique to visualize the directory tree layout
Each bit represents a block allocation status bit
A graphical representation of files on screen
explanation: A bitmap uses one bit per block to efficiently track free and allocated blocks.

text: What is direct access file organization?
options: Any record can be accessed directly by position
Files can only be read sequentially from start
Files are accessed by searching all records first
Files can only be accessed through symbolic links
explanation: Direct access allows reading or writing any record by specifying its position.

text: What is the purpose of the Virtual File System layer?
options: It provides uniform interface to file system types
It creates virtual copies of files for backup
It manages virtual memory not file system at all
It encrypts all files stored on virtual disk drives
explanation: VFS provides a uniform interface allowing the OS to support multiple file system types.

text: What is file system consistency checking used for?
options: Ensuring all files have correct extensions set here
Checking if files contain valid program source
Verifying integrity and repairing corruption now
Checking if file system has enough free space left
explanation: Consistency checking verifies integrity and repairs inconsistencies from crashes.

text: How does contiguous allocation organize file storage?
options: Files are indexed using a multi-level tree structure
Each file occupies contiguous disk blocks together
Files are in random blocks scattered across the disk
Files are stored using linked list of distant blocks
explanation: Contiguous allocation stores files in consecutive disk blocks for good read performance.

text: How does ext4 use extents instead of block pointers?
options: Extents are only used for files smaller than 4 KB
An extent describes a contiguous run with one entry
Extents eliminate the need for any metadata on disk
Extents store each block address individually here
explanation: An extent describes a contiguous range of blocks with a single entry, reducing metadata.

text: What is a copy-on-write file system and its advantage?
options: It copies all data twice for redundancy on every write
It writes data in place and copies only on deletion
It never overwrites existing data ensuring consistency
It only allows reads and prevents all write operations
explanation: COW file systems never overwrite existing data, providing crash consistency and snapshots.

text: How does the B+ tree structure benefit NTFS?
options: It provides efficient O(log n) lookups and range scans
It only works with files smaller than one megabyte now
It stores file data directly inside B-tree leaf nodes
It eliminates the need for any disk space management
explanation: B+ trees provide O(log n) directory lookups and efficient range queries in NTFS.

text: What is the log-structured file system design philosophy?
options: LFS stores data in traditional inode-based tree layout
LFS writes all data and metadata sequentially to log
LFS requires all data to be stored in main memory now
LFS uses random writes to maximize disk head movement
explanation: LFS writes everything sequentially to a log, converting random writes to sequential ones.

text: How does ZFS ensure data integrity using checksums?
options: ZFS checksums are stored alongside data in same block
ZFS stores checksums in parent blocks enabling healing
ZFS does not use any checksums for integrity checking
ZFS only checksums metadata, not actual file data now
explanation: ZFS stores checksums in parent blocks, enabling detection and self-healing of corruption.

text: What is the TRIM command for SSDs in file systems?
options: It trims the physical size of the SSD device now
It informs SSD which blocks are no longer in use
It compresses data on SSD to save storage space
It increases read speed by defragmenting the SSD
explanation: TRIM informs the SSD which blocks are free so its garbage collector can erase them proactively.

text: How do deduplication techniques work in enterprise storage?
options: They duplicate important files for safety purposes
They compress each file individually to reduce size
They identify and eliminate duplicate blocks to save
They remove all files not accessed in recent months
explanation: Deduplication identifies identical blocks and stores only one copy with references.

text: What differs between ordered and writeback journaling?
options: Ordered mode does not use a journal; writeback does
Writeback writes data first; ordered metadata first now
Both modes write data and metadata in the same order
Ordered writes data before metadata; writeback either
explanation: Ordered writes data before metadata journal entry; writeback may journal metadata first.

text: What is the purpose of the superblock in Unix file systems?
options: It stores the boot loader program for the OS kernel
It contains critical file system metadata like size now
It stores the contents of the largest file in system
It is extra storage reserved for the root user only
explanation: The superblock contains critical metadata about the file system including size and status.

text: How does FUSE work in Linux?
options: FUSE moves all file system code into kernel space
FUSE allows implementing file systems in user space
FUSE replaces the VFS layer with a simpler version
FUSE is a hardware device for connecting ext drives
explanation: FUSE allows creating file systems in user space without kernel modifications.

text: What is secondary storage in a computer system?
options: Non-volatile storage for permanent data
The main memory used by running procs
The CPU cache memory for fast access
Registers inside the CPU for computation
explanation: Secondary storage provides non-volatile, permanent data retention even when powered off.

text: What is a hard disk drive composed of?
options: Optical discs using laser beams to read the data
Solid-state flash memory chips sealed in case
Spinning magnetic platters with read-write heads
Volatile memory modules losing data when off now
explanation: An HDD consists of spinning magnetic platters with read-write heads.

text: What is disk seek time?
options: Time to transfer data from disk to memory
Time to format the entire disk from scratch
Time to move head to the correct track now
Time for platter to complete one rotation
explanation: Seek time is the time to move the read-write head to the correct track.

text: What is a solid-state drive (SSD)?
options: A mechanical drive with spinning platters
A volatile device requiring constant power now
Storage using flash memory, no moving parts
A type of optical disc using laser for data
explanation: An SSD uses flash memory with no moving parts for faster access and durability.

text: What is disk rotational latency?
options: Total time to read an entire disk platter now
Time for head to move to the correct track
Time for desired sector to rotate under head
Time required to start spinning disk platters
explanation: Rotational latency is waiting for the desired sector to rotate under the head.

text: What is a disk partition?
options: A hardware device connecting disks to CPU
A logical division of disk into sections
A type of disk error corrupting stored data
A physical break in disk platter surface
explanation: A partition is a logical division of a physical disk into separate independent sections.

text: What is the transfer rate of a disk?
options: Speed at which disk platters rotate per minute
Time taken to partition the disk into sections
Rate of data flow between disk and computer now
Rate at which disk head moves across all tracks
explanation: Transfer rate is the rate of data flow between disk and computer during operations.

text: What is a disk track on a magnetic hard drive?
options: A type of error correction code on the disk
A line connecting disk to the motherboard now
A physical groove cut into disk for head path
A concentric circle on disk platter surface
explanation: A track is a concentric circular path on the platter surface where data is stored.

text: What is a sector on a hard disk drive?
options: Total storage capacity of one disk platter now
A complete circular track on the platter now
Smallest addressable unit of storage on disk
Hardware component controlling the read head
explanation: A sector is the smallest addressable unit of storage on a disk, typically 512 or 4096 bytes.

text: What is RAID in secondary storage?
options: A method to encrypt data on single disk
A type of RAM for caching disk operations
A protocol for transferring data on network
Combining multiple disks for redundancy
explanation: RAID combines multiple disks for improved performance, reliability, or both.

text: How does the SCAN disk scheduling algorithm work?
options: Head moves one direction servicing then reverses
It randomly selects the next request from queue
It services requests in arrival order always
It always jumps to the closest request first now
explanation: SCAN moves the head in one direction servicing requests then reverses at the end.

text: How does SSTF scheduling differ from FCFS?
options: SSTF services requests in the exact arrival order
SSTF always moves the head to outermost track first
SSTF and FCFS produce identical scheduling results
SSTF services the request nearest to current head
explanation: SSTF selects the closest request to minimize seek time; FCFS uses arrival order.

text: What is the difference between RAID 0 and RAID 1?
options: RAID 0 provides redundancy; RAID 1 stripes only
RAID 0 stripes for speed; RAID 1 mirrors for safety
Both provide identical performance and safety here
RAID 0 uses parity; RAID 1 uses error correction
explanation: RAID 0 stripes for speed without redundancy; RAID 1 mirrors for fault tolerance.

text: What is the C-SCAN disk scheduling algorithm?
options: It services the closest request to minimize seeking
It processes requests in the order submitted always
It scans in both directions alternately each time
Head services one direction then jumps back to start
explanation: C-SCAN services in one direction then jumps back without servicing on return.

text: What is the purpose of disk defragmentation for HDDs?
options: Encrypting all data stored on the hard disk drive
Removing all deleted files permanently from disk
Reorganizing fragmented files into contiguous blocks
Increasing physical speed of disk platter rotation
explanation: Defragmentation reorganizes files into contiguous blocks to reduce seek times.

text: How does RAID 5 provide fault tolerance?
options: It keeps three copies of every data block on disk
It distributes parity information across all drives
It mirrors all data on two separate disk drives
It uses error-correcting codes on a separate disk
explanation: RAID 5 distributes parity across all disks; one disk failure can be recovered.

text: Why is SSTF disk scheduling prone to starvation?
options: Far requests may wait indefinitely for any service
Because it services all requests equally in order
It services farthest request first causing slow times
SSTF scheduling never causes starvation of requests
explanation: SSTF can starve far requests if new closer requests keep arriving.

text: What is the disk controller's role?
options: It manages CPU scheduling for all running processes
It handles network routing for file sharing on LAN
It controls user interface for file management
It translates logical addresses to physical locations
explanation: The disk controller translates logical block addresses to physical disk locations.

text: What is wear leveling in solid-state drives?
options: Technique to physically level SSD circuit boards
Distributing writes evenly to extend cell lifespan
A process to cool down overheated memory chips now
A method to reduce power consumption of SSD drive
explanation: Wear leveling distributes writes evenly across NAND cells to extend SSD lifespan.

text: What is the boot block and where is it stored?
options: It stores the file system journal for crash recovery
It stores user data in the first track of disk
It contains bootstrap code in a fixed disk location
It contains disk encryption keys for secure access
explanation: The boot block contains bootstrap loader code in a fixed disk location.

text: How does LOOK improve upon the SCAN algorithm?
options: LOOK eliminates the need for any head movement
LOOK moves head to the physical end of disk
LOOK reverses at the last request, not disk end
LOOK processes requests in strict FCFS order now
explanation: LOOK reverses at the last pending request rather than traveling to the disk end.

text: What is the write amplification problem in SSDs?
options: Writes are amplified to improve SSD performance
SSDs amplify signals to write data faster on chip
Writes are duplicated to multiple chips for safety
More physical data is written than logical requested
explanation: Write amplification occurs when more physical data is written than logically requested.

text: How does RAID 6 improve on RAID 5 fault tolerance?
options: RAID 6 uses triple mirroring of all data blocks
RAID 6 uses dual parity allowing two disk failures
RAID 6 stores four copies of parity on one drive
RAID 6 and RAID 5 offer exactly the same safety
explanation: RAID 6 uses dual parity allowing survival of two simultaneous disk failures.

text: What is the FTL in solid-state drives?
options: A network layer for transferring data between SSDs
A physical layer connecting SSD to motherboard
Firmware mapping logical addresses to flash pages
A software tool for formatting flash drives on PC
explanation: The Flash Translation Layer maps logical addresses to physical NAND flash pages.

text: What is shingled magnetic recording for HDDs?
options: It uses circular tracks with no overlap at all
It uses multiple read heads for faster read speed
Tracks overlap like shingles for higher density now
It eliminates platters using solid-state memory
explanation: SMR overlaps tracks like shingles for higher density but requires sequential writing.

text: What is NCQ in SATA drives?
options: It prevents commands from executing out of order
It queues user interface commands for the OS now
It lets drives reorder commands for optimal execution
It queues network commands for remote disk access
explanation: NCQ lets drives accept multiple commands and reorder them for optimal execution.

text: How does NVMe improve upon AHCI for SSDs?
options: NVMe uses the same command set as legacy IDE chips
NVMe and AHCI provide identical performance for SSDs
NVMe uses deeper queues and lower latency than AHCI
NVMe is designed for spinning disks limiting SSDs
explanation: NVMe supports many more queues and commands with lower latency via PCIe.

text: What is the NOOP I/O scheduler and when is it used?
options: NOOP completely eliminates all I/O scheduling here
NOOP is the most advanced scheduler for disk drives
NOOP uses simple FIFO queue with minimal ordering
NOOP performs extensive sorting of I/O for HDDs
explanation: NOOP uses simple FIFO queue, appropriate for SSDs and VMs where hardware optimizes.

text: What is the RAID write hole problem?
options: Power failure during write leaves parity inconsistent
It prevents any write from completing on RAID now
It is a physical hole in disk platter causing damage
It only occurs when reading data from RAID array now
explanation: A power failure during write can leave data and parity inconsistent across stripes.

text: How does host-managed zoned storage work with ZNS SSDs?
options: SSD firmware manages zones without any host input
ZNS SSDs work identically to traditional block SSDs
The host has no control over data placement here
Host manages zones and writes sequentially in them
explanation: ZNS divides storage into zones requiring sequential writes, reducing garbage collection.

text: What is an I/O device in a computer system?
options: A device that only stores the OS kernel
A device generating electrical power supply
A device used for input, output, or both
A device that only processes internal data
explanation: An I/O device sends data to or receives data from a computer.

text: What is a device driver in an operating system?
options: Software controlling a specific hardware device
A person who operates hardware devices
A physical cable connecting devices to the PC
A tool for testing hardware functionality now
explanation: A device driver provides an interface between the OS and a specific hardware device.

text: What is programmed I/O for device communication?
options: Data transfers directly between device and memory
OS ignores device until a user requests the data
CPU continuously checks device status for data
Device notifies CPU when it completes operation
explanation: In programmed I/O the CPU continuously polls the device status register.

text: What is interrupt-driven I/O?
options: CPU continuously polls device for data status
OS disables all interrupts during I/O operations
Data transfers without any CPU involvement here
Device signals CPU via interrupt when it is ready
explanation: The device sends an interrupt to the CPU when ready, freeing the CPU meanwhile.

text: What is Direct Memory Access used for?
options: Transferring data between I/O devices and memory
Directly accessing CPU registers from a program
Providing direct access to the OS kernel code now
Managing memory allocation for running processes
explanation: DMA transfers data directly between I/O devices and memory without CPU involvement.

text: What is a block device in I/O classification?
options: A device transferring data one character at a time
A device that blocks all other devices from running
A device transferring data in fixed-size blocks now
A device working only when the system is blocked
explanation: A block device transfers data in fixed-size blocks, supporting random access.

text: What is a character device in I/O classification?
options: A device storing character encoding tables on disk
A device displaying only text characters on screen
A device transferring data one character at a time
A device used for character recognition in documents
explanation: A character device transfers data one byte at a time in a sequential stream.

text: What is the purpose of an I/O port?
options: An address for CPU to communicate with devices
A physical hole for ventilation of the case
A port used only for internet connections now
A type of software app for managing printers
explanation: An I/O port is a set of addresses used by the CPU to communicate with devices.

text: What is buffering in I/O systems?
options: Encrypting data before sending to output devices
Temporarily storing data in transit between devices
Permanently storing data on the hard disk drive now
Deleting data no longer needed by any process here
explanation: Buffering temporarily stores data being transferred between devices or applications.

text: What is the I/O request lifecycle?
options: Time from device manufacture to its disposal
Lifespan of cable connecting device to computer
Sequence from application request to I/O completion
Period when I/O device is powered on and running
explanation: The lifecycle is the sequence from application I/O request through to completion.

text: How do memory-mapped and port-mapped I/O differ?
options: Memory-mapped requires special CPU instructions for access
Port-mapped I/O is faster than memory-mapped in every case
Both methods use exactly the same addressing mechanism now
Memory-mapped uses address space; port-mapped uses separate
explanation: Memory-mapped uses the address space; port-mapped uses a separate I/O address space.

text: How does double buffering improve I/O performance?
options: One buffer fills while other is being processed by CPU
It eliminates the need for any buffering in the system
It slows down I/O by requiring twice the memory space
It doubles the speed of the physical I/O device hardware
explanation: Double buffering overlaps I/O and processing by using two alternating buffers.

text: What is device-independent I/O software?
options: It handles only network device communication protocol
It makes all devices behave like character devices
It eliminates the need for any device drivers at all
It provides uniform interfacing for diverse devices
explanation: Device-independent I/O provides a uniform interface for diverse device types.

text: What is an interrupt vector table used for?
options: Storing data to be written to I/O devices later
Tracking which I/O devices are powered on currently
Mapping interrupt numbers to handler routines fast
Counting total number of interrupts since boot time
explanation: The interrupt vector table maps interrupt numbers to their handler routine addresses.

text: What is the role of the I/O scheduler?
options: It schedules manufacturing of I/O device hardware
It orders and merges I/O requests for optimal access
It schedules network packets for wire transmission
It determines which user can log into the system now
explanation: The I/O scheduler orders and merges requests to optimize device access patterns.

text: How does spooling differ from buffering?
options: Spooling uses disk as buffer for slow device queues
Buffering uses disk while spooling uses only main memory
Spooling is only used for input devices like keyboards
Spooling and buffering are the same I/O technique here
explanation: Spooling uses disk for slow output device queues; buffering typically uses main memory.

text: What is the device status table?
options: Storing serial numbers of all connected devices here
Maintaining firmware update history for every device
Displaying device information to end user on screen
Tracking status and request queue for each device now
explanation: The device status table tracks status and pending requests for each I/O device.

text: How do blocking and non-blocking I/O differ?
options: Blocking suspends process; non-blocking returns immediately
Both blocking and non-blocking I/O work the same way
Blocking I/O is always faster than non-blocking I/O now
Non-blocking suspends process until I/O completes fully
explanation: Blocking suspends the process; non-blocking returns immediately with available data.

text: What is the DMA controller's role during transfer?
options: It performs all CPU computations during the transfer
It compiles programs while data is being transferred
It encrypts all data before transferring it to memory
It manages data transfer between device and memory now
explanation: The DMA controller manages the entire transfer independently, interrupting CPU only when done.

text: How does asynchronous I/O differ from non-blocking?
options: Asynchronous I/O is identical to non-blocking I/O now
Non-blocking sends completion notification to caller
Asynchronous notifies process upon I/O completion later
Asynchronous blocks process until data is fully ready
explanation: Asynchronous I/O notifies the process upon completion; non-blocking returns partial results immediately.

text: How does scatter-gather DMA improve I/O performance?
options: It transfers data to non-contiguous memory regions now
It eliminates DMA and uses programmed I/O instead
It gathers all data into single buffer before transfer
It scatters data randomly across memory for security
explanation: Scatter-gather DMA transfers to/from non-contiguous memory buffers in one operation.

text: What is the IOCP model in Windows?
options: A port number used for network socket communication
A scalable model for handling many async I/O events
A model that blocks all threads until I/O finishes
A physical port for connecting I/O devices to PC
explanation: IOCP is a scalable asynchronous I/O model where completions are queued to a port for thread pool processing.

text: How does Linux epoll improve over select()?
options: epoll uses O(1) notification instead of O(n) scanning
epoll only works with network sockets not file I/O
epoll and select have identical performance overall
epoll monitors fewer file descriptors than select
explanation: epoll uses O(1) event notification instead of O(n) scanning of all descriptors.

text: What is IOMMU and how does it improve I/O security?
options: It is a type of MMU used exclusively for memory mgmt
It encrypts all data transferred between devices and CPU
It translates device addresses providing memory isolation
It is a memory unit storing I/O driver code only
explanation: IOMMU translates device addresses and enforces access control, preventing DMA attacks.

text: How do edge-triggered and level-triggered I/O notifications differ?
options: Edge-triggered notifications are always slower than level
Both notification types trigger in the same situation here
Level-triggered only works with sockets not with disk I/O
Edge notifies on state transition; level while state holds
explanation: Edge triggers on state transitions; level triggers while the condition holds true.

text: What is the io_uring interface in modern Linux?
options: It provides high-performance async I/O via ring buffers
It creates circular network connections between nodes
It manages circular disk platters in HDD controllers
It is a graphical interface for configuring I/O drivers
explanation: io_uring uses shared ring buffers for high-performance async I/O with minimal system calls.

text: How does RDMA work?
options: It requires CPU to copy all data for remote transfers
It allows direct memory access between remote machines
It only works within a single machine for local DMA
It uses traditional TCP/IP for remote memory operations
explanation: RDMA allows direct memory access between remote machines through the NIC without remote CPU involvement.

text: What is interrupt coalescing and why is it used?
options: It eliminates all interrupts using only polling for I/O
It combines multiple events into fewer interrupt signals
Each I/O event generates a separate interrupt always
It splits one interrupt into multiple signals for detail
explanation: Interrupt coalescing batches multiple events into fewer interrupts to reduce overhead.

text: What is the SPDK approach to storage?
options: It uses user-space drivers and polling for low latency
It is a standard kernel-based storage driver framework
It is a hardware kit for assembling storage arrays now
It provides a graphical toolkit for storage management
explanation: SPDK uses user-space drivers and polling to eliminate kernel overhead for ultra-low latency.

text: How does virtio enable efficient I/O virtualization?
options: It uses hardware passthrough without any abstraction
It eliminates the need for any I/O drivers in the VM
It provides standard paravirtual I/O interface for VMs
It emulates physical hardware perfectly for each VM
explanation: virtio provides a paravirtual I/O interface using shared ring buffers, avoiding emulation overhead.

text: What is the difference between protection and security?
options: Protection controls internal; security guards external
Neither is relevant to modern operating systems
Security controls internal; protection guards external
Protection and security mean the same thing
explanation: Protection controls internal access; security defends against external threats.

text: What is authentication in OS security?
options: Installing antivirus software on the system
Compressing data to save storage on disk
Verifying the identity of a user or process
Encrypting all files stored on the disk
explanation: Authentication verifies the identity of a user or process before granting access.

text: What is an access control list in operating systems?
options: A list specifying user permissions for objects
A list of all network connections currently
A list of all hardware devices attached now
A list of all software installed on PC
explanation: An ACL specifies which users have what permissions for a particular resource.

text: What is a password used for in OS security?
options: Encrypting the entire hard disk contents
Compressing files to save storage on disk
Increasing the processing speed of the CPU
Authenticating user identity before access
explanation: A password authenticates a user's identity before granting system access.

text: What is malware in computer security?
options: A type of hardware failure in the computer
Malicious software designed to harm systems
Software improving computer performance
A legitimate tool for administration tasks
explanation: Malware is malicious software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorized access.

text: What is encryption used for in OS security?
options: Converting data to unreadable format for privacy
Deleting sensitive files permanently from the disk
Compressing files to reduce their storage size now
Increasing the speed of data transfer on network
explanation: Encryption converts data into an unreadable format to protect confidentiality.

text: What is a firewall in computer security?
options: A tool for formatting hard drives securely now
A device supplying emergency power to the PC
A physical wall preventing computer overheating
A system filtering network traffic based on rules
explanation: A firewall monitors and filters network traffic based on security rules.

text: What is a computer virus?
options: A tool for legitimate system maintenance tasks
A type of operating system for mobile phones
A self-replicating program attaching to files
A hardware malfunction from overheating chips
explanation: A virus self-replicates by attaching to other files and spreading when executed.

text: What is the principle of least privilege?
options: Granting users only minimum permissions needed
Assigning same permissions to every system user
Giving every user full administrator access
Preventing all users from accessing the system
explanation: Least privilege grants users only the minimum permissions necessary for their tasks.

text: What is a Trojan horse in computer security?
options: A security tool protecting against all viruses
A hardware device used for network security
A backup system storing copies of vital files
Malware disguised as legitimate software here
explanation: A Trojan horse is malware disguised as legitimate software to trick users.

text: What is a buffer overflow attack?
options: It fills printer buffer with too many print jobs
It fills the disk buffer causing file system crash
It overwrites memory beyond buffer to inject code
It overflows network buffer causing packet loss now
explanation: Buffer overflow writes beyond a buffer boundary to overwrite memory and inject code.

text: What is role-based access control (RBAC)?
options: Assigning permissions to roles then roles to users
Assigning permissions directly to individual users
Assigning all permissions to every user in system
Removing all access control from the OS entirely
explanation: RBAC assigns permissions to roles, then assigns roles to users.

text: How does the access matrix represent protection?
options: Rows are resources and columns are users only now
It is a physical matrix of hardware protection now
Rows are domains, columns are objects with rights
It only represents network access permissions here
explanation: The access matrix has domains as rows and objects as columns with access rights in cells.

text: What is a denial-of-service attack?
options: Deleting all files from the target system forever
Denying users permission to install software
Encrypting data and demanding ransom for the key
Overwhelming system with requests to make unavailable
explanation: A DoS attack floods a system to exhaust resources and make it unavailable.

text: What is a rootkit and why is it dangerous?
options: Malware hiding itself providing persistent root access
A security scanner checking for root vulnerabilities
A toolkit for administrators to manage root access
A legitimate tool for recovering forgotten passwords
explanation: A rootkit hides its presence and provides persistent privileged access to attackers.

text: How does sandboxing improve application security?
options: It isolates apps in restricted execution environments
It prevents applications from being installed at all
It buries security threats deep in system storage
It encrypts all data processed by sandboxed apps now
explanation: Sandboxing isolates applications in restricted environments to contain potential damage.

text: What is two-factor authentication (2FA)?
options: Using same password on two different computers
Logging into two different accounts at same time
Using two different passwords for one account
Requiring two independent verification for access
explanation: 2FA requires two independent verification methods to authenticate a user.

text: What is an intrusion detection system (IDS)?
options: Installing security patches on system software now
Managing user passwords and auth credentials only
Monitoring system activity to detect malicious acts
Detecting physical break-ins at office buildings
explanation: An IDS monitors system activity for signs of malicious behavior or attacks.

text: How do symmetric and asymmetric encryption differ?
options: Symmetric uses two keys; asymmetric uses one shared
Both encryption types use the same key mechanism now
Symmetric encryption is always weaker than asymmetric
Symmetric uses one shared key; asymmetric a key pair
explanation: Symmetric uses one shared key; asymmetric uses a public-private key pair.

text: What is privilege escalation in system security?
options: Exploiting flaws to gain higher privileges than allowed
Reducing user privileges for security enforcement now
Escalating a security ticket to management team here
Legitimately granting more permissions by an admin
explanation: Privilege escalation exploits vulnerabilities to gain unauthorized higher access.

text: How does return-oriented programming bypass NX protection?
options: ROP disables the NX bit in CPU page table entries
ROP chains existing code gadgets to execute payload
ROP injects new executable code into stack directly
ROP only works on systems without memory protection
explanation: ROP chains existing code gadgets ending in return instructions to bypass NX protection.

text: What is the Trusted Platform Module and its security role?
options: A hardware chip providing cryptographic security ops
A kernel module managing user authentication only
A software module scanning for viruses on system
A network module filtering traffic like a firewall
explanation: TPM is a hardware chip providing cryptographic functions and root of trust.

text: How does mandatory access control differ from discretionary?
options: MAC uses system-enforced labels; DAC lets owners control
MAC and DAC are different names for the same mechanism
Both give resource owners full control over all access
MAC lets owners set permissions; DAC uses system policy
explanation: MAC uses system-enforced labels; DAC lets resource owners determine permissions.

text: What is a side-channel attack and how does Spectre work?
options: Spectre exploits speculative execution via cache timing
Spectre exploits buffer overflows in browser apps only
It attacks through unused network ports on system
Side-channel attacks directly modify target source code
explanation: Spectre exploits speculative execution to leak data through cache timing differences.

text: How does SELinux implement mandatory access control?
options: SELinux assigns security labels to subjects and objects
SELinux only encrypts file system data for privacy
SELinux manages firewalls but not file access at all
SELinux replaces Linux kernel with a secure version
explanation: SELinux assigns security labels and enforces policies based on these labels.

text: What is a zero-day vulnerability?
options: A flaw affecting zero users due to limited deployment
A flaw existing only on the day software is released
A flaw known for zero days and still unpatched now
A flaw taking zero effort to exploit using any tool
explanation: A zero-day is an unknown flaw exploited before a patch is available.

text: How does control flow integrity protect against code-reuse?
options: CFI encrypts all control flow instructions in binary
CFI ensures execution follows predetermined control graph
CFI only protects against buffer overflow not ROP now
CFI prevents any branching instructions from executing
explanation: CFI verifies control flow follows a predetermined graph, preventing redirection attacks.

text: What is the confused deputy problem in security?
options: A user confused about which password to use for login
A privileged program tricked into misusing its authority
An admin who misconfigures the security settings now
A process confusing virtual and physical memory addrs
explanation: A privileged program is tricked into misusing its authority by a less-privileged attacker.

text: How does memory tagging (MTE) prevent memory safety bugs?
options: MTE tags memory with metadata detecting out-of-bounds
MTE increases the amount of physical RAM available here
MTE encrypts all memory contents using hardware now
MTE only works with interpreted languages not compiled
explanation: MTE tags memory allocations and checks pointer tags match on access, detecting bugs.

text: What is attestation in trusted computing?
options: A technique for testing software under heavy load stress
A legal document certifying software license compliance
Cryptographic proof that system is in a known trusted state
A user declaring identity through a sworn statement now
explanation: Attestation provides cryptographic proof of system state to a remote verifier.

text: Which of the following is a primary goal of an operating system?
options: Convenience for the user
Designing hardware circuits
Writing application software
Manufacturing peripheral devices
explanation: A primary goal of an operating system is to make the computer system convenient and easy to use for the user.

text: What is a batch operating system?
options: An OS that processes jobs in groups without user interaction
An OS that allows real-time user input during execution
An OS designed only for mobile devices
An OS that runs a single program at a time with user control
explanation: A batch operating system collects and groups jobs together, processing them sequentially without direct user interaction during execution.

text: Which of the following best describes a time-sharing operating system?
options: An OS that allocates dedicated hardware to each user
An OS that allows multiple users to share CPU time interactively
An OS that processes only one task at a time
An OS used exclusively for scientific calculations
explanation: A time-sharing OS allows multiple users to interact with the computer simultaneously by rapidly switching the CPU among them.

text: What does the term 'kernel mode' mean in an operating system?
options: A privileged execution mode with full hardware access
A mode where only user applications can run
A power-saving mode that reduces CPU speed
A mode used exclusively during system shutdown
explanation: Kernel mode is a privileged CPU execution mode where the operating system has unrestricted access to all hardware and can execute any instruction.

text: Which component of a computer system directly executes instructions?
options: Central Processing Unit (CPU)
Hard disk drive
Network interface card
Power supply unit
explanation: The CPU is the component that fetches, decodes, and executes instructions from programs stored in memory.

text: What is multiprogramming in operating systems?
options: Running multiple programs in memory to maximize CPU utilization
Running the same program on multiple computers
Writing programs in multiple programming languages
Using multiple monitors for display output
explanation: Multiprogramming keeps multiple programs in memory simultaneously so the CPU can switch to another job when the current one waits for I/O, maximizing CPU utilization.

text: What is the function of an operating system's resource manager?
options: Allocating and managing hardware and software resources
Designing new hardware components
Writing user application programs
Connecting to the internet
explanation: The OS acts as a resource manager that allocates and manages hardware resources like CPU, memory, and I/O devices among competing processes.

text: Which of the following is a network operating system?
options: Windows Server
MS-DOS
Android
Embedded Linux
explanation: Windows Server is a network operating system designed to manage network resources, provide services to clients, and handle network communication.

text: What is the purpose of a command-line interface in an operating system?
options: To allow users to interact with the OS by typing text commands
To display graphical windows and icons
To manage the file allocation table
To compile source code into machine language
explanation: A command-line interface (CLI) allows users to interact with the operating system by typing text-based commands, which the OS interprets and executes.

text: What is an embedded operating system?
options: An OS designed for specialized devices with limited resources
An OS used only on desktop computers
An OS that requires a graphical user interface
An OS that runs only in cloud environments
explanation: An embedded operating system is designed to run on specialized devices like routers, medical equipment, and IoT devices that have limited computing resources.

text: What is the difference between a hard real-time system and a soft real-time system?
options: Hard real-time guarantees deadlines while soft real-time allows occasional misses
Hard real-time uses hardware timers while soft real-time uses software timers
Hard real-time supports more users than soft real-time
Hard real-time is faster than soft real-time in all scenarios
explanation: In a hard real-time system, missing a deadline causes system failure, while a soft real-time system can tolerate occasional deadline misses with degraded performance.

text: How does a multiprocessor system differ from a multicomputer system?
options: Multiprocessor shares memory while multicomputer has distributed memory
Multiprocessor uses one OS while multicomputer uses multiple keyboards
Multiprocessor is slower than multicomputer in all workloads
Multiprocessor only runs batch jobs while multicomputer handles interactive tasks
explanation: A multiprocessor system has multiple CPUs sharing the same physical memory, while a multicomputer system consists of separate computers each with their own memory connected via a network.

text: What is the role of a timer interrupt in operating system design?
options: To prevent user processes from monopolizing the CPU indefinitely
To speed up arithmetic calculations
To increase the amount of available memory
To manage network packet routing
explanation: A timer interrupt ensures the OS regains control of the CPU after a set time period, preventing any single user process from running indefinitely and enabling multitasking.

text: What is the purpose of privileged instructions in a CPU?
options: To restrict certain dangerous operations to kernel mode only
To make programs execute faster in user mode
To allow users to directly access I/O devices
To enable parallel processing on multiple cores
explanation: Privileged instructions can only be executed in kernel mode, preventing user programs from performing dangerous operations like directly accessing hardware or disabling interrupts.

text: How does symmetric multiprocessing (SMP) differ from asymmetric multiprocessing?
options: SMP treats all processors equally while asymmetric assigns a master processor
SMP uses one processor while asymmetric uses many
SMP is only for real-time systems while asymmetric is for batch systems
SMP requires identical software on each processor while asymmetric does not
explanation: In SMP, all processors are peers and run the OS equally, while in asymmetric multiprocessing, a master processor controls the system and assigns work to subordinate processors.

text: What is the purpose of a system call in the operating system?
options: To provide an interface for user programs to request OS kernel services
To compile high-level code into machine language
To physically connect hardware devices to the motherboard
To partition the hard disk into volumes
explanation: A system call provides a programmatic interface for user programs to request services from the operating system kernel, such as file operations, process creation, and memory allocation.

text: What does Direct Memory Access (DMA) accomplish in a computer system?
options: Allows devices to transfer data to memory without CPU intervention for each byte
Directly increases the size of physical memory
Enables memory to execute instructions without the CPU
Provides encryption for data stored in memory
explanation: DMA allows I/O devices to transfer blocks of data directly to or from memory without the CPU being involved in every byte transfer, freeing the CPU for other tasks.

text: What is the memory hierarchy in a computer system?
options: Arrangement of storage from fast/small registers to slow/large secondary storage
A method for encrypting data in memory
The process of allocating memory to user processes
A technique for compressing files on disk
explanation: The memory hierarchy arranges storage levels from fastest and smallest (registers, cache) to slowest and largest (secondary storage), balancing speed, cost, and capacity.

text: What is the purpose of an interrupt vector in an operating system?
options: To store addresses of interrupt service routines for each interrupt type
To count the number of interrupts that have occurred
To prioritize user processes over system processes
To buffer data during I/O transfers
explanation: An interrupt vector is a table that stores the addresses of interrupt service routines (ISRs), allowing the CPU to quickly look up and jump to the correct handler for each type of interrupt.

text: What does it mean for an operating system to provide abstraction?
options: It hides complex hardware details behind simpler interfaces
It makes the hardware run at faster clock speeds
It reduces the physical size of hardware components
It eliminates the need for device drivers entirely
explanation: OS abstraction hides the complexity of hardware details by providing simpler, uniform interfaces such as files for disk access and processes for program execution.

text: What is the key difference between a Type-0 and a Type-2 hypervisor in virtualization?
options: Type-0 is firmware-based while Type-2 runs as an application on a host OS
Type-0 is software while Type-2 is hardware
Type-0 supports only Linux while Type-2 supports only Windows
Type-0 is slower than Type-2 in all scenarios
explanation: A Type-0 hypervisor is implemented in the hardware firmware itself, while a Type-2 hypervisor runs as a regular application on top of a host operating system, adding an extra layer of overhead.

text: What is paravirtualization and how does it improve VM performance?
options: Guests are modified to make hypercalls instead of privileged instructions, reducing overhead
Guests run unmodified but the hypervisor interprets all instructions
The host OS is removed and guests run directly on hardware
Multiple VMs share the same guest operating system image
explanation: In paravirtualization, guest OS code is modified to replace privileged instructions with explicit calls (hypercalls) to the hypervisor, reducing the overhead of trapping and emulating privileged operations.

text: What role does the IOMMU play in modern computer architectures?
options: It translates device I/O addresses to physical memory addresses and provides isolation
It manages CPU cache coherence across multiple cores
It schedules interrupt handling among processors
It encrypts all data transferred between CPU and memory
explanation: The IOMMU translates I/O device virtual addresses to physical memory addresses and provides memory isolation, preventing devices from accessing memory regions they should not, which is critical for virtualization and security.

text: Why is the separation of user mode and kernel mode considered insufficient for modern security?
options: Side-channel attacks like Spectre can leak kernel data even from user mode
User mode programs cannot make system calls
Kernel mode does not have access to hardware
There are no privileged instructions in modern CPUs
explanation: Side-channel attacks such as Spectre and Meltdown exploit speculative execution to leak kernel memory data to user-mode processes, showing that hardware privilege separation alone is not sufficient for security.

text: What is the multikernel approach to OS design for many-core processors?
options: It treats the machine as a network of independent cores communicating via messages
It runs a single monolithic kernel replicated on each core
It assigns one core to the kernel and all others to user processes
It uses virtual machines to isolate each core
explanation: The multikernel approach, exemplified by the Barrelfish OS, treats a many-core machine as a distributed system where each core runs its own OS instance and cores communicate via explicit message passing rather than shared memory.

text: What is a container and how does it differ from a virtual machine?
options: A container shares the host OS kernel while a VM runs its own full OS
A container requires a hypervisor while a VM does not
A container provides stronger isolation than a VM
A container can only run Linux while a VM supports any OS
explanation: Containers share the host operating system kernel and isolate processes using namespaces and cgroups, while VMs each run a complete guest OS on a hypervisor, making containers lighter weight but providing weaker isolation.

text: What is binary translation in the context of full virtualization?
options: The hypervisor dynamically rewrites guest privileged instructions at runtime
The compiler converts source code to a different CPU architecture
The OS translates binary files between file formats
The CPU converts binary to decimal during arithmetic operations
explanation: Binary translation is a technique where the hypervisor scans and rewrites sensitive or privileged instructions in the guest OS code at runtime, replacing them with safe equivalents, enabling full virtualization on CPUs without hardware virtualization support.

text: What design challenge does heterogeneous multiprocessing (big.LITTLE) introduce for operating systems?
options: The scheduler must match task requirements to cores with different performance and power profiles
All cores must run at the same clock speed
The OS must use a separate kernel for each core type
Applications must be compiled separately for each core type
explanation: In heterogeneous multiprocessing architectures like ARM big.LITTLE, the OS scheduler must intelligently assign tasks to high-performance or energy-efficient cores based on workload characteristics, adding scheduling complexity.

text: What is the principle behind a library OS in the context of cloud computing?
options: Application and OS are compiled together into a single-purpose image for minimal overhead
The OS loads libraries dynamically from a central cloud repository
All OS functions are implemented as shared libraries on a network
Applications use library calls instead of system calls for I/O
explanation: A library OS bundles only the needed OS functionality directly with the application into a single-purpose image (unikernel), eliminating unnecessary OS services and reducing attack surface and overhead in cloud deployments.

text: What is the primary purpose of hardware-assisted virtualization extensions like Intel VT-x?
options: To provide CPU hardware support for efficiently running virtual machines
To increase the clock speed of the CPU
To add more physical cores to the processor
To enable GPUs to execute operating system code
explanation: Hardware-assisted virtualization extensions like Intel VT-x and AMD-V add new CPU modes and instructions that allow hypervisors to run guest operating systems more efficiently by handling privilege transitions in hardware rather than through costly software emulation.

text: What is a system call used for in an operating system?
options: Requesting services from the OS kernel
Directly accessing hardware without the OS
Compiling programs into machine code
Formatting a hard disk drive
explanation: A system call is the mechanism by which user programs request services from the operating system kernel, such as reading a file or creating a process.

text: What is a shell in an operating system?
options: A command interpreter that accepts and executes user commands
The innermost part of the operating system kernel
A hardware component on the motherboard
A type of system memory
explanation: A shell is a command interpreter that provides an interface between the user and the operating system, accepting text commands and executing them.

text: What type of system call is 'read()' in Unix?
options: File management system call
Process control system call
Device management system call
Communication system call
explanation: The read() system call is a file management system call used to read data from a file or file descriptor.

text: In a layered OS design, which layer interacts directly with the hardware?
options: The lowest layer (Layer 0)
The highest layer (user interface)
The middle layer
All layers equally
explanation: In a layered OS design, the lowest layer (Layer 0) directly interacts with the hardware, and each higher layer builds upon the services of the layer below it.

text: What is a device management system call used for?
options: Requesting and releasing hardware devices
Creating new files on disk
Terminating running processes
Sending messages between computers
explanation: Device management system calls are used to request, release, read from, and write to hardware devices managed by the operating system.

text: What is the purpose of a boot loader?
options: To load the operating system kernel into memory during startup
To install new application software
To manage user login credentials
To defragment the hard disk
explanation: A boot loader is a program stored in firmware or the boot sector that loads the operating system kernel into main memory during the computer startup process.

text: Which of the following is a process control system call?
options: fork()
open()
read()
chmod()
explanation: The fork() system call is a process control system call that creates a new child process by duplicating the calling parent process.

text: What is an operating system kernel?
options: The core component of the OS that is always resident in memory
A user-level application for managing files
An external hardware device controller
A network communication protocol
explanation: The kernel is the central and most fundamental component of an operating system that manages system resources and is always loaded in memory.

text: What is a system utility program?
options: A program that provides common system functions like file management
The core kernel of the operating system
A hardware diagnostic chip on the motherboard
An application for browsing the internet
explanation: System utility programs provide convenient functions for system administration tasks such as file management, disk operations, and system monitoring.

text: What does the 'write()' system call do?
options: Writes data to a file or output device
Creates a new directory
Deletes a file from disk
Changes file permissions
explanation: The write() system call writes data from a buffer in the process's address space to a file, device, or other output destination.

text: What is the main trade-off between monolithic and microkernel architectures?
options: Monolithic has better performance but less modularity than microkernel
Monolithic uses less memory than microkernel in all cases
Microkernel is always faster because it has a smaller kernel
Monolithic supports more hardware devices than microkernel
explanation: Monolithic kernels have better performance due to fewer context switches and no IPC overhead, but microkernels offer better modularity, reliability, and security by running services in user space.

text: How are parameters passed to system calls via registers?
options: Parameter values are placed directly in CPU registers before the system call
Parameters are written to a file that the kernel reads
Parameters are encoded in the system call number itself
Parameters are passed through network sockets
explanation: One method of passing parameters to system calls is placing the values directly in CPU registers, which is fast but limited by the number of available registers.

text: What is the key benefit of loadable kernel modules over a static monolithic kernel?
options: New functionality can be added at runtime without recompiling the kernel
They eliminate the need for device drivers entirely
They make the kernel run in user space
They remove the need for system calls
explanation: Loadable kernel modules allow new functionality such as device drivers and file systems to be dynamically loaded into the kernel at runtime without needing to recompile or reboot.

text: What is the run-time environment (RTE) in the context of system calls?
options: A software layer that intercepts API calls and invokes the correct system calls
A special hardware unit that executes system calls
A debugging tool for testing operating system code
A network protocol for remote procedure calls
explanation: The run-time environment provides a system call interface that intercepts function calls made by application programs through the API and invokes the corresponding system calls in the kernel.

text: Why is inter-process communication slower in microkernel systems?
options: Services communicate via message passing through the kernel rather than direct calls
The microkernel uses slower processors
User space runs at reduced clock speed
Microkernel systems cannot use DMA for I/O
explanation: In microkernel systems, services running in user space must communicate through message passing via the kernel, requiring additional context switches and data copying compared to direct function calls in a monolithic kernel.

text: What is the purpose of the /proc file system in Linux?
options: It provides a virtual file interface to kernel data structures and process info
It stores user documents and personal files
It contains compiled application programs
It holds backup copies of the boot loader
explanation: The /proc file system in Linux is a virtual filesystem that exposes kernel data structures, process information, and system configuration as files, allowing user-space programs to read and modify kernel parameters.

text: What is the difference between a policy and a mechanism in OS design?
options: Mechanism defines how to do something, policy decides what should be done
Mechanism is software while policy is hardware
Policy runs in kernel mode while mechanism runs in user mode
There is no practical difference between the two
explanation: Mechanisms determine how to perform an operation (e.g., a timer interrupt), while policies decide what action to take (e.g., how long each process gets the CPU). Separating them allows flexible OS configuration.

text: How does the stack-based method of passing system call parameters work?
options: Parameters are pushed onto the program stack and the kernel pops them off
Parameters are stored in a special CPU cache
Parameters are passed as part of the instruction opcode
Parameters are sent via interrupt signals
explanation: In the stack-based method, the user program pushes system call parameters onto the stack before invoking the system call, and the kernel retrieves them by reading from the stack.

text: What are information maintenance system calls used for?
options: Transferring information between the user program and the OS
Creating new user accounts
Formatting storage devices
Establishing network connections
explanation: Information maintenance system calls transfer information between user programs and the OS, such as getting the current time, retrieving system data, or setting system attributes.

text: What advantage does a hybrid kernel offer over a pure microkernel?
options: It keeps performance-critical services in kernel space while maintaining some modularity
It completely eliminates context switches
It runs all services in user space for maximum reliability
It requires no device drivers
explanation: A hybrid kernel keeps performance-critical services like the file system and networking in kernel space for speed while running less critical services in user space, balancing performance and modularity.

text: How does the Darwin kernel used in macOS combine different architectural approaches?
options: It uses a Mach microkernel core with BSD monolithic services in the same address space
It runs entirely as a microkernel with all services in user space
It uses a pure monolithic design identical to Linux
It relies on a hypervisor to separate kernel components
explanation: Darwin combines the Mach microkernel (providing IPC, memory management, and threading) with BSD subsystem services running in the same kernel address space, creating a hybrid architecture that balances modularity and performance.

text: What is the difference between a system call and a library call in terms of execution?
options: A system call transitions to kernel mode while a library call stays in user mode
A library call is always slower than a system call
System calls cannot return values to the caller
Library calls bypass the operating system entirely in all cases
explanation: A system call requires a mode switch from user mode to kernel mode via a trap instruction, incurring overhead, while a library call executes entirely in user mode unless it internally invokes a system call.

text: What is the vDSO mechanism in Linux and why is it used?
options: It maps kernel code into user space to avoid system call overhead for certain functions
It provides virtual device drivers for emulated hardware
It creates virtual disk storage from RAM
It is a debugging tool for tracing system calls
explanation: The virtual Dynamic Shared Object (vDSO) is a small shared library mapped by the kernel into every process's address space, allowing certain frequently used system calls like gettimeofday() to execute in user space without the overhead of a mode switch.

text: What is the challenge of maintaining ABI compatibility in operating system development?
options: Changes to system call interfaces or data structures can break existing compiled applications
ABI compatibility only affects interpreted languages
Maintaining ABI compatibility slows down hardware performance
ABI compatibility is automatically handled by the CPU
explanation: Application Binary Interface (ABI) compatibility requires that changes to the OS do not alter the binary-level interface (system call numbers, structure layouts, calling conventions), as breaking the ABI would require recompiling all existing applications.

text: How do system calls work on x86-64 Linux using the SYSCALL instruction?
options: The syscall number is placed in RAX and SYSCALL transfers control to the kernel entry point
The CPU automatically determines the system call from the program context
System calls are made by writing to a special memory address
The SYSCALL instruction is only available in kernel mode
explanation: On x86-64 Linux, the system call number is placed in the RAX register, arguments in specific registers (RDI, RSI, RDX, etc.), and the SYSCALL instruction performs a fast transition to kernel mode at a predetermined entry point.

text: What is the seccomp mechanism in Linux kernel architecture?
options: A security facility that restricts the system calls a process can make
A tool for compressing the kernel image during boot
A scheduler for secondary storage devices
A method for secure communication between microkernels
explanation: Seccomp (secure computing mode) is a Linux kernel facility that restricts the set of system calls a process can invoke, reducing the kernel attack surface by limiting what sandboxed processes can do.

text: What problem does the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL2) solve architecturally?
options: It runs a real Linux kernel in a lightweight VM to provide native Linux binary compatibility
It translates Linux source code to Windows executables at compile time
It emulates x86 Linux on ARM Windows devices
It replaces the Windows kernel with the Linux kernel
explanation: WSL2 runs a real Linux kernel inside a lightweight Hyper-V virtual machine, providing full Linux system call compatibility without the translation layer limitations of WSL1, while integrating closely with the Windows host.

text: What is the role of the init system (e.g., systemd) in modern Linux architecture?
options: It is the first user-space process that initializes services and manages the system lifecycle
It is part of the kernel that handles hardware interrupts
It compiles the kernel from source code during boot
It manages the BIOS firmware settings
explanation: The init system (PID 1) is the first user-space process started by the kernel. Modern implementations like systemd manage service startup, dependencies, logging, and the overall system lifecycle.

text: What is the difference between a stable and unstable ABI in kernel module development?
options: A stable ABI guarantees compatibility across versions while unstable ABI may change at any time
Stable ABI is faster while unstable ABI is more secure
Stable ABI runs in user mode while unstable ABI runs in kernel mode
There is no practical difference in modern operating systems
explanation: A stable ABI guarantees that kernel modules compiled for one kernel version will work on future versions without recompilation. Linux intentionally has an unstable in-kernel ABI, requiring modules to be recompiled for each kernel version to allow rapid kernel evolution.

text: How does the Fuchsia OS Zircon kernel differ from traditional Unix-like kernels?
options: It uses a capability-based object system instead of Unix file descriptors for resource access
It is a monolithic kernel identical to Linux in design
It does not support user-space applications
It runs exclusively on x86 architecture
explanation: Zircon uses a capability-based security model where access to kernel objects is mediated by handles with specific rights, unlike Unix which uses file descriptors and permission bits. This provides finer-grained access control.

text: What is a process state in operating systems?
options: The current activity or condition of a process during its lifecycle
The physical location of the process on disk
The programming language used to write the process
The amount of memory installed in the system
explanation: A process state describes the current activity of a process, such as new, ready, running, waiting, or terminated, during its lifecycle.

text: What happens when a running process requests an I/O operation?
options: It moves from the running state to the waiting state
It moves from the running state to the terminated state
It continues executing without any state change
It is permanently removed from memory
explanation: When a running process requests an I/O operation, it cannot continue executing until the I/O completes, so it transitions to the waiting (blocked) state.

text: What information does the process ID (PID) provide?
options: A unique numerical identifier assigned to each process by the OS
The memory address where the process is stored
The priority level of the process
The name of the user who started the process
explanation: A process ID (PID) is a unique numerical identifier assigned by the operating system to each process, used to distinguish and manage processes.

text: What is a child process?
options: A process created by another process using a system call like fork()
A process that has the highest priority in the system
A process that manages hardware devices
A process that is waiting for user input
explanation: A child process is a new process created by an existing process (the parent) using a system call like fork() in Unix/Linux systems.

text: What is the 'new' state of a process?
options: The process is being created and has not yet been admitted to the ready queue
The process is currently executing on the CPU
The process is waiting for an I/O event to complete
The process has finished execution
explanation: The 'new' state indicates that a process is being created. It has not yet been admitted by the OS to the pool of ready processes.

text: What is the 'terminated' state of a process?
options: The process has finished execution and is being cleaned up by the OS
The process is actively running on the CPU
The process is waiting in the ready queue
The process is blocked on an I/O request
explanation: The terminated state means the process has finished execution. The OS deallocates its resources and removes its entry from the process table.

text: What does the wait() system call do in Unix?
options: Causes the parent process to wait until a child process terminates
Pauses the CPU clock for a specified duration
Waits for user keyboard input
Delays the boot sequence of the operating system
explanation: The wait() system call causes the calling parent process to block until one of its child processes terminates, allowing the parent to collect the child's exit status.

text: What is a process table in an operating system?
options: A data structure that stores information about all active processes
A physical table of hardware components in the computer
A list of all installed software applications
A directory of files stored on the hard disk
