explanation: The process table is a kernel data structure that maintains a list of all currently active processes and their associated Process Control Blocks (PCBs).

text: What is the ready state of a process?
options: The process is loaded in memory and waiting for CPU allocation
The process is currently executing instructions
The process is waiting for an I/O device to respond
The process has not yet been created
explanation: A process in the ready state is loaded in main memory and is waiting to be assigned to a processor for execution.

text: What does the exit() system call do?
options: Terminates the calling process and returns a status to the parent
Restarts the operating system
Closes all open files on the system
Logs the user out of the system
explanation: The exit() system call terminates the calling process, releases its resources, and returns an exit status code to the parent process.

text: What is the difference between a process and a program?
options: A process is an active instance of a program in execution with its own state
A program runs on the CPU while a process is stored on disk
A process is always larger in size than a program
There is no difference between a process and a program
explanation: A program is a passive entity stored on disk (executable file), while a process is an active entity that includes the program code, current activity (program counter, registers), stack, and allocated resources.

text: What is the purpose of the process address space?
options: It defines the memory layout including text, data, heap, and stack sections
It specifies the physical location of the process on the hard disk
It determines the network address used by the process
It sets the display resolution for the process window
explanation: The process address space defines the logical memory layout of a process, containing the text (code) section, data section, heap (dynamically allocated memory), and stack (function calls and local variables).

text: How does message passing IPC differ from shared memory IPC in terms of synchronization?
options: Message passing provides implicit synchronization while shared memory requires explicit synchronization
Shared memory is always faster for large data transfers
Message passing cannot be used between processes on the same machine
Shared memory does not require any OS support
explanation: Message passing provides implicit synchronization because send/receive operations naturally coordinate processes, while shared memory requires explicit synchronization mechanisms like semaphores to prevent race conditions.

text: What is a message queue as an IPC mechanism?
options: A linked list of messages stored in the kernel that processes can read and write
A hardware buffer on the network card
A queue of CPU instructions waiting to execute
A list of print jobs waiting for the printer
explanation: A message queue is a kernel-maintained linked list of messages that allows processes to exchange data by writing messages to and reading messages from the queue, even if the processes are not running simultaneously.

text: What is the difference between direct and indirect inter-process communication?
options: Direct IPC names the recipient process explicitly while indirect uses shared mailboxes
Direct IPC is faster because it skips the kernel
Indirect IPC can only be used in distributed systems
Direct IPC requires shared memory while indirect does not
explanation: In direct IPC, the sending process explicitly names the receiving process. In indirect IPC, messages are sent to and received from shared mailboxes (ports), allowing more flexible communication patterns.

text: What information is saved during a context switch?
options: The CPU registers, program counter, and process state of the outgoing process
Only the process name and user ID
The contents of the hard disk
The screen display buffer
explanation: During a context switch, the OS saves the complete CPU state of the outgoing process (registers, program counter, stack pointer, process state) into its PCB and loads the saved state of the incoming process.

text: What is the role of the job queue in process scheduling?
options: It contains all processes in the system regardless of their state
It only holds processes currently executing on the CPU
It stores terminated processes waiting for cleanup
It manages disk I/O requests
explanation: The job queue contains all processes in the system. As processes enter the system, they are placed in the job queue, from which the long-term scheduler selects processes to load into memory.

text: How does the Unix kill() system call work?
options: It sends a specified signal to a process identified by its PID
It immediately destroys the process without any cleanup
It only works on child processes of the calling process
It deletes the executable file of the target process
explanation: The kill() system call sends a specified signal (not necessarily a termination signal) to a process or process group identified by PID, allowing inter-process signaling and process management.

text: What is the difference between I/O-bound and CPU-bound processes?
options: I/O-bound processes spend more time on I/O operations while CPU-bound spend more time computing
I/O-bound processes are faster than CPU-bound processes
CPU-bound processes use more memory than I/O-bound processes
There is no meaningful difference between them
explanation: I/O-bound processes spend most of their time performing I/O operations with short CPU bursts, while CPU-bound processes spend most of their time computing with infrequent I/O requests.

text: What is swapping in the context of process management?
options: Moving a process between main memory and a backing store to free memory
Exchanging data between two running processes
Switching the CPU between user mode and kernel mode
Replacing one operating system with another
explanation: Swapping is the process of temporarily moving an entire process from main memory to a backing store (usually disk) and later bringing it back for continued execution, allowing the OS to manage limited memory.

text: What is the double fork technique in Unix and what problem does it solve?
options: Forking twice makes the grandchild an orphan adopted by init to avoid zombies
It creates two identical copies of a process for load balancing
It forks a process on two different CPUs simultaneously
It creates a backup copy of the process for fault tolerance
explanation: The double fork technique involves forking twice and terminating the intermediate child, making the grandchild an orphan that is adopted by the init process. This avoids zombie processes because the parent does not need to wait for the grandchild.

text: How does the Linux cgroup mechanism control process resource usage?
options: It groups processes and enforces limits on CPU, memory, and I/O usage per group
It encrypts process data for security
It compresses process memory to save space
It pins processes to specific CPU cores only
explanation: Control groups (cgroups) in Linux organize processes into hierarchical groups and enforce resource limits (CPU time, memory, I/O bandwidth, etc.) on each group, forming a foundation for container technologies.

text: What is the difference between posix_spawn() and fork()+exec() for process creation?
options: posix_spawn creates a process in a single call avoiding the overhead of copying the parent's address space
posix_spawn only works on Windows systems
fork()+exec() is always faster than posix_spawn
posix_spawn cannot set up file descriptors for the child process
explanation: posix_spawn() combines process creation and program execution in a single call, avoiding the overhead of fork() copying the parent's entire address space only to immediately replace it with exec(), making it more efficient especially on systems without copy-on-write.

text: What are Linux namespaces and how do they relate to process isolation?
options: They isolate system resources so processes see their own independent view of PIDs, network, and mounts
They provide a naming convention for files on disk
They organize kernel source code into modules
They assign human-readable names to IP addresses
explanation: Linux namespaces isolate different aspects of system resources (PID, network, mount, user, IPC, UTS) so that processes within a namespace have their own independent view, forming the basis of container isolation in technologies like Docker.

text: What is the thundering herd problem in process management?
options: Multiple waiting processes are all woken up for an event but only one can handle it, wasting resources
Too many processes run simultaneously causing CPU overheating
Processes consume too much memory during startup
Network packets arrive faster than processes can handle them
explanation: The thundering herd problem occurs when many processes waiting on the same event are all woken up simultaneously, but only one can successfully proceed, causing unnecessary context switches and wasted CPU cycles for the others.

text: How does the Windows process creation model differ from the Unix fork/exec model?
options: Windows uses CreateProcess() which creates a new process from scratch with a specified executable
Windows uses fork() identically to Unix
Windows cannot create child processes
Windows processes do not have process IDs
explanation: Windows uses CreateProcess() which creates a new process and loads the specified executable in a single operation, unlike Unix where fork() duplicates the parent and exec() replaces the image, making the Windows model conceptually simpler but less flexible.

text: What is process affinity in the context of NUMA architectures?
options: Binding processes to specific NUMA nodes to keep memory accesses local and reduce latency
Measuring how much a process likes its assigned CPU
Preventing processes from accessing network resources
Grouping processes by the user who created them
explanation: In NUMA architectures, process affinity binds processes to specific processors or NUMA nodes, ensuring that memory accesses stay local to the node where the process's memory is allocated, reducing access latency.

text: What is the OOM (Out-of-Memory) killer in Linux?
options: A kernel mechanism that selects and terminates processes when the system runs out of memory
A tool that prevents users from allocating too much disk space
A hardware circuit that shuts down when RAM overheats
A security feature that kills processes attempting buffer overflows
explanation: The Linux OOM killer is a kernel mechanism that is invoked when the system runs critically low on memory. It selects processes to terminate based on a scoring algorithm to free memory and keep the system running.

text: What is a process descriptor in the Linux kernel and what does it contain?
options: A task_struct that holds all information the kernel needs about a process
A text description of what the process does
A file containing the process source code
A log of all commands the process has executed
explanation: In Linux, each process is represented by a task_struct (process descriptor), a large data structure containing the process state, scheduling information, memory management data, open files, signal handlers, and other kernel-level process metadata.

text: What is the seccomp-bpf mechanism used for in process sandboxing?
options: It filters system calls using BPF programs to restrict what a process can do
It encrypts process memory using Berkeley Packet Filters
It monitors network packets sent by a process
It compresses process data to reduce memory usage
explanation: Seccomp-BPF allows attaching BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter) programs that filter system calls made by a process, enabling fine-grained sandboxing where each system call can be allowed, denied, or logged based on its arguments.

text: What is the main goal of CPU scheduling?
options: To maximize CPU utilization and ensure fair process execution
To increase the physical clock speed of the CPU
To add more cores to the processor
To reduce the amount of installed RAM
explanation: The main goal of CPU scheduling is to maximize CPU utilization by keeping the CPU busy and ensuring fair allocation of CPU time among competing processes.

text: What is a scheduling algorithm in operating systems?
options: A method that determines which process gets the CPU next
A formula for calculating memory addresses
A technique for compressing files on disk
A procedure for installing device drivers
explanation: A scheduling algorithm is a method used by the OS scheduler to determine the order in which ready processes are allocated CPU time for execution.

text: In Round Robin scheduling, what happens when a process's time quantum expires?
options: The process is preempted and moved to the end of the ready queue
The process is terminated immediately
The process continues running until it finishes
The process is moved to a higher priority queue
explanation: In Round Robin scheduling, when a process's time quantum expires, it is preempted by the CPU scheduler and placed at the end of the ready queue, giving other processes a turn.

text: What is a non-preemptive scheduling algorithm?
options: An algorithm where a running process keeps the CPU until it voluntarily releases it
An algorithm that can interrupt any process at any time
An algorithm that runs all processes simultaneously
An algorithm used only for real-time systems
explanation: In non-preemptive scheduling, once a process is allocated the CPU, it keeps it until it voluntarily releases it by terminating or switching to a waiting state.

text: What does response time measure in CPU scheduling?
options: The time from submission of a request until the first response is produced
The total time a process takes to complete execution
The time a process spends waiting in the ready queue
The time the CPU is idle between processes
explanation: Response time measures the elapsed time from when a request is submitted until the first response is produced, which is important for interactive systems.

text: Which scheduling algorithm is the simplest to implement?
options: First-Come, First-Served (FCFS)
Multilevel Feedback Queue
Completely Fair Scheduler
Rate Monotonic Scheduling
explanation: FCFS is the simplest scheduling algorithm, using a basic FIFO queue where processes are executed in the order they arrive.

text: What is CPU burst in the context of process execution?
options: A period during which a process is actively using the CPU for computation
The maximum speed at which the CPU can operate
A hardware failure that causes the CPU to overheat
The time it takes to boot the CPU
explanation: A CPU burst is a period during which a process is actively executing instructions on the CPU, typically alternating with I/O bursts in a process's execution cycle.

text: In priority scheduling, what happens when two processes have the same priority?
options: They are typically scheduled using FCFS among equal-priority processes
The process with the larger PID always runs first
Both processes run simultaneously on the same core
One process is terminated to resolve the conflict
explanation: When two processes have the same priority in priority scheduling, a secondary criterion such as FCFS (first-come, first-served) is typically used to break the tie.

text: What is an I/O burst in process execution?
options: A period during which a process waits for an I/O operation to complete
A spike in CPU processing speed
A burst of data transmitted over the network
A sudden increase in available memory
explanation: An I/O burst is a period during which a process is waiting for an I/O operation (such as disk read or network receive) to complete, during which it does not use the CPU.

text: What does the dispatcher module do in CPU scheduling?
options: Gives control of the CPU to the process selected by the scheduler
Selects which process should run next from the ready queue
Calculates the priority of each process
Manages memory allocation for processes
explanation: The dispatcher is the module that gives control of the CPU to the process selected by the short-term scheduler, performing context switching, mode switching, and jumping to the proper location in the program.

text: Why does Shortest Job First (SJF) scheduling produce the minimum average waiting time?
options: Shorter jobs finish quickly, reducing the waiting time for all subsequent jobs
It uses the fastest CPU clock speed for short jobs
It skips I/O operations to save time
It runs all processes simultaneously
explanation: SJF minimizes average waiting time because executing shorter jobs first reduces the cumulative waiting time for all jobs in the queue, which is mathematically provable as optimal for this metric.

text: What is the problem of starvation in priority scheduling?
options: Low-priority processes may never execute because higher-priority processes keep arriving
All processes starve for memory simultaneously
The CPU runs out of power during execution
Processes consume too much disk space
explanation: Starvation occurs in priority scheduling when low-priority processes wait indefinitely because a continuous stream of higher-priority processes keeps preempting them from the CPU.

text: How does the aging technique prevent starvation?
options: It gradually increases the priority of waiting processes over time
It reduces the priority of all running processes
It terminates processes that have waited too long
It allocates more memory to starving processes
explanation: Aging prevents starvation by gradually increasing the priority of processes that have been waiting in the ready queue for a long time, ensuring they eventually get scheduled.

text: What is the effect of time quantum size on Round Robin performance?
options: Too large behaves like FCFS; too small causes excessive context switching overhead
Larger quanta always improve performance
Smaller quanta always improve response time without any downside
Time quantum size has no effect on system performance
explanation: If the time quantum is too large, Round Robin degenerates into FCFS. If too small, the overhead of frequent context switches dominates, reducing useful CPU time. An optimal quantum balances responsiveness and overhead.

text: In multilevel queue scheduling, how are processes assigned to queues?
options: Based on process characteristics such as type, priority, or memory requirements
Randomly distributed among available queues
All placed in a single queue sorted by arrival time
Assigned based on the day of the week they were created
explanation: In multilevel queue scheduling, processes are permanently assigned to specific queues based on properties like process type (system, interactive, batch), priority level, or memory size.

text: What advantage does multilevel feedback queue scheduling have over multilevel queue?
options: Processes can move between queues based on their behavior allowing dynamic adaptation
It uses fewer CPU resources for scheduling decisions
It eliminates the need for context switching
It guarantees that all processes finish in the same amount of time
explanation: Multilevel feedback queue scheduling allows processes to move between queues based on their CPU burst behavior. CPU-bound processes move to lower-priority queues, while I/O-bound processes stay in higher-priority queues, adapting dynamically.

text: What is the difference between preemptive SJF and non-preemptive SJF?
options: Preemptive SJF interrupts a running process if a shorter one arrives; non-preemptive does not
Non-preemptive SJF is always faster than preemptive SJF
Preemptive SJF does not require knowing burst times
Non-preemptive SJF causes more context switches
explanation: Preemptive SJF (also called SRTF) interrupts the currently running process if a newly arrived process has a shorter remaining burst time, while non-preemptive SJF lets the running process complete its burst before choosing the shortest job.

text: What is the role of the medium-term scheduler in CPU scheduling?
options: It swaps processes in and out of memory to control the degree of multiprogramming
It selects which process runs on the CPU next
It decides which jobs enter the system from the job pool
It manages disk I/O scheduling
explanation: The medium-term scheduler temporarily removes processes from memory (swapping) and reintroduces them later, controlling the degree of multiprogramming and improving the process mix.

text: Why is predicting the next CPU burst length important for SJF scheduling?
options: SJF requires knowing burst lengths to select the shortest job but future bursts are unknown
Burst length determines how much memory a process needs
Longer bursts indicate higher priority processes
Burst prediction is only needed for real-time systems
explanation: SJF needs to know the next CPU burst length of each process to schedule the shortest one, but since future burst lengths are unknown, they must be predicted using techniques like exponential averaging of past bursts.

text: What is the difference between throughput and turnaround time as scheduling metrics?
options: Throughput measures processes completed per time unit while turnaround time measures total time for one process
Throughput is always larger than turnaround time
They measure the same thing using different units
Turnaround time applies only to I/O-bound processes
explanation: Throughput is the number of processes that complete execution per unit time, while turnaround time is the total time taken for a single process from submission to completion, including waiting and execution time.

text: How does the Linux CFS use a red-black tree for scheduling decisions?
options: Processes are ordered by virtual runtime in the tree, and the leftmost node with smallest vruntime runs next
The tree stores processes sorted by PID and the root node runs next
Processes are randomly inserted and the tree auto-balances priorities
The tree is used only for I/O scheduling, not CPU scheduling
explanation: CFS maintains a red-black tree ordered by each process's virtual runtime (vruntime). The process with the smallest vruntime (leftmost node) is selected next, and insertion/removal operations are O(log n), ensuring efficient fair scheduling.

text: What is the utilization bound test for rate-monotonic scheduling?
options: The set of tasks is schedulable if total utilization does not exceed n(2^(1/n) - 1)
Tasks are schedulable if each task's period is greater than its execution time
The test checks if total memory usage is below the physical memory limit
Tasks are schedulable only if they all have the same period
explanation: The Liu and Layland utilization bound test states that a set of n periodic tasks is guaranteed to be schedulable under rate-monotonic scheduling if the total CPU utilization does not exceed n(2^(1/n) - 1), which approaches ln(2) ≈ 0.693 as n approaches infinity.

text: What is the priority inversion problem and how did it affect the Mars Pathfinder?
options: A high-priority task was blocked by a low-priority task holding a shared resource, causing system resets
The rover's CPU overheated due to excessive computation
A software bug caused the scheduler to run processes in reverse priority order
Network communication delays caused missed commands from Earth
explanation: On Mars Pathfinder, a high-priority task was blocked waiting for a mutex held by a low-priority task, while a medium-priority task preempted the low-priority task, preventing it from releasing the mutex. This caused system resets until priority inheritance was enabled.

text: What is the difference between global scheduling and partitioned scheduling on multicore systems?
options: Global scheduling uses a single queue for all cores while partitioned assigns each task to a fixed core
Global scheduling is always worse than partitioned scheduling
Partitioned scheduling does not support priority-based algorithms
Global scheduling can only be used with two cores
explanation: Global scheduling maintains a single ready queue from which any core can pick the next task, allowing better load balancing but causing migration overhead. Partitioned scheduling assigns each task to a specific core, eliminating migration but potentially causing load imbalance.

text: What is the BFS (Brain F*** Scheduler) design philosophy compared to CFS?
options: BFS uses a single global run queue with an earliest eligible virtual deadline algorithm for simplicity
BFS uses per-CPU run queues with work stealing for scalability
BFS is designed only for real-time tasks on embedded systems
BFS uses a multilevel feedback queue identical to traditional Unix schedulers
explanation: BFS, designed by Con Kolivas, uses a single global run queue and an earliest eligible virtual deadline first (EEVDF) approach, prioritizing simplicity, low latency, and desktop responsiveness over the scalability focus of CFS's per-CPU run queues.

text: How does Earliest Deadline First (EDF) differ from rate-monotonic scheduling for real-time tasks?
options: EDF assigns dynamic priorities based on deadlines achieving 100% utilization while rate-monotonic uses fixed priorities
EDF only works with aperiodic tasks while rate-monotonic handles periodic tasks
Rate-monotonic achieves higher utilization bounds than EDF
EDF requires hardware support that rate-monotonic does not
explanation: EDF assigns dynamic priorities based on absolute deadlines (nearest deadline gets highest priority) and can achieve 100% CPU utilization. Rate-monotonic assigns fixed priorities based on period length and has a lower utilization bound of about 69%.

text: What is work stealing in multiprocessor scheduling?
options: Idle processors take tasks from the run queues of busy processors to balance load
Processes steal CPU cycles from higher-priority processes
The scheduler removes tasks from slow processors and discards them
Memory is reallocated from one core's cache to another
explanation: Work stealing is a load balancing technique where idle processors steal tasks from the run queues of busy processors, dynamically distributing work across all available cores to maximize utilization.

text: What is the SCHED_DEADLINE scheduling class in Linux?
options: An EDF-based scheduler that guarantees CPU bandwidth to tasks using runtime/deadline/period parameters
A scheduler that assigns deadlines based on process creation time
A file system scheduling class for managing disk deadlines
A network scheduler for prioritizing packet delivery
explanation: SCHED_DEADLINE is a Linux scheduling class implementing earliest deadline first (EDF) with constant bandwidth server (CBS), where each task specifies its runtime, deadline, and period, guaranteeing CPU bandwidth reservation.

text: What is the problem with using lottery scheduling in practice?
options: Unpredictable short-term behavior due to randomness even though long-term fairness is achieved
It requires specialized hardware random number generators
It cannot handle more than ten processes simultaneously
It always gives equal time to all processes regardless of priority
explanation: Lottery scheduling uses randomized ticket draws to allocate CPU time, providing probabilistic fairness proportional to ticket counts. However, short-term behavior can be unpredictable due to the randomness, and achieving precise proportional sharing requires many scheduling decisions.

text: How does the Energy Aware Scheduler (EAS) in Linux optimize for mobile devices?
options: It uses CPU energy models to place tasks on the most energy-efficient core that meets performance needs
It shuts down the CPU when battery is below 50%
It reduces screen brightness to save power during computation
It limits the number of processes to reduce energy consumption
explanation: EAS integrates energy models of the platform's CPUs into scheduling decisions, placing tasks on the most energy-efficient processor that can meet their performance requirements, optimizing battery life on heterogeneous mobile SoCs like ARM big.LITTLE.

text: Which component is NOT shared between threads of the same process?
options: Global variables
Stack
Code section
Open file descriptors
explanation: Each thread has its own stack for local variables and function call management. Code section, global variables, and open file descriptors are shared among all threads in a process.

text: What is the primary reason thread creation is faster than process creation?
options: Threads use faster CPUs
Threads share the address space and resources of their parent process
Threads do not need an operating system
Threads are stored on the hard disk
explanation: Thread creation is faster because threads share the address space, code, data, and OS resources of their parent process, avoiding the overhead of duplicating these resources as required in process creation.

text: Which of the following best describes a single-threaded process?
options: A process that can run on multiple CPUs simultaneously
A process with exactly one thread of execution
A process that cannot perform I/O operations
A process that runs only in kernel mode
explanation: A single-threaded process has exactly one thread of execution, meaning it can only perform one task at a time within that process.

text: What is the role of a thread library?
options: To manage hardware interrupts
To provide an API for creating and managing threads
To compile multithreaded programs
To allocate disk space for threads
explanation: A thread library provides programmers with an API for creating, managing, and synchronizing threads. Examples include POSIX Pthreads, Windows threads, and Java threads.

text: Context switching between threads of the same process is generally faster than between processes because:
options: Threads have smaller program counters
No need to switch the memory address space
Threads do not use the CPU
The operating system does not track threads
explanation: Thread context switching is faster because threads within the same process share the same address space, so there is no need to flush and reload the TLB or switch page tables.

text: Which threading model maps many user-level threads to many kernel threads?
options: One-to-one model
Many-to-one model
Many-to-many model
One-to-many model
explanation: The many-to-many model multiplexes many user-level threads to an equal or smaller number of kernel threads, allowing the OS to create a sufficient number of kernel threads.

text: In Java, which interface must a class implement to define a task that can be executed by a thread?
options: Serializable
Runnable
Callable only
Throwable
explanation: In Java, a class implements the Runnable interface and defines the run() method to specify the code that constitutes a new thread's task.

text: What does the term 'multicore programming' refer to?
options: Programming with multiple monitors
Writing programs that can utilize multiple processing cores
Running multiple operating systems simultaneously
Using multiple keyboards for input
explanation: Multicore programming refers to designing and writing software that can take advantage of multiple processing cores to execute tasks concurrently or in parallel.

text: What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism in threading?
options: They are identical concepts
Concurrency means tasks make progress over time; parallelism means tasks run simultaneously
Parallelism requires only one CPU core
Concurrency is only possible with GPUs
explanation: Concurrency involves managing multiple tasks that make progress over time (possibly through interleaving on a single core), while parallelism involves actually executing multiple tasks simultaneously on multiple cores.

text: Which register is unique to each thread and tracks the next instruction to execute?
options: Base register
Program counter
Memory address register
I/O register
explanation: Each thread has its own program counter (PC) that keeps track of the next instruction the thread should execute, allowing threads to execute different parts of the code independently.

text: What challenge does Amdahl's Law highlight for multicore systems?
options: Adding more memory always improves performance linearly
The serial portion of a program limits the maximum speedup achievable through parallelism
All programs can be fully parallelized
Multicore systems are slower than single-core systems
explanation: Amdahl's Law states that the speedup of a program from parallelization is limited by the fraction of the program that must execute serially. Even with infinite cores, the serial portion creates an upper bound on speedup.

text: What is the key disadvantage of the one-to-one threading model?
options: It does not support kernel-level threading
Creating a user thread requires creating a corresponding kernel thread, adding overhead
It cannot run on multiprocessor systems
It merges all threads into a single execution context
explanation: In the one-to-one model, each user thread maps to a kernel thread. While this provides true concurrency, creating each user thread requires creating a kernel thread, which adds overhead and may limit the total number of threads.

text: What is the purpose of the pthread_join() function?
options: To create a new thread
To wait for a specific thread to terminate before continuing
To send a signal to another thread
To detach a thread from the process
explanation: pthread_join() blocks the calling thread until the specified thread terminates. It is used to synchronize thread completion, similar to how wait() works for processes.

text: What happens when a user-level thread makes a blocking system call in a many-to-one model?
options: Only that thread blocks while others continue
The entire process blocks because the single kernel thread is blocked
The thread is automatically converted to a kernel thread
The system call is ignored
explanation: In the many-to-one model, all user threads map to a single kernel thread. When one user thread makes a blocking system call, the kernel thread blocks, which effectively blocks all user threads in that process.

text: What is the difference between task parallelism and data parallelism?
options: Task parallelism distributes data across cores; data parallelism distributes tasks
Data parallelism distributes subsets of data across cores performing the same operation; task parallelism distributes different tasks across cores
They are different names for the same concept
Data parallelism only works with databases
explanation: Data parallelism distributes subsets of the same data across multiple cores and performs the same operation on each subset. Task parallelism distributes different tasks (threads) across cores, where each thread may perform a unique operation.

text: What is the purpose of pthread_cancel() in the Pthreads API?
options: To create a cancelable thread
To request termination of a target thread
To pause a thread temporarily
To increase a thread's priority
explanation: pthread_cancel() sends a cancellation request to a target thread. Whether and when the thread actually terminates depends on the thread's cancellation state and type (deferred or asynchronous).

text: Why is asynchronous thread cancellation considered more dangerous than deferred cancellation?
options: It uses more memory
The target thread is terminated immediately, potentially leaving shared data in an inconsistent state
It requires kernel mode exclusively
It can only cancel one thread at a time
explanation: Asynchronous cancellation terminates the target thread immediately, which may leave shared resources in an inconsistent state if the thread was in the middle of updating them. Deferred cancellation lets the thread check for cancellation at safe points.

text: What advantage does a thread pool provide over creating threads on demand?
options: Thread pools use more memory
Pre-created threads reduce the overhead of thread creation and limit the total number of threads
Thread pools eliminate the need for synchronization
Thread pools allow only one thread at a time
explanation: A thread pool maintains a set of pre-created threads that wait for tasks, reducing the latency of thread creation for each request and preventing resource exhaustion by limiting the number of concurrent threads.

text: In the context of threading, what is the fork-join model?
options: A model where threads can only fork new processes
A model where a parent thread creates child threads that execute tasks and then join back with the parent
A model specific to database transactions
A model that prevents any thread from terminating
explanation: In the fork-join model, a parent thread forks (creates) multiple child threads to perform work in parallel, and then waits (joins) for all child threads to complete before continuing execution.

text: What is the relationship between threads and signals in UNIX?
options: Signals can only be sent to processes, never to individual threads
A signal may be delivered to the specific thread to which it applies, to every thread, or to a designated thread
Threads automatically ignore all signals
Each thread has its own independent signal handler table
explanation: In UNIX, signal delivery in multithreaded programs can vary: synchronous signals go to the thread that caused them, while asynchronous signals may go to any thread, all threads, or a specific designated thread depending on the signal type.

text: How does the Linux kernel implement threads differently from traditional UNIX thread implementations?
options: Linux does not support threads at all
Linux uses the clone() system call to create threads as tasks that share the address space, treating threads and processes uniformly
Linux requires all threads to run in user mode only
Linux threads cannot share file descriptors
explanation: Linux does not distinguish between threads and processes internally. It uses the clone() system call with flags to control which resources are shared, creating tasks (threads) that share the parent's address space, file descriptors, and signal handlers.

text: What is the N:M threading model's primary implementation challenge that led most OSes to abandon it?
options: It uses too much disk space
Coordinating user-level and kernel-level schedulers is complex and the performance benefits rarely justify the complexity
It cannot support more than two threads
It is incompatible with virtual memory
explanation: The N:M (many-to-many) model requires complex coordination between the user-level thread scheduler and the kernel scheduler, including upcalls and LWP management. Most modern OSes abandoned it because the 1:1 model with lightweight kernel threads provides simpler, comparable performance.

text: What is thread affinity and why does it matter for performance?
options: Thread affinity refers to how friendly threads are to each other
It binds a thread to a specific CPU core to exploit warm caches and reduce cache migration overhead
Thread affinity means threads must always run on different cores
It is a security feature that isolates threads
explanation: Thread affinity (soft or hard) binds a thread to a specific CPU core. This is important for performance because it allows the thread to benefit from warm L1/L2 caches, avoiding the overhead of cache-line migration when a thread migrates between cores.

text: What problem does the NUMA-aware thread scheduling try to solve?
options: Reducing the number of threads in the system
Minimizing memory access latency by scheduling threads on cores close to the memory they access
Preventing threads from accessing shared memory
Converting kernel threads to user threads
explanation: In Non-Uniform Memory Access (NUMA) architectures, memory access time depends on the memory location relative to the processor. NUMA-aware scheduling places threads on cores closest to their frequently accessed memory to minimize latency.

text: What is cooperative multithreading and what is its main drawback?
options: Threads are scheduled by the kernel preemptively; the drawback is high overhead
Threads voluntarily yield the CPU; a misbehaving thread can monopolize the CPU indefinitely
Threads share all variables including the stack; the drawback is data corruption
Only one thread can exist per process; the drawback is no parallelism
explanation: In cooperative (non-preemptive) multithreading, threads voluntarily yield control. The main drawback is that a thread that fails to yield (due to a bug or long computation) can starve other threads and monopolize the CPU.

text: How do green threads differ from native threads?
options: Green threads use less electricity
Green threads are scheduled by the runtime or virtual machine in user space, not by the OS kernel
Green threads are always faster than native threads
Green threads can only run on green computing hardware
explanation: Green threads are managed entirely by a runtime library or virtual machine (e.g., early Java VMs) in user space, without kernel support. They cannot achieve true parallelism on multicore systems since the kernel sees only one thread.

text: What is the purpose of thread-specific data (TSD) and how does it differ from regular global variables?
options: TSD is stored on disk for persistence
TSD provides each thread with its own copy of a variable, unlike global variables which are shared among all threads
TSD makes variables immutable across all threads
TSD encrypts thread data for security
explanation: Thread-specific data (TSD) or thread-local storage (TLS) gives each thread its own private copy of a variable. Unlike global variables that are shared and require synchronization, TSD is unique per thread, useful for maintaining per-thread state like errno.

text: What are coroutines and how do they relate to threads?
options: Coroutines are a type of hardware thread
Coroutines are cooperatively scheduled lightweight execution units that can suspend and resume at specific points, often multiplexed over fewer OS threads
Coroutines and threads are identical concepts
Coroutines can only execute sequentially with no concurrency
explanation: Coroutines are lightweight, cooperatively scheduled execution units that can suspend and resume at programmer-defined points (yield/await). Languages like Go (goroutines), Python (async/await), and Kotlin use coroutines multiplexed over OS threads for efficient concurrency.

text: What is the thundering herd problem in the context of thread pools?
options: Too many threads are created, exhausting memory
Multiple threads are woken up to handle a single event, but only one can process it, wasting resources
Threads run too slowly due to excessive synchronization
All threads in the pool terminate simultaneously
explanation: The thundering herd problem occurs when multiple waiting threads are all woken up by a single event (e.g., a new connection), but only one can handle it. The rest wake up unnecessarily, causing CPU waste and contention. Solutions include waking only one thread (e.g., EPOLLEXCLUSIVE).

text: What is work stealing in the context of thread pool task scheduling?
options: A malicious technique where threads steal CPU time
An idle thread takes tasks from the queue of a busy thread to balance the workload dynamically
A method to prevent threads from executing tasks
A technique that forces all threads to execute the same task
explanation: Work stealing is a load-balancing technique where idle threads steal tasks from the work queues of busy threads. This dynamically balances the workload across cores, improving throughput. It is used in frameworks like Java ForkJoinPool and Intel TBB.

text: What is the purpose of process synchronization?
options: To make all processes run at the same speed
To coordinate the execution of processes that share resources to prevent data inconsistency
To synchronize the system clock with an external server
To ensure all processes start at the same time
explanation: Process synchronization ensures that processes sharing resources (such as memory or files) coordinate their execution properly to prevent data inconsistency and race conditions.

text: The 'progress' requirement for the critical section problem states that:
options: All processes must execute at the same speed
Only processes not in their remainder section can participate in deciding which process enters the critical section next
A process must never leave the critical section
All processes must enter the critical section simultaneously
explanation: The progress requirement states that if no process is executing in its critical section, then only those processes not in their remainder section can participate in deciding which will enter next, and this selection cannot be postponed indefinitely.

text: The 'bounded waiting' requirement for the critical section problem ensures that:
options: The critical section has a fixed size
There is a limit on the number of times other processes can enter the critical section after a process has made a request
Processes wait for exactly 10 seconds
Only bounded variables are used
explanation: Bounded waiting ensures that after a process has requested entry into its critical section, there is a bound on the number of times other processes are allowed to enter their critical sections before the requesting process is granted access.

text: What does a mutex lock provide?
options: Unlimited concurrent access to shared data
Mutual exclusion so that only one thread can hold the lock at a time
Automatic parallelization of code
Network synchronization between computers
explanation: A mutex (mutual exclusion) lock ensures that only one thread can hold the lock and access the protected shared resource at any given time. Other threads must wait until the lock is released.

text: What operation decreases the value of a semaphore?
options: signal()
post()
wait()
release()
explanation: The wait() operation (also called P() or down()) decrements the value of a semaphore. If the resulting value is negative, the calling process is blocked until the semaphore is incremented by a signal() operation.

text: What is a spinlock?
options: A lock that puts the waiting thread to sleep
A lock where the waiting thread continuously checks if the lock is available in a loop
A lock that only works on single-core systems
A lock that spins the hard disk for faster access
explanation: A spinlock is a synchronization mechanism where a thread waiting to acquire the lock continuously polls (spins) in a loop checking whether the lock has become available, consuming CPU cycles while waiting.

text: In the producer-consumer problem, what shared resource do the producer and consumer access?
options: The CPU registers
A bounded buffer
The page table
The file system journal
explanation: In the producer-consumer problem, the producer generates data items and places them in a bounded buffer (shared memory), while the consumer removes items from the buffer. Synchronization is needed to prevent buffer overflow and underflow.

text: What is an atomic operation?
options: An operation related to nuclear physics
An operation that completes entirely without interruption
An operation that can be partially executed
An operation that requires multiple CPU cycles
explanation: An atomic operation is an operation that is indivisible — it completes in its entirety without being interrupted. This property is essential for implementing synchronization primitives correctly.

text: What does the signal() operation do on a semaphore?
options: Decreases its value by one
Increases its value by one and may wake a waiting process
Destroys the semaphore
Resets the semaphore to zero
explanation: The signal() operation (also called V() or up()) increments the semaphore's value. If there are processes waiting on the semaphore, one of them is woken up and allowed to proceed.

text: What is the key difference between a binary semaphore and a counting semaphore?
options: Binary semaphores are faster
A binary semaphore has only values 0 and 1, while a counting semaphore can range over an unrestricted domain
Counting semaphores cannot be used for mutual exclusion
Binary semaphores work only in user mode
explanation: A binary semaphore can only take values 0 or 1, functioning similarly to a mutex lock. A counting semaphore can take any non-negative integer value, making it suitable for controlling access to a resource with a finite number of instances.

text: How does the test-and-set instruction help solve the critical section problem?
options: It tests whether the CPU is available
It atomically reads a variable and sets it to true, enabling mutual exclusion without interruption
It sets all variables in memory to zero
It tests the speed of the system bus
explanation: The test-and-set instruction atomically reads the current value of a boolean variable and sets it to true in a single indivisible step. This allows implementing mutual exclusion: a thread acquires the lock only if the old value was false.

text: In the bounded buffer problem using semaphores, what are the three semaphores typically used?
options: read, write, execute
mutex (for mutual exclusion), full (counts filled slots), empty (counts empty slots)
start, stop, pause
lock, unlock, wait
explanation: The bounded buffer solution uses three semaphores: 'mutex' (binary, for mutual exclusion when accessing the buffer), 'full' (counting, initialized to 0, tracks filled slots), and 'empty' (counting, initialized to buffer size, tracks empty slots).

text: What is the key challenge in the readers-writers problem?
options: Ensuring that files can only be read once
Allowing multiple readers simultaneous access while ensuring writers get exclusive access
Preventing any process from reading data
Synchronizing disk rotational speed
explanation: The readers-writers problem requires allowing multiple readers to read shared data simultaneously (since reading doesn't modify data), while ensuring that writers have exclusive access (no other readers or writers) when writing.

text: How does priority inversion occur in synchronization?
options: A high-priority process runs before a low-priority process
A high-priority process is blocked waiting for a lock held by a low-priority process, which is itself preempted by medium-priority processes
All processes run with the same priority
The scheduler inverts the order of the ready queue
explanation: Priority inversion occurs when a high-priority process is indirectly blocked by a medium-priority process: the high-priority process waits for a lock held by a low-priority process, which cannot run because medium-priority processes preempt it.

text: What is the purpose of a condition variable in a monitor?
options: To store the return value of a function
To allow a thread to wait until a particular condition is met, and to be notified when it changes
To count the number of active monitors
To monitor CPU temperature conditions
explanation: A condition variable allows a thread inside a monitor to wait for a specific condition to become true. The thread releases the monitor lock and sleeps until another thread signals the condition variable, indicating the condition may now be satisfied.

text: What is the difference between wait() and signal() on a condition variable versus on a semaphore?
options: They are identical in behavior
Condition variable signal() has no effect if no thread is waiting, while semaphore signal() always increments the value
Semaphore wait() never blocks
Condition variables can only be used in Java
explanation: A key difference is that a signal() on a condition variable is lost if no thread is currently waiting (it has no memory), while a signal() on a semaphore always increments the counter, affecting future wait() calls even if no thread is currently blocked.

text: What problem can occur when semaphores are used incorrectly, such as swapping the order of wait() operations?
options: Faster execution
Deadlock, where two or more processes are each waiting on semaphores held by the other
Automatic error correction
Improved throughput
explanation: Incorrect use of semaphores, such as calling wait(mutex) before wait(empty) in the producer-consumer problem, can lead to deadlock where processes are permanently blocked waiting for each other to release semaphores.

text: What is a reentrant lock and how does it differ from a standard mutex?
options: A reentrant lock can only be acquired once
A reentrant lock allows the same thread to acquire it multiple times without deadlocking
A reentrant lock is slower than a standard mutex in all cases
A reentrant lock provides no mutual exclusion
explanation: A reentrant (recursive) lock allows the same thread to acquire the lock multiple times without causing a deadlock. The lock maintains an acquisition count and is only truly released when the count drops to zero, unlike a standard mutex which would deadlock on re-acquisition.

text: Why are spinlocks more efficient than blocking locks on multiprocessor systems for short critical sections?
options: Spinlocks use less memory
Spinning avoids the overhead of context switching, which would take longer than the short wait time
Spinlocks automatically parallelize the critical section
Blocking locks are not supported on multiprocessors
explanation: For short critical sections on multiprocessors, the time spent spinning is less than the overhead of two context switches (blocking and waking up). Since another CPU can release the lock while the waiting thread spins, spinlocks avoid unnecessary context switch costs.

text: How does the priority ceiling protocol prevent both deadlock and priority inversion?
options: By removing all locks from the system

By assigning each lock a priority ceiling equal to the highest priority of any thread that may lock it, and only allowing a thread to acquire a lock if its priority is higher than the ceiling of all currently locked mutexes
By running all threads at the same priority
By disabling interrupts permanently
explanation: The priority ceiling protocol assigns each mutex a ceiling priority equal to the highest-priority thread that may use it. A thread can only acquire a mutex if its priority exceeds the ceiling of all mutexes currently locked by other threads, preventing circular dependencies and priority inversion.

text: What is sequence consistency (sequential consistency) in the context of concurrent memory operations?
options: All memory operations execute in parallel without ordering
The result of any execution is the same as if the operations of all processors were executed in some sequential order, preserving each processor's program order
Memory is accessed only sequentially by one processor at a time
It means data is stored in sorted sequence
explanation: Sequential consistency (Lamport, 1979) guarantees that the result of any concurrent execution appears as if all memory operations from all processors were interleaved in some total order that respects each processor's individual program order.

text: What is the difference between lock-free and wait-free algorithms?
options: They are identical concepts
Lock-free guarantees system-wide progress (at least one thread progresses); wait-free guarantees per-thread progress (every thread completes in bounded steps)
Wait-free algorithms use more locks than lock-free algorithms
Lock-free means no synchronization is needed
explanation: Lock-free algorithms guarantee that at least one thread makes progress in a finite number of steps (no system-wide blocking). Wait-free algorithms are stronger: every thread completes its operation in a bounded number of steps, regardless of other threads' behavior.

text: How does Read-Copy-Update (RCU) allow readers to access shared data without acquiring locks?
options: By encrypting the data so readers can safely access it
Readers access the current version without locks; writers create a new copy, update it, and atomically switch the pointer, deferring reclamation of old data until all readers finish
Readers must always copy the data before reading
RCU prevents any concurrent writes
explanation: RCU allows readers to access shared data without locks by ensuring they always see a consistent version. Writers create a copy, update it, and swap the pointer atomically. The old version is reclaimed after a grace period when all pre-existing readers have completed.

text: What is the ABA problem and how is it typically solved?
options: It is a problem with alphabetical sorting in concurrent programs
A thread reads value A, another thread changes it to B then back to A; the first thread's compare-and-swap succeeds incorrectly; solved using version counters or hazard pointers
It occurs when three threads simultaneously access the same variable
It is a deadlock pattern involving three resources
explanation: The ABA problem occurs in lock-free algorithms when a CAS operation succeeds because a value is A, but the value was changed to B and back to A by other threads, potentially hiding structural changes. It is typically solved with version counters (double-width CAS) or hazard pointers.

text: What is a memory barrier (fence) and why is it necessary on modern processors?
options: A physical barrier in RAM chips that prevents electrical interference
An instruction that enforces ordering constraints on memory operations, preventing the CPU and compiler from reordering reads and writes across the barrier
A software firewall for memory protection
A method to increase memory bandwidth
explanation: Modern CPUs and compilers reorder memory operations for performance. A memory barrier (fence) is an instruction that enforces ordering: all memory operations before the barrier complete before operations after it begin, which is critical for correct synchronization.

text: How does transactional memory simplify concurrent programming compared to lock-based approaches?
options: It eliminates the need for any concurrency control
Programmers designate code regions as transactions that execute atomically; conflicts are detected automatically, eliminating manual lock management and reducing deadlock risk
It makes all memory accesses sequential
It converts concurrent programs into single-threaded programs
explanation: Transactional memory allows programmers to mark code regions as transactions that appear atomic. The system detects conflicts and aborts/retries conflicting transactions automatically, eliminating manual lock ordering, reducing deadlocks, and simplifying concurrent code.

text: In the context of Linux kernel synchronization, what are per-CPU variables and why are they efficient?
options: Variables that count the number of CPUs in the system
Each CPU has its own copy of the variable, eliminating the need for locking since no sharing occurs between CPUs
Variables that are shared equally among all CPUs
Variables that store CPU temperature readings
explanation: Per-CPU variables give each CPU its own private copy of a variable. Since each CPU accesses only its own copy, no locking or atomic operations are needed, eliminating synchronization overhead and cache-line bouncing between cores.

text: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic concurrency control?
options: Optimistic control is always faster
Pessimistic control acquires locks before accessing data; optimistic control proceeds without locks and validates at commit time, aborting on conflict
They produce different results for the same operations
Optimistic control does not support concurrent access
explanation: Pessimistic concurrency control acquires locks before accessing shared data, preventing conflicts. Optimistic control allows concurrent access without locks, then validates at commit time that no conflicts occurred — if a conflict is detected, the transaction is aborted and retried.

text: What is the sleeping barber problem and what synchronization challenge does it illustrate?
options: A problem about scheduling CPU sleep states
A barber sleeps when no customers are present and customers must wake the barber or wait; it illustrates coordination between producers and consumers with a bounded waiting area
A deadlock scenario involving three barber threads
A memory management problem involving page sleeping
explanation: The sleeping barber problem models a barber shop where the barber sleeps when idle and customers either wake the barber or wait in a limited waiting room. It illustrates inter-process communication, bounded buffer synchronization, and the need to avoid race conditions between arriving customers and the barber.

text: Which of the following is a necessary condition for deadlock?
options: Preemption of all resources
Mutual exclusion
Unlimited resources
Single-threaded execution
explanation: Mutual exclusion is one of the four necessary conditions for deadlock. It means at least one resource must be held in a non-sharable mode, so only one process can use it at a time.

text: In a resource allocation graph, what does an edge from a process to a resource indicate?
options: The process has finished using the resource
The process is requesting the resource
The resource is allocated to the process
The process has released the resource
explanation: In a resource allocation graph, a request edge goes from a process to a resource type, indicating that the process is requesting an instance of that resource. An assignment edge goes from a resource to a process, indicating allocation.

text: What are the four necessary conditions for deadlock?
options: Speed, memory, I/O, CPU
Mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait
Scheduling, paging, caching, buffering
Authentication, authorization, encryption, logging
explanation: The four necessary conditions for deadlock (Coffman conditions) are: mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait. All four must hold simultaneously for a deadlock to occur.

text: What is deadlock avoidance?
options: Ignoring the possibility of deadlock
Using additional information about future resource requests to ensure the system never enters an unsafe state
Terminating all processes periodically
Removing all shared resources from the system
explanation: Deadlock avoidance uses information about the maximum resources each process might request to dynamically check whether granting a request would leave the system in a safe state, ensuring deadlock can never occur.

text: What does deadlock recovery involve?
options: Preventing deadlock from ever occurring
Detecting that a deadlock has occurred and taking action to break it
Ignoring the deadlock permanently
Adding more resources to the system preemptively
explanation: Deadlock recovery is the process of detecting that a deadlock has occurred and then taking corrective action, such as terminating one or more processes or preempting resources, to break the circular wait and restore progress.

text: An unsafe state means:
options: Deadlock has already occurred
The system may potentially lead to deadlock; there is no guarantee of a safe sequence
All processes have terminated
The system has run out of memory
explanation: An unsafe state does not mean deadlock has occurred, but rather that no safe sequence of process execution is guaranteed. The system may or may not deadlock — it simply cannot guarantee that deadlock will be avoided.

text: In deadlock handling, what does the 'no preemption' condition mean?
options: Resources can be forcibly taken from processes at any time
Resources cannot be forcibly taken away from a process; they must be released voluntarily
Processes cannot be scheduled on the CPU
No process has higher priority than another
explanation: The no preemption condition means that a resource held by a process cannot be forcibly taken away — the process must voluntarily release it. This is one of the four necessary conditions for deadlock.

text: Which of the following is an example of a reusable resource?
options: A CPU interrupt signal
A printer or a memory block
A network packet
A one-time password
explanation: Reusable resources like printers, memory blocks, and CPU cores can be used by one process, released, and then used again by another process. Consumable resources like messages or interrupts are created and destroyed during use.

text: What is the simplest method to deal with deadlocks?
options: Use the Banker's Algorithm
Ignore the problem entirely (ostrich algorithm)
Preempt all resources
Allow only one process to run at a time
explanation: The simplest approach is the ostrich algorithm — simply ignore the deadlock problem. This is used when deadlocks are rare and the cost of prevention or detection is higher than the cost of an occasional restart. Many general-purpose OSes including UNIX use this approach.

text: What type of graph is used specifically for deadlock detection with single-instance resources?
options: A binary tree
A wait-for graph
A B+ tree
A hash table
explanation: A wait-for graph is a simplified version of a resource allocation graph used when each resource type has a single instance. It has only process nodes, with an edge from Pi to Pj meaning Pi is waiting for a resource held by Pj. A cycle indicates deadlock.

text: How does imposing a total ordering on resource types prevent circular wait?
options: It requires processes to release all resources before requesting new ones
Processes must request resources in increasing order of their assigned numbers, making circular dependencies impossible
It limits the number of resources available
It assigns equal priority to all resources
explanation: By assigning each resource type a unique number and requiring processes to request resources only in increasing numerical order, circular wait is impossible because a cycle would require some process to request a lower-numbered resource while holding a higher-numbered one.

text: In the Banker's Algorithm, what does the 'Available' vector represent?
options: The total number of resources in the system
The number of instances of each resource type currently available for allocation
The maximum resources any process can request
The resources that have been permanently allocated
explanation: The Available vector in the Banker's Algorithm represents the number of instances of each resource type that are currently free and available for allocation. It is updated as processes request and release resources.

text: What factors should be considered when selecting a victim process for deadlock recovery?
options: Only the process name
Priority, computation time completed, resources held, remaining time, and number of rollbacks
Only the process ID number
The color of the user's terminal
explanation: Selecting a victim for deadlock recovery should consider multiple factors: process priority, how much computation has been done, resources held, how much longer the process needs, and how many times it has been rolled back (to avoid starvation).

text: What is the difference between deadlock prevention and deadlock avoidance?
options: They are identical approaches
Prevention ensures one of the four necessary conditions can never hold; avoidance dynamically checks each request to stay in a safe state
Avoidance eliminates resources; prevention adds resources
Prevention is used only in distributed systems
explanation: Deadlock prevention constrains the system so that at least one of the four necessary conditions (mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait) can never hold. Deadlock avoidance uses runtime information to dynamically ensure the system stays in a safe state.

text: What is the safe sequence in the context of the Banker's Algorithm?
options: A random ordering of all processes
An ordering of processes such that each process can obtain its maximum required resources from currently available resources plus resources held by all preceding processes
The order in which processes were created
A sequence where no process ever requests resources
explanation: A safe sequence is an ordering <P1, P2, ..., Pn> of all processes such that for each Pi, the resources that Pi can still request can be satisfied by the currently available resources plus the resources held by all Pj where j < i.

text: How can the hold-and-wait condition be prevented by requiring processes to request all resources at once?
options: Processes request resources one at a time
A process must request all needed resources before execution begins; it cannot hold some and wait for others
Processes share all resources simultaneously
The OS holds all resources and never allocates them
explanation: One way to prevent hold-and-wait is to require each process to request all its needed resources at the start before execution. The downside is low resource utilization (resources held but not yet needed) and potential starvation.

text: What is the difference between process termination and resource preemption as deadlock recovery methods?
options: They are the same approach
Process termination kills processes to break deadlock; resource preemption forcibly takes resources from processes and reassigns them
Resource preemption always terminates all processes
Process termination only pauses processes temporarily
explanation: Process termination breaks the deadlock by aborting one or more processes (either all deadlocked processes or one at a time). Resource preemption breaks deadlock by forcibly taking resources from some processes and giving them to others, then rolling back the preempted processes.

text: Why is the Banker's Algorithm considered impractical for many real systems?
options: It is too simple to implement
It requires processes to declare their maximum resource needs in advance, which is often unknown
It only works with one resource type
It causes deadlock instead of preventing it
explanation: The Banker's Algorithm requires each process to declare its maximum resource needs before execution. In practice, processes often cannot predict their maximum requirements in advance, making the algorithm impractical for general-purpose operating systems.

text: In deadlock detection with multiple resource instances, what algorithm is used?
options: Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm
A variant of the Banker's Algorithm that checks if the current state can complete all processes
Binary search on resource allocation
Depth-first search on a binary tree
explanation: For multiple-instance resources, deadlock detection uses an algorithm similar to the Banker's safety algorithm. It checks whether existing processes can all finish with the available resources. Processes that cannot finish are considered deadlocked.

text: What is starvation in the context of deadlock recovery, and how can it be prevented?
options: Starvation is when the CPU runs out of power
The same process is repeatedly selected as the victim for rollback; prevented by including rollback count in victim selection criteria
Starvation means processes do not have enough memory
It is a type of deadlock that cannot be prevented
explanation: In deadlock recovery, starvation occurs when the same process is always selected as the victim for termination or rollback. This is prevented by including the number of rollbacks as a cost factor in victim selection, ensuring no process is repeatedly victimized.

text: What is the wait-die scheme for deadlock prevention in database systems?
options: Processes wait indefinitely until resources become available
An older process waits for a younger one to release a resource, but a younger process requesting a resource held by an older one is aborted (dies)
All processes die when a deadlock is detected
Processes are assigned random wait times
explanation: In the wait-die scheme, if an older process requests a resource held by a younger one, it waits. If a younger process requests a resource held by an older one, the younger process is rolled back (dies). This prevents circular wait by ensuring only older processes wait.

text: How does the edge-chasing algorithm detect deadlocks in distributed systems?
options: By checking all resource allocation graphs simultaneously
Probe messages are forwarded along edges of the distributed wait-for graph; if a probe returns to its initiator, a cycle (deadlock) exists
By centralizing all resource information on one node
By terminating all processes on all nodes periodically
explanation: In the edge-chasing (path-pushing) algorithm, when a process blocks, it sends a probe message along the wait-for edges across nodes. Each blocked process forwards the probe to the process it's waiting for. If the probe returns to the initiator, a cycle (deadlock) is detected.

text: What is a phantom deadlock in distributed deadlock detection?
options: A deadlock that occurs in virtual machines only
A falsely detected deadlock caused by delays in propagating status information across nodes in a distributed system
A deadlock involving phantom processes that do not exist
A deadlock that occurs during system startup
explanation: A phantom deadlock is a false positive in distributed deadlock detection. It occurs because the global wait-for graph is constructed from local snapshots taken at different times, and communication delays can cause a non-existent cycle to appear temporarily.

text: What is the relationship between deadlock and livelock in resource contention?
options: Livelock is a more severe form of deadlock
In deadlock, processes are blocked and make no progress; in livelock, processes continuously change state in response to each other but still make no progress
Livelock is identical to deadlock
Livelock occurs only with non-preemptive scheduling
explanation: Deadlock involves processes that are blocked, waiting for each other, and making no state changes. Livelock involves processes that continuously change state (e.g., repeatedly backing off and retrying) in response to each other but make no actual progress — like two people in a corridor repeatedly stepping aside in the same direction.

text: How does lock ordering (hierarchical locking) prevent deadlock in practice?
options: By requiring all locks to be held for the same duration
By defining a global order on all locks and requiring every thread to acquire locks only in that order, preventing cycles in the wait-for graph
By limiting the number of locks in the system to one
By making all locks recursive
explanation: Lock ordering assigns a unique rank to each lock and requires all threads to acquire locks in strictly increasing rank order. This eliminates the possibility of circular wait: if thread A holds lock i and requests lock j, then i < j, so no cycle can form.

text: What is the knot in a resource allocation graph?
options: A cycle involving exactly two processes
A set of nodes such that every node in the set has a path to every other node in the set with no outgoing edges leaving the set
A single node with no edges
An edge that connects a resource to itself
explanation: A knot in a resource allocation graph is a strongly connected component where every node can reach every other node within the set, and no node in the set has an outgoing edge to a node outside the set. A knot indicates a deadlock even with multiple-instance resources.

text: What is two-phase locking and how does it relate to deadlock in database systems?
options: It prevents deadlock by ensuring locks are acquired in two distinct phases
In the growing phase all needed locks are acquired and in the shrinking phase locks are released; it ensures serializability but does not prevent deadlock
It acquires and releases each lock twice for safety
It uses two separate lock managers to prevent deadlock
explanation: Two-phase locking (2PL) has a growing phase (only acquiring locks) and a shrinking phase (only releasing locks). While 2PL guarantees serializability of transactions, it does not prevent deadlock — cycles can still form during the growing phase.

text: How does the Linux kernel detect potential deadlocks using lockdep?
options: By running the Banker's Algorithm at each lock acquisition
By tracking lock acquisition order at runtime and checking for potential cycles in the lock dependency graph, even if the actual deadlock hasn't occurred
By limiting the number of locks to 10
By preventing any two threads from running simultaneously
explanation: Linux's lockdep subsystem tracks the order in which locks are acquired at runtime and builds a lock dependency graph. It detects potential deadlocks proactively by checking for cycles in this graph, catching ordering violations even before an actual deadlock occurs.

text: What is the convoy effect in the context of resource scheduling, and how does it relate to deadlock-like behavior?
options: A high-speed scheduling algorithm
Many processes queue behind a slow process holding a resource, causing a chain of delays similar to but distinct from deadlock
All processes execute in parallel without any delays
Processes are permanently blocked and never execute
explanation: The convoy effect occurs when a slow process holds a resource (like a lock or I/O device), causing many faster processes to queue behind it, drastically reducing throughput. While not a deadlock (processes do eventually proceed), it creates severe performance degradation similar to a traffic convoy.

text: What is the difference between centralized and distributed deadlock detection, and what are the trade-offs?
options: They are identical approaches applied to different operating systems

Centralized detection maintains a global wait-for graph at one coordinator (simpler but single point of failure); distributed detection uses local algorithms with message passing (more resilient but may detect phantom deadlocks)
Centralized detection is always faster and more accurate
Distributed detection eliminates all false positives
explanation: Centralized detection collects wait-for information at a single coordinator to build a global graph — simple but creates a bottleneck and single point of failure. Distributed detection uses local algorithms with probe messages — more resilient but susceptible to phantom deadlocks due to message delays.

text: What is the primary function of a Memory Management Unit (MMU)?
options: To increase the size of physical RAM
To translate logical addresses to physical addresses at runtime
To manage the file system
To schedule CPU processes
explanation: The MMU is a hardware device that translates logical (virtual) addresses generated by the CPU to physical addresses in main memory at runtime, enabling processes to use virtual address spaces.

text: What is a frame in the context of paging?
options: A division of logical memory
A fixed-size block of physical memory
A type of CPU register
A section of the hard disk
explanation: In paging, physical memory is divided into fixed-size blocks called frames. Pages (equal-sized blocks of logical memory) are loaded into these frames. The page size equals the frame size.

text: What is the difference between a logical address and a physical address?
options: They are always the same value
A logical address is generated by the CPU and a physical address is the actual location in memory hardware
A physical address is virtual and a logical address is real
Logical addresses are used only for disk storage
explanation: A logical (virtual) address is generated by the CPU during program execution, while a physical address is the actual location in the physical memory hardware. The MMU translates logical addresses to physical addresses.

text: What is the purpose of a base register in memory management?
options: To store the process's name
To define the starting physical address of a process's memory space
To count the number of pages
To store the program counter
