Two processes must always execute at the same time
Processes are prevented from ever communicating
Only one process can access a critical section at a time
explanation: Mutual exclusion ensures that only one process can execute in its critical section at a time, preventing simultaneous access to shared resources that could lead to data inconsistency.

text: What is a critical section?
options: A code segment where shared resources are accessed
The most computationally expensive part of a program
A section of code that handles hardware interrupts
The first line of code executed when a process starts
explanation: A critical section is a segment of code in which a process accesses shared resources (variables, files, etc.) that must not be accessed concurrently by other processes.

text: What is a semaphore?
options: A hardware device for network communication
A type of CPU register for storing addresses
An integer variable used for process synchronization
A memory management technique for paging
explanation: A semaphore is an integer variable accessed only through two atomic operations, wait() and signal(), used to solve synchronization problems like mutual exclusion and process coordination.

text: What is the difference between a binary and a counting semaphore?
options: Binary semaphores cannot be used for synchronization
Binary has values 0 or 1; counting has unrestricted range
Both types have identical functionality and range
Counting has values 0 or 1; binary has unrestricted range
explanation: A binary semaphore can only have values 0 or 1 (acting like a mutex), while a counting semaphore can range over an unrestricted integer domain, useful for controlling access to resources with multiple instances.

text: What is a mutex lock?
options: A hardware lock on the computer's physical chassis
A type of memory allocation strategy for processes
A synchronization tool that provides mutual exclusion
A network security protocol for data encryption
explanation: A mutex (mutual exclusion) lock is a synchronization tool where a process must acquire the lock before entering a critical section and release it when exiting, ensuring only one process enters at a time.

text: What is busy waiting in synchronization?
options: A process loops continuously while waiting to enter a section
A process sleeps until a resource becomes available
A process terminates when it cannot access a resource
A process creates a new thread to wait on its behalf
explanation: Busy waiting (spinlocking) occurs when a process continuously checks a condition in a loop while waiting to enter a critical section, wasting CPU cycles that could be used by other processes.

text: What is the purpose of the wait() operation on a semaphore?
options: It increments the semaphore and wakes a waiting process
It destroys the semaphore and frees its memory resources
It decrements the semaphore and blocks if the value is negative
It creates a new semaphore with an initial value of one
explanation: The wait() (or P/down) operation decrements the semaphore value. If the value becomes negative, the process is blocked and placed in a waiting queue until signaled.

text: What does the signal() operation do on a semaphore?
options: It decrements the semaphore and blocks the calling process
It resets the semaphore value to its initial value
It deletes the semaphore from the system permanently
It increments the semaphore and may wake a waiting process
explanation: The signal() (or V/up) operation increments the semaphore value. If there are processes waiting on the semaphore, one of them is woken up and allowed to proceed.

text: What is the Peterson's solution used for?
options: Scheduling CPU time among multiple processes in a queue
Solving the critical section problem for two processes
Allocating memory pages to processes using paging
Translating virtual addresses to physical addresses
explanation: Peterson's solution is a classical software-based algorithm that solves the critical section problem for two processes using two shared variables: a turn variable and a flag array.

text: What are the three requirements for a valid critical section solution?
options: Atomicity, consistency, and isolation of transactions
Deadlock prevention, avoidance, and detection methods
Confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data
Mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting
explanation: A valid solution to the critical section problem must satisfy mutual exclusion (only one in CS), progress (selection cannot be postponed indefinitely), and bounded waiting (limit on how long a process waits).

text: How does the test-and-set instruction support mutual exclusion?
options: It tests whether a file exists and sets its permissions
It atomically reads and sets a variable in one operation
It sets all semaphores in the system to their initial state
It tests memory speed and sets the CPU clock accordingly
explanation: Test-and-set is an atomic hardware instruction that reads the old value of a variable and sets it to true in one indivisible operation, used to implement locks without being interrupted.

text: What is the bounded buffer problem?
options: A problem where buffers cannot store any data at all
A network buffering issue causing packet loss on routers
A buffer that grows without limit consuming all memory
Producers and consumers share a fixed-size buffer needing sync
explanation: The bounded buffer problem involves producer and consumer processes sharing a fixed-size buffer, requiring synchronization to ensure the producer does not write to a full buffer and the consumer does not read from an empty one.

text: What is the readers-writers problem?
options: A problem related to disk read-write head scheduling
Allowing concurrent reads but exclusive writes to shared data
Ensuring all processes write to data at the same time
Preventing any process from reading or writing shared files
explanation: The readers-writers problem involves allowing multiple readers to access shared data simultaneously, but requiring exclusive access for writers, ensuring no reader and writer access the data at the same time.

text: What is the dining philosophers problem illustrating?
options: How to manage file permissions across user accounts
The fastest algorithm for sorting data in memory
The optimal way to schedule CPU-bound processes
Deadlock and resource contention among concurrent processes
explanation: The dining philosophers problem illustrates deadlock and resource contention: five philosophers share chopsticks and can deadlock if each picks up one chopstick and waits for the other, modeling concurrent resource allocation.

text: What is a monitor in concurrent programming?
options: A hardware device that displays output on a screen
A high-level synchronization construct encapsulating shared data
A network utility that tracks packet transmission rates
A tool that monitors CPU temperature during execution
explanation: A monitor is a high-level synchronization construct that encapsulates shared data, procedures, and synchronization mechanisms, ensuring only one process can be active within the monitor at a time.

text: What is the compare-and-swap (CAS) atomic instruction?
options: It atomically compares a value and swaps it if it matches
It compares memory sizes and swaps active processes
It compares two files and swaps their disk locations
It compares CPU speeds and swaps scheduling algorithms
explanation: Compare-and-swap atomically compares the contents of a memory location with an expected value and, only if they match, modifies the contents to a new value, used for lock-free synchronization.

text: What is the difference between a spinlock and a blocking lock?
options: A spinlock busy-waits; a blocking lock puts the thread to sleep
Spinlocks are only used in user-space applications
A blocking lock busy-waits; a spinlock puts the thread to sleep
Both mechanisms are identical in their implementation
explanation: A spinlock continuously checks (busy-waits) for the lock to become available, wasting CPU cycles, while a blocking lock puts the waiting thread to sleep and wakes it when the lock is released.

text: What is a condition variable in a monitor?
options: A variable that holds the process priority assignment
A variable allowing a process to wait until a condition is met
A variable that counts the number of active processes
A variable that stores the current CPU utilization level
explanation: A condition variable allows a process inside a monitor to wait for a specific condition to become true. It supports wait() to block and signal() to wake a waiting process.

text: Why is disabling interrupts not a good solution for mutual exclusion on multiprocessors?
options: Disabling interrupts solves the problem perfectly always
Disabling interrupts is too slow for any practical use
Disabling interrupts on one CPU does not affect other CPUs
Interrupts cannot be disabled on modern CPU architectures
explanation: On multiprocessor systems, disabling interrupts on one CPU does not prevent processes on other CPUs from entering their critical sections, making it an insufficient solution for mutual exclusion.

text: What is the priority ceiling protocol in real-time synchronization?
options: All tasks are assigned the same priority to prevent inversion
Priority ceiling only applies to single-threaded programs
Tasks with the lowest priority always access resources first
A task inherits the highest priority ceiling of resources it holds
explanation: The priority ceiling protocol assigns each resource a ceiling equal to the highest priority of any task that may lock it. A task can only lock a resource if its priority is higher than all locked resource ceilings, preventing deadlock and priority inversion.

text: What is a read-copy-update (RCU) synchronization mechanism?
options: RCU requires all processes to stop during any update
Data is copied to disk before every read operation begins
All readers and writers must acquire exclusive locks always
Readers access data without locks; writers create updated copies
explanation: RCU allows readers to access shared data without acquiring locks by ensuring they see consistent snapshots. Writers create modified copies and atomically update pointers, deferring old data reclamation until all readers finish.

text: What is the ABA problem in lock-free programming?
options: A thread accesses memory address A and B simultaneously
A process acquires lock A then lock B causing deadlock
A value changes from A to B then back to A fooling CAS
Two processes named A and B conflict over a shared file
explanation: The ABA problem occurs when a CAS operation reads value A, another thread changes it to B then back to A, and the CAS succeeds even though the data was modified, potentially causing incorrect behavior.

text: What is a memory barrier (fence) instruction used for?
options: It enforces ordering of memory operations across processors
It prevents the CPU from accessing any memory addresses
It compresses memory contents to save physical space
It allocates a barrier of protected memory for a process
explanation: A memory barrier (fence) forces the CPU to complete all memory reads/writes before the barrier before executing any memory operations after it, preventing reordering that could break synchronization on modern out-of-order processors.

text: What is the difference between Mesa and Hoare monitor semantics?
options: Mesa uses while-loop checks; Hoare gives CPU to signaled thread
Mesa monitors do not support condition variables at all
Both semantics are identical in behavior and implementation
Hoare uses while-loop checks; Mesa gives CPU to signaled thread
explanation: In Hoare semantics, signal() immediately transfers control to the waiting thread. In Mesa semantics, signal() only moves the waiting thread to the ready queue, requiring it to recheck the condition in a while loop.

text: What is transactional memory as a synchronization approach?
options: Transactional memory replaces RAM with persistent storage
Each process has a separate memory bank for transactions
Memory is partitioned into transaction logs and data segments
Memory operations execute atomically as transactions without locks
explanation: Transactional memory allows a group of memory operations to execute atomically and in isolation (similar to database transactions), either committing all changes or rolling back, eliminating the need for explicit locks.

text: What is a futex in Linux synchronization?
options: A type of semaphore that only works in kernel space
A future execution context scheduled for later processing
A fast user-space mutex that avoids syscalls in uncontested cases
A file-based locking mechanism for networked file systems
explanation: A futex (fast user-space mutex) provides synchronization that operates entirely in user space when uncontested, only making a system call to the kernel when contention requires blocking, minimizing overhead.

text: What is the lock-free versus wait-free distinction in concurrent data structures?
options: Both terms mean exactly the same thing in practice
Lock-free uses locks internally; wait-free uses no synchronization
Wait-free guarantees system progress; lock-free guarantees per-thread
Lock-free guarantees system progress; wait-free guarantees per-thread
explanation: Lock-free algorithms guarantee that at least one thread makes progress in a finite number of steps (system-wide progress). Wait-free algorithms guarantee that every thread makes progress in a bounded number of steps (per-thread progress).

text: How does the ticket lock improve fairness over a simple test-and-set lock?
options: It assigns tickets to threads ensuring FIFO ordering of access
It randomly selects which thread enters the critical section
It eliminates the need for atomic hardware instructions
It allows multiple threads to hold the lock simultaneously
explanation: A ticket lock assigns incrementing ticket numbers to requesting threads and grants the lock in ticket order (FIFO), preventing starvation and ensuring fairness, unlike test-and-set which has no ordering guarantee.

text: What is the sequence lock (seqlock) mechanism in the Linux kernel?
options: Sequence locks prevent all writes until all reads complete
Readers and writers both acquire the same exclusive lock
Sequence locks only work for data stored on disk
Writers increment a sequence counter; readers retry on odd count
explanation: A seqlock uses a sequence counter incremented by writers before and after updates. Readers check the counter before and after reading; if it changed or is odd (write in progress), they retry, favoring writers over readers.

text: What is a deadlock in an operating system?
options: A process finishes before its allocated time expires
Processes wait for each other and none can proceed
A process executes at maximum CPU speed continuously
The operating system shuts down due to overheating
explanation: A deadlock is a situation where two or more processes are each waiting for a resource held by another, forming a circular wait so that none can proceed.

text: Which of the following is one of the four necessary conditions for deadlock?
options: Mutual exclusion of resources
Single-user operating system
Unlimited resource availability
Preemptive scheduling policy
explanation: Mutual exclusion is one of the four necessary conditions for deadlock. The others are hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait.

text: What is the hold and wait condition for deadlock?
options: A process waits without holding any resources at all
A process releases all resources before requesting new ones
A process holds resources while waiting for additional ones
A process completes execution before requesting resources
explanation: Hold and wait means a process is holding at least one resource and is waiting to acquire additional resources that are currently held by other processes.

text: What is a resource allocation graph?
options: A bar chart showing CPU utilization over time
A pie chart showing memory usage by each process
A directed graph showing resource assignments and requests
A line graph showing network bandwidth consumption
explanation: A resource allocation graph is a directed graph with vertices representing processes and resources, and edges showing assignments (resource to process) and requests (process to resource).

text: What is the circular wait condition for deadlock?
options: Processes release resources in a circular pattern
Only one process waits while all others are running
Processes form a circular chain each waiting for the next
Processes wait in a straight line for a single resource
explanation: Circular wait means there exists a set of waiting processes where each process is waiting for a resource held by the next process in the chain, forming a circular dependency.

text: What does the no preemption condition for deadlock mean?
options: Resources are always preempted when a new request comes
Processes voluntarily release resources when not needed
The CPU can always preempt any running process at will
Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a holding process
explanation: The no preemption condition means that resources already allocated to a process cannot be forcibly taken away; they must be released voluntarily by the process holding them.

text: What is deadlock prevention?
options: Allowing deadlock to occur and then recovering from it
Detecting deadlock after it has already occurred
Ensuring at least one necessary condition cannot hold
Ignoring the possibility of deadlock in the system
explanation: Deadlock prevention ensures that at least one of the four necessary conditions (mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait) cannot hold, making deadlock structurally impossible.

text: What is starvation in the context of resource allocation?
options: A process receives too many resources from the system
A process is indefinitely denied resources it needs
The system has more resources than processes need
All processes complete their execution successfully
explanation: Starvation occurs when a process is indefinitely denied access to resources it needs to proceed, even though the system is not in deadlock.

text: What is the safe state in deadlock avoidance?
options: A state where no processes are currently running
A state where all resources are allocated to one process
A state where a safe sequence of process execution exists
A state where all processes have completed execution
explanation: A system is in a safe state if there exists a safe sequence of all processes such that each process can obtain its needed resources from available resources plus those held by preceding processes.

text: What is livelock?
options: All processes are permanently blocked waiting for resources
A process runs indefinitely consuming all CPU resources
Two processes successfully complete without any conflict
Processes change state continuously but make no progress
explanation: Livelock occurs when processes continuously change their states in response to each other but make no actual progress, unlike deadlock where processes are completely blocked.

text: How does the Banker's Algorithm determine if a state is safe?
options: It prevents any process from requesting more than one resource
It allocates all resources to one process at a time only
It kills the process with the lowest priority to free resources
It checks if all processes can finish with available resources
explanation: The Banker's Algorithm simulates allocation by checking if there exists a safe sequence where each process can obtain its maximum needed resources from available resources plus those released by completed processes.

text: How can circular wait be prevented?
options: By letting processes request resources in any order
By removing all shared resources from the system
By allowing processes to hold unlimited resources
By imposing a total ordering on all resource types
explanation: Circular wait can be prevented by imposing a total ordering on resource types and requiring that each process requests resources in increasing order, breaking any possible circular chain.

text: What is the difference between deadlock prevention and deadlock avoidance?
options: Prevention constrains requests; avoidance checks each allocation
Both approaches are identical in method and result
Avoidance constrains requests; prevention checks each allocation
Prevention detects deadlock; avoidance recovers from it
explanation: Deadlock prevention restricts how resource requests can be made to ensure a necessary condition cannot hold. Deadlock avoidance dynamically checks each allocation request to ensure the system remains in a safe state.

text: How does deadlock detection work using a wait-for graph?
options: The graph shows only resource usage without deadlock info
A wait-for graph prevents deadlock from ever occurring
Wait-for graphs are used only for scheduling decisions
A cycle in the wait-for graph indicates a deadlock exists
explanation: In a wait-for graph (a simplified resource allocation graph for single-instance resources), a cycle indicates that a deadlock exists among the processes involved in the cycle.

text: What is the difference between safe and unsafe states?
options: Unsafe states guarantee deadlock; safe states allow deadlock
Safe states guarantee no deadlock; unsafe may lead to deadlock
Both safe and unsafe states can always avoid deadlock
Safe and unsafe states are identical in their behavior
explanation: A safe state guarantees that deadlock cannot occur because a safe sequence exists. An unsafe state does not guarantee deadlock but means the system cannot prevent deadlock from possibly occurring.

text: How can hold and wait be prevented?
options: Require processes to request all resources before starting
Grant resources based on random selection of processes
Allow processes to hold resources while requesting more
Let processes request resources one at a time in any order
explanation: Hold and wait can be prevented by requiring each process to request and receive all its resources before execution begins, or by requiring it to release all held resources before requesting new ones.

text: What recovery methods exist after deadlock is detected?
options: Ignoring the deadlock and hoping it resolves itself
Restarting the entire operating system from scratch
Adding more physical resources to the computer system
Process termination or resource preemption from processes
explanation: After deadlock detection, recovery can be done by terminating deadlocked processes (all at once or one at a time) or by preempting resources from some processes and giving them to others to break the cycle.

text: What is the ostrich algorithm for handling deadlock?
options: Detect deadlock and terminate all affected processes
Avoid deadlock by running the Banker's Algorithm always
Ignore the problem assuming deadlock is rare in practice
Prevent deadlock by restricting resource allocation rules
explanation: The ostrich algorithm ignores the deadlock problem entirely, assuming it occurs so rarely that the overhead of prevention, avoidance, or detection is not justified. Most general-purpose OSes use this approach.

text: What are the Need, Max, and Allocation matrices in the Banker's Algorithm?
options: All three matrices contain identical values for each process
Need is total need; Max is held; Allocation is remaining need
Max is total need; Allocation is held; Need is Max minus Allocation
Allocation is total need; Need is held; Max is remaining need
explanation: In the Banker's Algorithm, Max defines the maximum resources a process may request, Allocation is the resources currently allocated, and Need = Max - Allocation represents the remaining resources needed.

text: What is the problem with deadlock prevention through eliminating mutual exclusion?
options: Some resources are inherently non-sharable like printers
Mutual exclusion can always be eliminated for all resources
Eliminating mutual exclusion speeds up all processes
Mutual exclusion is not a necessary condition for deadlock
explanation: Eliminating mutual exclusion is impractical because some resources (like printers and writable files) are inherently non-sharable and must be accessed exclusively by one process at a time.

text: What is the time complexity of the Banker's Algorithm safety check?
options: O(n) where n is the number of processes in the system
O(m * n^2) where m is resource types and n is processes
O(1) since it uses a constant-time lookup table
O(n * log n) using a priority queue implementation
explanation: The Banker's Algorithm safety check has O(m * n^2) complexity because it must iterate through up to n processes (in the worst case n times) comparing their need vectors of size m against available resources.

text: How does deadlock detection differ for single-instance versus multiple-instance resources?
options: Single-instance resources can never be involved in deadlock
Both use identical algorithms regardless of instance count
Single uses the Banker's Algorithm; multiple uses wait-for graph
Single uses wait-for graph; multiple uses detection algorithm
explanation: For single-instance resources, deadlock can be detected by finding a cycle in the wait-for graph. For multiple-instance resources, a more complex detection algorithm similar to the Banker's Algorithm is needed.

text: Why is the Banker's Algorithm impractical for large-scale systems?
options: It does not support any form of resource preemption
It only works with exactly two processes in the system
It requires exclusive access to all system resources
It requires advance knowledge of maximum resource needs
explanation: The Banker's Algorithm requires each process to declare its maximum resource needs in advance, which is often impractical. It also has significant computational overhead and assumes a fixed number of resources.

text: What is the relationship between a resource allocation graph and deadlock in multi-instance resources?
options: A cycle is necessary but not sufficient for deadlock
Resource allocation graphs cannot represent multi-instance
A cycle is both necessary and sufficient for deadlock
A cycle means deadlock can never occur in the system
explanation: With multi-instance resources, a cycle in the resource allocation graph is necessary but not sufficient for deadlock. Deadlock requires a knot where no process in the cycle can be satisfied.

text: How does victim selection work in deadlock recovery through process termination?
options: The process with minimum cost based on priority and resources used
The process with the highest priority is always terminated first
Processes are terminated in alphabetical order of their names
All deadlocked processes are always terminated simultaneously
explanation: Victim selection considers factors like process priority, how long it has run, resources used, resources needed to complete, and the number of processes that need to be terminated to break the cycle.

text: What is the problem of rollback in deadlock recovery by preemption?
options: Determining a safe state to roll back to is complex
Rollback can only be applied to a single process at once
Rollback always restores the process to its initial state
Rollback eliminates the need for checkpointing entirely
explanation: When a resource is preempted from a process, the process must be rolled back to a safe state where it can restart. Determining the minimal safe rollback point is complex and may require periodic checkpointing.

text: How do distributed deadlocks differ from single-system deadlocks?
options: They are handled identically to single-system deadlocks
They span multiple machines making detection much harder
They cannot occur in distributed computing systems ever
They are easier to detect than single-system deadlocks
explanation: Distributed deadlocks span multiple machines with no shared memory, making detection much harder due to communication delays, partial views of system state, and the possibility of phantom deadlocks.

text: What is a two-phase locking protocol in deadlock prevention?
options: Locks are acquired and released randomly in any order
Two processes share the same lock for mutual access
Only two resources can be locked at any given time
First phase acquires all locks; second phase releases them
explanation: In two-phase locking, the first (growing) phase acquires all needed locks without releasing any, and the second (shrinking) phase releases locks without acquiring new ones, preventing circular wait.

text: How does the wound-wait scheme handle deadlock in database systems?
options: Younger transactions always have higher priority access
All transactions are treated equally regardless of age
Older transactions wound younger ones; younger ones wait
Wound-wait eliminates the need for transaction timestamps
explanation: In wound-wait, if an older transaction requests a resource held by a younger one, the younger is wounded (rolled back). If a younger transaction requests from an older one, it waits, preventing circular wait.

text: What is the wait-die scheme and how does it differ from wound-wait?
options: Older transactions wait; younger ones die and restart later
Both transactions always die and restart from the beginning
Older transactions die; younger ones wait for resources
Wait-die and wound-wait are identical in their behavior
explanation: In wait-die, an older transaction waits for a younger one's resource, but a younger transaction requesting an older one's resource dies (is rolled back and restarted). This is the opposite direction of wound-wait.

text: What is logical (virtual) address?
options: The address of a peripheral device controller
An address generated by the CPU during execution
The actual physical location in main memory
The address of a file stored on the hard disk
explanation: A logical (virtual) address is generated by the CPU during program execution and is mapped to a physical address by the memory management unit (MMU) before accessing main memory.

text: What is paging in memory management?
options: Dividing memory into fixed-size frames and processes into pages
Storing all processes contiguously in main memory only
Dividing memory into variable-size segments for each process
Moving entire processes between main memory and disk
explanation: Paging divides physical memory into fixed-size blocks called frames and logical memory into same-size blocks called pages, eliminating external fragmentation.

text: What is a page table?
options: A table showing network routing information
A table mapping logical page numbers to physical frames
A table listing all files stored on the hard disk
A table of CPU scheduling priorities for processes
explanation: A page table is a per-process data structure that maps each logical page number to the corresponding physical frame number in main memory, enabling address translation.

text: What is a page fault?
options: A situation where all memory pages are free and empty
An error in the page table data structure itself
A hardware failure in the memory management unit
An event when a referenced page is not in physical memory
explanation: A page fault occurs when a process references a page that is not currently loaded in physical memory, triggering the OS to load the page from disk into a free frame.

text: What is segmentation in memory management?
options: Dividing memory into equal-size fixed physical frames
Encrypting memory contents for security purposes
Dividing a process into variable-size logical segments
Combining all processes into a single memory block
explanation: Segmentation divides a process's logical address space into variable-size segments based on logical divisions like code, data, and stack, each with its own base address and length.

text: What is internal fragmentation?
options: Free memory scattered between allocated blocks externally
Memory used by the operating system kernel itself
The total amount of memory available in the system
Wasted memory inside an allocated block that is unused
explanation: Internal fragmentation is the wasted space inside an allocated memory block when the allocated block is larger than the requested size, common in fixed-size allocation schemes like paging.

text: What is external fragmentation?
options: Wasted space inside a single allocated memory block
Free memory exists but is scattered in small non-contiguous blocks
Extra memory added to the system through expansion
Memory that is permanently damaged and unusable
explanation: External fragmentation occurs when enough total free memory exists to satisfy a request, but it is scattered in small non-contiguous blocks, making it unusable for large allocations.

text: What is the base register used for in memory management?
options: It stores the total amount of free memory available
It stores the starting physical address of a process
It stores the number of active processes in the system
It stores the CPU clock speed for timing operations
explanation: The base register holds the smallest legal physical memory address for a process, used with the limit register to define the range of addresses a process can access.

text: What is virtual memory?
options: A backup copy of data stored on an external drive
The total physical RAM installed in the computer system
A technique allowing execution of processes not fully in memory
A type of cache memory located inside the CPU chip
explanation: Virtual memory is a technique that allows the execution of processes that are not completely in physical memory, using disk space as an extension of RAM to provide a larger apparent memory.

text: What is the LRU page replacement algorithm?
options: It replaces the page that has not been used for the longest time
It replaces pages in the order they were loaded
It replaces the most recently used page in memory
It replaces a randomly selected page from memory
explanation: The Least Recently Used (LRU) algorithm replaces the page that has not been referenced for the longest period of time, based on the principle that recently used pages are likely to be used again soon.

text: What is the Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)?
options: A register storing the current instruction being decoded
A buffer for storing data being written to the disk
A fast cache storing recent page-to-frame translations
A queue holding processes waiting for memory allocation
explanation: The TLB is a small, fast hardware cache in the MMU that stores recently used page table entries, allowing address translation without accessing the page table in memory for every reference.

text: What is thrashing in virtual memory systems?
options: A process writes data to disk faster than it can read
A process spends more time paging than actually executing
A process runs extremely fast consuming all CPU time
A process allocates more memory than physically available
explanation: Thrashing occurs when a process has too few frames and constantly page faults, spending more time swapping pages in and out of memory than executing instructions, severely degrading performance.

text: How does the FIFO page replacement algorithm work?
options: It replaces the page with the highest access frequency
It replaces the page with the fewest access references
It replaces the page that was most recently loaded
It replaces the page that was loaded into memory first
explanation: The FIFO page replacement algorithm replaces the oldest page in memory (the one that was loaded first), using a simple queue to track the order in which pages were brought in.

text: What is Belady's anomaly?
options: Fewer frames always cause more page faults with any algorithm
More frames can cause more page faults with FIFO replacement
The optimal algorithm always produces the fewest page faults
LRU algorithm always outperforms FIFO in all workloads
explanation: Belady's anomaly is the counterintuitive phenomenon where increasing the number of frames can increase the number of page faults for certain page reference strings with the FIFO replacement algorithm.

text: What is the optimal page replacement algorithm?
options: It replaces the page that has been in memory the longest
It replaces the page that will not be used for the longest time
It replaces the page that was most recently used by the CPU
It replaces the page that was accessed the most times
explanation: The optimal (OPT) algorithm replaces the page that will not be used for the longest period in the future. It produces the lowest possible page fault rate but requires future knowledge, making it impractical.

text: What is the working set model?
options: The total set of all pages in the physical memory
The set of processes currently in the ready queue
The set of files that a process has open for access
The set of pages a process actively uses in a time window
explanation: The working set model defines the set of pages that a process is actively using within a recent time window. Allocating enough frames for the working set prevents thrashing.

text: What is the difference between contiguous and non-contiguous memory allocation?
options: Both methods always place processes in contiguous blocks
Non-contiguous places process in one block; contiguous scatters
Contiguous places process in one block; non-contiguous can scatter
Contiguous allocation uses paging; non-contiguous uses swapping
explanation: Contiguous allocation places each process in a single continuous block of memory, while non-contiguous allocation (like paging) allows a process's memory to be scattered across different locations.

text: How does the clock (second chance) page replacement algorithm work?
options: It replaces pages based on the system clock time only
It always replaces the page that was accessed most recently
It uses a reference bit and gives pages a second chance before replacing
It replaces pages in reverse order of their loading time
explanation: The clock algorithm uses a circular queue with reference bits. When seeking a victim, it clears reference bits and skips pages with set bits (giving them a second chance), replacing the first page with a cleared bit.

text: What is the purpose of a multi-level page table?
options: It reduces page table memory by paging the page table itself
It stores multiple processes in a single page table
It eliminates the need for a TLB in the system
It increases the speed of page fault handling operations
explanation: A multi-level page table reduces memory overhead by splitting the page table into levels, only allocating inner page table entries for address ranges actually in use, avoiding a large contiguous page table.

text: What is the purpose of the dirty (modified) bit in a page table entry?
options: It indicates whether the page has been modified since loading
It indicates whether the page belongs to the kernel or user
It indicates whether the page is read-only or writable
It indicates whether the page is currently in physical memory
explanation: The dirty bit indicates whether a page has been modified since it was loaded into memory. When replacing a dirty page, it must be written back to disk; a clean page can simply be discarded.

text: How does the inverted page table reduce memory usage?
options: It has one entry per physical frame instead of per logical page
It compresses page table entries using data compression
It shares a single page table among all system processes
It stores page tables on disk instead of in main memory
explanation: An inverted page table has one entry for each physical frame (instead of one per logical page per process), dramatically reducing memory overhead for systems with large address spaces and many processes.

text: Given a logical address space of 2^32 bytes and a page size of 4 KB, how many entries are in the page table?
options: 2^32 entries in the page table
2^12 entries in the page table
2^10 entries in the page table
2^20 entries in the page table
explanation: With a 2^32 byte address space and 4 KB (2^12 byte) pages, the number of pages (and page table entries) is 2^32 / 2^12 = 2^20 = 1,048,576 entries.

text: What is the effective memory access time with TLB hit ratio of 90%, TLB access 10ns, and memory access 100ns?
options: 110 nanoseconds effective access time
200 nanoseconds effective access time
120 nanoseconds effective access time
100 nanoseconds effective access time
explanation: Effective access = 0.9*(10+100) + 0.1*(10+100+100) = 0.9*110 + 0.1*210 = 99 + 21 = 120 ns. TLB hit requires TLB + memory access; TLB miss requires TLB + page table + memory access.

text: What is the page fault frequency (PFF) approach to frame allocation?
options: It removes all frames from a process when a fault occurs
It prevents page faults by loading all pages at startup
It allocates a fixed number of frames to every process equally
It adjusts frames based on the actual page fault rate per process
explanation: The PFF approach monitors each process's page fault rate. If the rate exceeds an upper threshold, more frames are allocated; if it falls below a lower threshold, frames are reclaimed, directly controlling thrashing.

text: What is memory-mapped I/O and how does it differ from port-mapped I/O?
options: Memory-mapped I/O stores device data directly on the hard disk
All I/O operations are performed through dedicated I/O instructions
Devices are accessed through memory addresses instead of I/O ports
Port-mapped I/O uses memory addresses to access all devices
explanation: Memory-mapped I/O maps device registers to memory addresses, allowing standard load/store instructions to control devices. Port-mapped I/O uses separate I/O address space accessed through special instructions.

text: How do huge pages (large pages) improve TLB performance?
options: They reduce the physical size of the TLB chip
They reduce TLB misses by covering more memory per entry
They increase the number of entries in the TLB hardware
They eliminate the need for page tables entirely
explanation: Huge pages (e.g., 2 MB or 1 GB instead of 4 KB) allow each TLB entry to cover more physical memory, dramatically reducing TLB misses for applications with large memory footprints.

text: What is kernel address space layout randomization (KASLR)?
options: It arranges kernel memory in a fixed predictable layout
It compresses the kernel to reduce memory consumption
It randomizes kernel memory layout to prevent exploitation
It removes the kernel from memory during user execution
explanation: KASLR randomizes the base address of the kernel in memory at boot time, making it harder for attackers to predict kernel code and data locations, mitigating return-oriented programming (ROP) attacks.

text: What is the slab allocator in the Linux kernel?
options: It replaces the buddy system for all memory allocations
It pre-allocates object caches for frequently used kernel structures
It allocates memory pages one at a time using a free list
It is used only for user-space memory allocation requests
explanation: The slab allocator groups frequently allocated kernel objects into caches of pre-initialized objects (slabs), reducing allocation overhead and internal fragmentation by reusing objects without reinitializing them.

text: How does the buddy system allocate memory?
options: It uses a linked list of arbitrarily sized free blocks
It splits and merges power-of-two sized blocks recursively
It assigns one contiguous block to each process on creation
It allocates memory in fixed-size chunks regardless of request
explanation: The buddy system allocates memory in power-of-two sized blocks. When a block is too large, it splits into two equal buddies. When both buddies are freed, they merge back into a larger block, reducing external fragmentation.

text: What is the NUMA-aware memory allocation policy in Linux?
options: It stores all kernel data on a single dedicated NUMA node
It distributes all memory equally across all NUMA nodes
It ignores NUMA topology and uses a single global allocator
It allocates memory from the node closest to the requesting CPU
explanation: Linux's NUMA-aware allocation preferentially allocates memory from the NUMA node closest to the requesting CPU (local allocation) to minimize memory access latency, with policies like first-touch and interleave.

text: What is a file in an operating system?
options: A named collection of related data stored on secondary storage
A hardware device connected to the computer system
A temporary variable stored in a CPU register
A running program currently loaded in main memory
explanation: A file is a named collection of related information recorded on secondary storage, treated as a logical unit of storage by the operating system.

text: What is a directory in a file system?
options: A network protocol for transferring files remotely
A hardware controller for managing disk operations
A structure that organizes and provides information about files
A type of executable program that runs on startup
explanation: A directory is a file system structure that contains entries mapping file names to their metadata and locations, organizing files into a hierarchical structure.

text: What is sequential file access?
options: Reading multiple files simultaneously in parallel
Accessing file data in reverse order from end to start
Accessing any position in the file directly by offset
Reading file data in order from beginning to end
explanation: Sequential access reads data from a file in order, one record after another, which is the simplest access method and works like reading a tape.

text: What is the purpose of a file extension?
options: It indicates the type or format of the file contents
It specifies which CPU core should process the file
It determines the physical size of the file on disk
It controls the access permissions of the file
explanation: A file extension (e.g., .txt, .pdf, .exe) indicates the type or format of the file's contents, helping the OS and applications determine how to interpret and handle the file.

text: What is an inode in UNIX file systems?
options: A data structure storing file metadata and block pointers
A physical sector on the disk surface where data is stored
A network node that stores shared files for users
A type of directory that contains only subdirectories
explanation: An inode (index node) in UNIX file systems is a data structure that stores a file's metadata (permissions, size, timestamps) and pointers to the data blocks containing the file's contents.

text: What is the difference between a hard link and a soft link?
options: Hard links share the same inode; soft links point to a path
Both types of links are identical in implementation
Hard links work across file systems; soft links do not
Soft links share the same inode; hard links point to a path
explanation: A hard link is an additional directory entry pointing to the same inode, while a soft (symbolic) link is a separate file containing the pathname of the target file.

text: What file allocation method stores files in consecutive disk blocks?
options: Indexed allocation method
Distributed allocation method
Contiguous allocation method
Linked allocation method
explanation: Contiguous allocation stores each file in a set of consecutive disk blocks, providing fast sequential and direct access but suffering from external fragmentation.

text: What is the root directory in a file system?
options: The last directory created in the file system hierarchy
The top-level directory from which all other directories branch
A directory that contains only system configuration files
A directory that can only be accessed by the root user
explanation: The root directory is the top-level directory in a hierarchical file system, serving as the starting point from which all other files and directories are organized.

text: What are file access permissions in UNIX?
options: Only read and write permissions for the file owner
Permissions that control CPU scheduling priority
Permissions that apply only to directories, not files
Read, write, and execute permissions for owner, group, others
explanation: UNIX file permissions define read (r), write (w), and execute (x) access rights for three categories: the file owner, the group, and all other users.

text: What is direct (random) file access?
options: Accessing files only through the directory structure
Accessing any position in a file directly by its offset
Reading the file only from the beginning to the end
Reading file data in reverse order from end to start
explanation: Direct (random) access allows reading or writing data at any position in a file by specifying a byte offset, without needing to read all preceding data.

text: How does linked allocation solve the external fragmentation problem?
options: An index block holds pointers to all file data blocks
All blocks for a file are stored contiguously on disk
Files are split into fixed-size segments stored sequentially
Each block contains a pointer to the next block in the file
explanation: Linked allocation eliminates external fragmentation by storing each file as a linked list of disk blocks, where each block contains a pointer to the next, allowing blocks to be scattered anywhere on disk.

text: What is the File Allocation Table (FAT) file system?
options: A file system that stores all files in contiguous blocks
A file system that does not support directories at all
A file system designed exclusively for network storage
A file system using a table to track block allocation chains
explanation: FAT uses a table at the beginning of the disk volume where each entry corresponds to a disk block and contains the number of the next block in the file chain, supporting linked allocation efficiently.

text: How does indexed allocation work for file storage?
options: Files are stored without any block tracking mechanism
All data blocks are stored in sequential disk positions
An index block contains pointers to all data blocks of a file
Each data block contains a pointer to the next data block
explanation: Indexed allocation stores all pointers to a file's data blocks in a single index block, supporting direct access to any block without traversing a linked list.

text: What is a journaling file system?
options: It journals network traffic for security monitoring
It stores a diary of all files accessed by each user
It logs changes before committing them to prevent corruption
It only allows sequential writing to the disk surface
explanation: A journaling file system (e.g., ext4, NTFS) logs pending changes in a journal before writing them to the main file system, enabling quick recovery after crashes by replaying or undoing incomplete operations.

text: What is the free space management method using a bitmap?
options: A counter stores the total number of free blocks only
Free blocks are linked together in a chain on the disk
Free space is managed by keeping all free blocks contiguous
Each bit represents a block with 0 for free and 1 for allocated
explanation: A bitmap (or bit vector) uses one bit per disk block to indicate whether it is free (0) or allocated (1), providing an efficient way to find free blocks and track disk space usage.

text: What is a virtual file system (VFS)?
options: An abstraction layer allowing uniform access to different file systems
A file system used only for virtual machine disk images
A temporary file system that is erased on system shutdown
A file system that exists entirely in virtual memory
explanation: A VFS provides a uniform interface (API) between the kernel and different file system implementations, allowing user programs to access ext4, NTFS, FAT, and other file systems through the same system calls.

text: What is the difference between a single-level and two-level directory structure?
options: Two-level has one directory; single-level has per-user directories
Single-level supports subdirectories; two-level does not
Both structures organize files in exactly the same way
Single-level has one directory; two-level has per-user directories
explanation: A single-level directory has all files in one directory, causing naming conflicts. A two-level directory gives each user their own directory, solving naming conflicts but not allowing file grouping.

text: What is mounting in file systems?
options: Copying all files from one disk to another disk
Removing a file system from the operating system permanently
Formatting a disk to create a new empty file system
Attaching a file system to a directory in the existing tree
explanation: Mounting is the process of making a file system accessible by attaching it to a specific directory (mount point) in the existing directory tree, allowing access to its files through that directory.

text: What is the purpose of the superblock in a UNIX file system?
options: It contains the actual data contents of all stored files
It stores metadata about the file system layout and structure
It holds the user authentication database for login
It manages the CPU scheduling queue for processes
explanation: The superblock stores critical file system metadata including the total number of blocks, inode count, block size, free block count, and other information about the file system layout.

text: What is the advantage of an acyclic graph directory structure?
options: It allows files to be shared between different directories
It eliminates the need for file access permissions
It prevents any form of file sharing between users
It stores all files in a single flat directory only
explanation: An acyclic graph directory structure allows files and subdirectories to be shared between different directories through links, enabling efficient sharing while preventing cycles that could cause infinite loops.

text: How does the ext4 file system handle large files using extent trees?
options: Ext4 uses only indirect block pointers for all file sizes
Extents map contiguous ranges of blocks reducing metadata overhead
Ext4 stores all files in a single contiguous disk region
Extent trees are only used for directory metadata storage
explanation: Ext4 uses extent trees where each extent maps a contiguous range of logical blocks to physical blocks, significantly reducing metadata overhead compared to traditional indirect block pointers for large files.

text: What is a log-structured file system?
options: All writes go to a sequential log and data is garbage collected
Files are structured as database logs with SQL queries
Log files are the only type of file the system supports
The file system logs only error messages for debugging
explanation: A log-structured file system writes all data and metadata sequentially to a log, optimizing write performance. A garbage collector reclaims space from old log segments, making it efficient for write-heavy workloads.

text: What is the B-tree structure used for in file systems like NTFS and HFS+?
options: It stores the actual file data contents on the disk
It encrypts file names to prevent unauthorized access
It manages CPU scheduling for file system operations
It efficiently indexes directory entries and file metadata
explanation: B-trees (and B+ trees) in file systems like NTFS and HFS+ efficiently index directory entries and metadata, providing O(log n) lookup, insertion, and deletion for large directories.

text: What is copy-on-write (COW) in file systems like ZFS and Btrfs?
options: Modified data is written to new locations preserving originals
Copy-on-write prevents any modification of existing files
Files are copied to backup before any read operation
Data is copied between disks on every write operation
explanation: COW file systems never overwrite data in place; modified blocks are written to new locations and metadata pointers are updated atomically, providing snapshot capability and crash consistency.

text: How does NTFS implement file system security through Access Control Lists?
options: ACLs in NTFS apply only to directories, never to files
NTFS uses only UNIX-style permission bits for access control
NTFS does not support any form of file access control
Each file has a security descriptor with DACLs and SACLs
explanation: NTFS assigns each file a security descriptor containing a Discretionary ACL (DACL) listing allowed/denied permissions per user/group, and a System ACL (SACL) for auditing, providing fine-grained access control.

text: What is the TRIM command and why is it important for SSDs?
options: It reduces the read speed of the SSD to extend its life
It tells the SSD which blocks are no longer in use for cleanup
It trims the physical size of the SSD to save space
It is a command for formatting traditional magnetic disks
explanation: TRIM informs the SSD controller which blocks are no longer in use by the file system, allowing the SSD to erase them proactively during idle time and maintain write performance by having pre-erased blocks ready.

text: What is the NFS (Network File System) protocol?
options: A compression algorithm for reducing file sizes on disk
A local file system format for formatting USB drives
A protocol for sending email attachments between servers
A protocol allowing remote file access transparently over a network
explanation: NFS is a distributed file system protocol that allows clients to access files on a remote server as if they were local, using RPCs (Remote Procedure Calls) for transparent network file access.

text: What are indirect block pointers in UNIX file systems?
options: Blocks that store the file's name and access permissions
Pointers that directly reference the file's first data block
Blocks that point to other blocks containing data block addresses
Pointers used exclusively for directory file management
explanation: Indirect blocks contain pointers to data blocks (single indirect), pointers to other indirect blocks (double indirect), or three levels deep (triple indirect), enabling UNIX inodes to address very large files.

text: What is write-ahead logging (WAL) in journaling file systems?
options: Write-ahead logging is used only for read-only file systems
Changes are logged to the journal before modifying file system data
Data is written to the file system before being logged
Logs are written only after the system shuts down cleanly
explanation: WAL requires that all changes are written to the journal (log) before being applied to the actual file system structures, ensuring that incomplete transactions can be replayed or rolled back after a crash.

text: How does deduplication work in modern file systems?
options: Each file is always stored as a separate unique copy on disk
Deduplication encrypts files to reduce their storage size
Files are compressed using lossy algorithms to save space
Identical data blocks are stored once and referenced by pointers
explanation: Data deduplication identifies duplicate data blocks using content hashing and stores only one copy, replacing duplicates with references to the shared copy, significantly reducing storage consumption.

text: What is a hard disk drive (HDD)?
options: A type of optical disc used for storing video content
A magnetic storage device with spinning platters and read heads
A solid-state storage device with no moving parts
A volatile memory chip that loses data when powered off
explanation: A hard disk drive is a non-volatile magnetic storage device that uses spinning platters coated with magnetic material and read/write heads to store and retrieve data.

text: What is seek time in disk scheduling?
options: The time to transfer data from the disk to memory
The time for the desired sector to rotate under the head
The time for the disk head to move to the desired track
The time to decode data read from the disk surface
explanation: Seek time is the time required for the disk arm to move the read/write head to the track containing the desired sector, and is typically the largest component of disk access time.

text: What is rotational latency?
options: The time to transfer data between disk and main memory
The time for the disk head to move to the correct track
The time for the disk motor to reach full rotation speed
The time for the desired sector to rotate under the disk head
explanation: Rotational latency is the time it takes for the desired sector to rotate under the read/write head after the head has been positioned on the correct track.

text: What is the FCFS disk scheduling algorithm?
options: The request with the largest seek distance is served first
The nearest request to the current head position is served
Requests are served based on their assigned priority
Requests are served in the order they arrive in the queue
explanation: FCFS (First-Come, First-Served) disk scheduling processes disk I/O requests in the order they arrive, which is fair but can result in large head movements and poor performance.

text: What is a solid-state drive (SSD)?
options: An optical disc drive that reads CDs and DVDs
A storage device using spinning magnetic platters
A volatile memory module that needs constant power
A storage device using flash memory with no moving parts
explanation: A solid-state drive uses flash memory chips to store data with no moving mechanical parts, providing faster access times, lower latency, and better shock resistance than traditional HDDs.

text: What is RAID in storage systems?
options: Random Access In Disk for faster data retrieval
Read Access Indexed Directory for file management
Redundant Array of Independent Disks for reliability and speed
Rapid Application Integration Device for network storage
explanation: RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks) combines multiple physical disks into a logical unit to improve performance, reliability, or both through techniques like striping, mirroring, and parity.

text: What is disk formatting?
options: Encrypting the disk contents for security purposes
Reading all data stored on the disk into main memory
Preparing a disk for use by creating the file system structures
Defragmenting files to improve read and write speed
explanation: Disk formatting prepares a storage device for use by creating the file system structures needed to organize and store data, including partition tables, boot sectors, and file allocation tables.

text: What is a disk partition?
options: A logical division of a physical disk into separate sections
A compression method for reducing disk storage usage
A backup copy of the disk stored on another device
A physical separation of the disk platters inside the drive
explanation: A disk partition is a logical division of a physical disk into separate sections, each of which can be formatted with its own file system and treated as an independent storage unit.

text: What is the SSTF disk scheduling algorithm?
options: It serves the request closest to the current head position
It always moves the head to the outermost track first
It serves requests in the order they arrive in the queue
It serves the request farthest from the current position
explanation: SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) selects the disk I/O request with the minimum seek time from the current head position, reducing total head movement but potentially causing starvation of distant requests.

text: What is a swap space?
options: A dedicated area for storing the operating system kernel
A space for temporarily storing deleted files for recovery
A disk area used as virtual memory extension for RAM
A buffer space for network data packet processing
explanation: Swap space is an area on a disk used by the operating system as an extension of physical RAM for virtual memory, allowing processes to use more memory than is physically available.

text: How does the SCAN (elevator) disk scheduling algorithm work?
options: The head jumps randomly between tracks to serve requests
The head always starts from track zero for every request
The head serves all requests at even tracks before odd ones
The head moves in one direction serving requests then reverses
explanation: The SCAN algorithm moves the disk head in one direction (toward higher or lower tracks) serving all requests along the way, then reverses direction, similar to an elevator in a building.

text: What is the C-SCAN disk scheduling algorithm?
options: The head processes all requests at the innermost track first
The head alternates direction after serving each request
The head moves to the closest request like SSTF algorithm
The head serves requests in one direction then returns to start
explanation: C-SCAN (Circular SCAN) moves the head in one direction serving requests, then returns to the beginning of the disk without servicing requests on the return trip, providing more uniform wait times.

text: What is the difference between RAID 0 and RAID 1?
options: RAID 0 uses parity; RAID 1 uses striping without parity
RAID 0 stripes data for speed; RAID 1 mirrors for redundancy
Both RAID levels provide identical performance and redundancy
RAID 1 stripes data for speed; RAID 0 mirrors for redundancy
explanation: RAID 0 stripes data across disks for improved performance but no redundancy (any disk failure loses data). RAID 1 mirrors data across disks for redundancy but with higher storage cost.

text: What is RAID 5 and how does it provide fault tolerance?
options: It mirrors data across exactly two disk drives
It stores three copies of all data across the array
It uses block-level striping with distributed parity
It stripes data with no parity or redundancy at all
explanation: RAID 5 uses block-level striping with parity distributed across all disks in the array. It can tolerate the failure of one disk by reconstructing the lost data from the parity information.

text: Why does SSTF disk scheduling cause starvation?
options: SSTF always moves the head to the farthest request first
SSTF processes requests in strict arrival order always
SSTF serves all requests equally without any discrimination
Requests far from the head may never be served if closer ones arrive
explanation: SSTF can cause starvation because requests far from the current head position may never be served if new closer requests keep arriving, continually pushing distant requests to the back.

text: What is the difference between SCAN and LOOK disk scheduling?
options: SCAN reverses at the last request; LOOK goes to disk end
LOOK does not service any requests during head movement
Both algorithms behave identically in all situations
LOOK reverses at the last request; SCAN goes to disk end
explanation: SCAN moves the head to the end of the disk before reversing, while LOOK reverses direction when there are no more requests in the current direction, avoiding unnecessary head movement.

text: What is wear leveling in SSDs?
options: Wearing out specific cells faster for planned replacement
Distributing writes evenly across flash cells to extend life
Leveling the physical surface of the storage medium
Reducing the read speed to minimize component wear
explanation: Wear leveling distributes write and erase operations evenly across all flash memory cells in an SSD, preventing premature failure of heavily used cells and extending the overall drive lifespan.

text: What is the purpose of disk defragmentation?
options: Encrypting all files on the disk for security
Increasing the total storage capacity of the disk
Deleting unnecessary temporary files from the disk
Rearranging fragmented file data into contiguous blocks
explanation: Disk defragmentation reorganizes fragmented data on an HDD by moving file fragments into contiguous blocks, reducing seek time and improving read performance.

text: What is the boot block in disk organization?
options: A special block containing code to bootstrap the operating system
A damaged block that has been marked as unusable
A block that prevents unauthorized access to the disk
A block that stores the disk manufacturer information
explanation: The boot block is a designated area on disk containing the bootstrap loader code that is read by the system firmware during startup to begin loading the operating system into memory.

text: What is the difference between a GPT and MBR partition table?
options: MBR supports larger disks and more partitions than GPT
Both partition table formats are identical in capability
GPT only works on SSDs while MBR only works on HDDs
GPT supports larger disks and more partitions than MBR
explanation: GPT (GUID Partition Table) supports disks larger than 2 TB and up to 128 partitions, while MBR (Master Boot Record) is limited to 2 TB disks and 4 primary partitions.

text: How does the Flash Translation Layer (FTL) work in SSDs?
options: It translates network protocols into disk commands
It converts analog signals from the disk into digital data
It translates user file names into inode numbers
It maps logical block addresses to physical NAND flash pages
explanation: The FTL maintains a mapping table from logical block addresses (used by the OS) to physical NAND flash page locations, handling wear leveling, garbage collection, and the write-erase asymmetry of flash memory.

text: What is the write amplification problem in SSDs?
options: Write operations become faster than read operations
Data is written to multiple SSDs simultaneously for backup
Write operations are amplified to increase volume level
More physical data is written than the logical data requested
explanation: Write amplification occurs when the actual amount of physical data written to flash is larger than the logical data, caused by garbage collection moving valid pages during block erases, reducing SSD endurance.

text: What is RAID 6 and how does it differ from RAID 5?
options: RAID 6 stripes data without any parity for maximum speed
RAID 6 uses double distributed parity tolerating two disk failures
RAID 6 mirrors data on exactly two disks like RAID 1
RAID 6 uses single parity like RAID 5 with no difference
explanation: RAID 6 extends RAID 5 by using two independent parity calculations distributed across all disks, allowing the array to survive the simultaneous failure of any two disks.

text: What is the NVMe protocol and why is it faster than SATA?
options: NVMe reduces storage capacity to achieve faster speeds
NVMe uses the same interface as SATA with identical speed
NVMe uses PCIe lanes with deeper queues for parallel I/O
NVMe is a magnetic storage protocol for traditional HDDs
explanation: NVMe (Non-Volatile Memory Express) connects directly to the PCIe bus with up to 65535 I/O queues each 65536 entries deep, achieving much lower latency and higher throughput than SATA's single queue of 32 entries.

text: What is RAID 10 and what are its characteristics?
options: It uses ten disks with single parity for fault tolerance
It stripes data across ten identical disk drives without parity
It mirrors data across ten drives with no performance gain
It combines mirroring and striping for speed and redundancy
explanation: RAID 10 (1+0) creates mirrored pairs (RAID 1) and then stripes across them (RAID 0), providing both the redundancy of mirroring and the performance of striping, requiring at least 4 disks.

text: How does garbage collection work in SSDs?
options: It collects error logs from the drive for diagnostics
It permanently deletes all data from the entire SSD
It moves valid pages and erases blocks to reclaim free space
It removes unused device drivers from the system
explanation: SSD garbage collection identifies blocks with invalid (stale) pages, copies valid pages to a new block, and erases the entire old block to make it available for new writes, as flash requires block-level erasure.

text: What is the I/O scheduler's deadline algorithm?
options: It only processes requests that arrive before a fixed time
It assigns deadlines to requests preventing starvation by age
It processes all requests simultaneously without ordering
It cancels requests that take too long to complete
explanation: The deadline I/O scheduler maintains separate read and write queues sorted by sector and deadline. It normally serves requests by sector but switches to the deadline queue when a request is about to expire, preventing starvation.

text: What is the difference between thin and thick provisioning in storage?
options: Thin provisioning only works with magnetic disk drives
Thin allocates space on demand; thick pre-allocates all space
Thick allocates space on demand; thin pre-allocates all space
Both methods allocate storage identically in all cases
explanation: Thin provisioning allocates physical storage on-demand as data is written, allowing over-commitment. Thick provisioning pre-allocates all requested space upfront, guaranteeing availability but potentially wasting unused space.

text: What is a storage area network (SAN)?
options: A local disk drive connected directly to the computer
A file-sharing protocol for home network use only
A dedicated high-speed network providing block-level storage access
A software-defined storage solution running on a single server
explanation: A SAN is a dedicated high-speed network that provides block-level data storage access, connecting servers to shared storage arrays using protocols like Fibre Channel or iSCSI for high-performance I/O.

text: How does the CFQ (Completely Fair Queuing) I/O scheduler work?
options: It processes all I/O requests in strict FIFO order only
It assigns per-process queues with time slices for fair I/O access
It randomly selects which process gets I/O access next
It gives all I/O bandwidth to the process with highest priority
explanation: CFQ creates a separate I/O queue for each process and allocates time slices to each queue in round-robin fashion, ensuring fair distribution of disk bandwidth among all processes.

text: What is an I/O device in a computer system?
options: A programming language used to write applications
A section of memory reserved for the operating system
Hardware that transfers data into or out of the computer
Software that manages CPU scheduling for processes
explanation: An I/O device is any hardware component used to transfer data into (input) or out of (output) a computer system, such as keyboards, monitors, disks, and network adapters.

text: What is a device controller?
options: Software that translates user commands into kernel calls
A scheduling algorithm for CPU time allocation
A memory region used for caching disk data
Hardware that operates a specific device and provides an interface
explanation: A device controller is an electronic component that operates a specific I/O device and provides a standardized interface (registers, buffers) for the operating system to communicate with the device.

text: What is polling in I/O handling?
options: The device sends an interrupt when it completes an operation
Data is transferred directly between device and memory
The CPU repeatedly checks device status until it is ready
Multiple devices share a single interrupt line equally
explanation: Polling (busy waiting) is a technique where the CPU repeatedly checks the status register of an I/O device in a loop until the device indicates it is ready for data transfer.

text: What is interrupt-driven I/O?
options: Data transfers bypass the CPU using DMA channels
The device waits indefinitely for CPU instructions
The device sends an interrupt to the CPU when ready
The CPU polls the device continuously until completion
explanation: In interrupt-driven I/O, the device sends a hardware interrupt to the CPU when it is ready for data transfer, freeing the CPU to perform other work while waiting.

text: What is a device driver in the I/O subsystem?
options: A physical cable connecting the device to the motherboard
A CPU register dedicated to I/O operations
A memory buffer used for temporary data storage
Software that provides a uniform interface to a specific device
explanation: A device driver is OS software that encapsulates the details of a specific hardware device, providing a uniform interface that the kernel's I/O subsystem can use to interact with the device.

text: What is buffering in I/O systems?
options: Permanently storing data on the hard disk drive
Compressing data to reduce transfer bandwidth
Encrypting data before sending it to a device
Storing data temporarily during transfer between devices
explanation: Buffering is the technique of temporarily storing data in memory during transfers between two devices or between a device and an application to handle speed mismatches and transfer size differences.

text: What is caching in the context of I/O systems?
options: Transferring data from one device to another directly
Converting data from one format to another for storage
Deleting old data to free up storage space on disk
Keeping copies of frequently accessed data in fast memory
explanation: Caching in I/O systems stores copies of frequently accessed data in faster memory (like RAM or CPU cache), reducing the need to access slower storage devices for repeated data requests.

text: What is the difference between character and block devices?
options: Character devices are faster than block devices always
Block transfers one byte at a time; character transfers in blocks
Both device types transfer data in exactly the same way
Character transfers one byte at a time; block transfers in blocks
explanation: Character devices (like keyboards and serial ports) transfer data one character at a time, while block devices (like disks) transfer data in fixed-size blocks and support random access.

text: What is a bus in computer architecture?
options: A type of CPU register for arithmetic operations
A communication pathway connecting components in a system
A storage device used for backup purposes
A software library for device driver development
explanation: A bus is a communication system consisting of wires and protocols that transfers data between components such as the CPU, memory, and I/O devices within a computer system.

text: What is the I/O request lifecycle in an operating system?
options: The CPU handles all I/O without involving any software
Application request to driver to controller to device and back
I/O requests are processed only during system shutdown
Device directly sends data to the application without the OS
explanation: An I/O request flows from the application through system calls to the kernel I/O subsystem, then to the device driver, device controller, and finally the physical device, with results returned along the same path.

text: What is double buffering and why is it used?
options: Two buffers alternate so one fills while the other empties
Two copies of data are stored permanently for backup
Buffering is disabled to improve I/O transfer speed
A single buffer is used twice for the same data transfer
explanation: Double buffering uses two buffers alternately: while one buffer is being processed (emptied/consumed), the other is being filled with new data, allowing overlap of I/O and processing for better throughput.

text: How does DMA reduce CPU overhead for I/O operations?
options: DMA uses software polling instead of hardware interrupts
DMA eliminates the need for device controllers entirely
DMA increases the CPU clock speed during I/O transfers
The DMA controller transfers data blocks without CPU for each byte
explanation: DMA allows the device controller to transfer entire blocks of data between device and memory without CPU involvement for each byte, interrupting the CPU only when the entire transfer is complete.

text: What is the purpose of the I/O scheduler in an operating system?
options: It schedules CPU time for processes waiting for I/O
It reorders I/O requests to improve device performance
It compiles device driver source code automatically
It manages the physical connections between devices
