explanation: The I/O scheduler reorders pending I/O requests to minimize seek time and improve throughput, using algorithms like SCAN, C-SCAN, or deadline to optimize the order in which requests are serviced.

text: What is the difference between blocking and non-blocking I/O?
options: Blocking I/O is always faster than non-blocking I/O
Both I/O modes behave identically from the process viewpoint
Non-blocking suspends the process; blocking returns immediately
Blocking suspends the process; non-blocking returns immediately
explanation: Blocking I/O suspends the calling process until the operation completes. Non-blocking I/O returns immediately with whatever data is available (or an indication that none is), allowing the process to continue executing.

text: What is asynchronous I/O?
options: The process waits until the I/O operation fully completes
The process continues while I/O completes and is notified later
I/O operations are performed only during CPU idle time
Multiple I/O operations are combined into a single request
explanation: Asynchronous I/O allows a process to issue an I/O request and continue executing immediately. The process is notified (via signal, callback, or polling) when the I/O operation completes.

text: What is the purpose of spooling in I/O management?
options: It compresses output data before sending to the device
It buffers output for slow devices so processes are not blocked
It encrypts output data for secure transmission to devices
It speeds up the physical operation of output devices
explanation: Spooling stores output data in a buffer (usually on disk) for slow devices like printers, allowing the sending process to complete without waiting for the slow device, improving system throughput.

text: What is the unified buffer cache in modern operating systems?
options: A separate cache for each individual device in the system
A hardware cache built into the storage device controller
A buffer that only caches network packet data for speed
A single cache serving both file system and virtual memory I/O
explanation: A unified buffer cache uses a single pool of kernel memory to cache both file system data and memory-mapped file pages, avoiding double caching and more efficiently using available memory.

text: How does memory-mapped file I/O work?
options: File contents are mapped to process address space for direct access
Memory addresses are written directly to the disk surface
File operations bypass the operating system kernel entirely
Files are copied entirely into CPU registers for processing
explanation: Memory-mapped file I/O maps a file's contents into the process's virtual address space, allowing the application to access file data using regular memory load/store instructions instead of read/write system calls.

text: What is the role of the interrupt handler in I/O processing?
options: It schedules which application runs next on the CPU
It prevents all interrupts from reaching the CPU permanently
It manages the physical connections between I/O devices
It processes device interrupts and transfers data or signals completion
explanation: The interrupt handler (ISR) is invoked when a device generates an interrupt. It processes the interrupt by transferring data, updating device status, and waking up any processes waiting for the I/O completion.

text: What is vectored I/O (scatter-gather)?
options: It compresses data into vectors before disk storage
It queues all I/O requests into a single ordered vector
It sends data to multiple I/O devices simultaneously
It transfers data between device and multiple memory regions at once
explanation: Vectored I/O (scatter-gather) allows a single I/O operation to transfer data between a device and multiple non-contiguous memory buffers, reducing system call overhead by avoiding multiple separate read/write calls.

text: What is zero-copy I/O and how does it improve performance?
options: Data moves from device to destination without CPU memory copies
The CPU copies data zero times by disabling all I/O
No data is transferred during the I/O operation at all
Data is copied zero times because it is always in cache
explanation: Zero-copy I/O eliminates unnecessary data copies between kernel and user buffers by transferring data directly from the source (e.g., disk) to the destination (e.g., network socket), reducing CPU overhead and memory bandwidth usage.

text: What is the Linux io_uring framework?
options: A kernel module that disables all asynchronous I/O
A graphical tool for monitoring I/O performance metrics
A device driver framework for USB storage devices only
A high-performance async I/O interface using shared ring buffers
explanation: io_uring is a Linux asynchronous I/O framework using shared submission and completion ring buffers between user and kernel space, achieving high-performance I/O with minimal system call overhead.

text: What is the difference between MMIO and PMIO for device communication?
options: MMIO is used only for input; PMIO is used only for output
PMIO uses memory addresses; MMIO uses separate I/O port space
Both methods use identical address spaces for device access
MMIO uses memory addresses; PMIO uses separate I/O port space
explanation: Memory-Mapped I/O (MMIO) maps device registers into the memory address space, accessed via load/store. Port-Mapped I/O (PMIO) uses a separate I/O address space accessed via special instructions (in/out on x86).

text: What is an I/O completion port in Windows?
options: A single-threaded I/O handler for sequential processing
A network port used exclusively for file transfer protocol
A physical port on the computer for connecting I/O devices
A scalable mechanism for handling async I/O with thread pool
explanation: I/O Completion Ports (IOCP) in Windows provide a highly scalable mechanism for processing asynchronous I/O using a kernel-managed queue and a thread pool that efficiently handles thousands of concurrent I/O operations.

text: What is IOMMU and how does it relate to device I/O?
options: It is a software driver that emulates hardware devices
It manages the input/output of the memory management unit
It translates device DMA addresses providing memory protection
It replaces the CPU for all memory management operations
explanation: The IOMMU (I/O Memory Management Unit) translates device-visible virtual addresses to physical addresses for DMA, providing memory protection and isolation, preventing devices from accessing unauthorized memory regions.

text: What is the top-half and bottom-half interrupt processing model?
options: Bottom-half runs immediately; top-half is deferred to later
Top-half handles all work; bottom-half is never executed
Both halves execute simultaneously on different CPU cores
Top-half runs immediately with minimal work; bottom-half defers rest
explanation: The top-half runs in interrupt context doing minimal time-critical work (acknowledging the interrupt, saving data). The bottom-half (softirq, tasklet, workqueue) runs later to complete remaining processing with interrupts enabled.

text: What is the purpose of the Linux udev subsystem?
options: It compiles the Linux kernel from source code
It provides a user interface for device driver development
It dynamically manages device nodes in /dev based on events
It uploads device firmware to the manufacturer's server
explanation: udev is a Linux device manager that dynamically creates and removes device nodes in /dev in response to kernel events when devices are added or removed, replacing the older static device node approach.

text: What is SR-IOV in the context of I/O virtualization?
options: It allows a physical device to appear as multiple virtual devices
It virtualizes the CPU to improve I/O performance
It combines multiple physical devices into one logical device
It reduces a single device to half its original speed
explanation: SR-IOV (Single Root I/O Virtualization) allows a single physical PCIe device to present multiple virtual functions, each assignable to a different virtual machine for near-native I/O performance without hypervisor overhead.

text: How does the Linux epoll mechanism differ from select/poll?
options: epoll uses event notification avoiding scanning all descriptors
epoll is slower than select for handling any number of sockets
epoll scans all file descriptors on every call like select
epoll can only monitor a maximum of 64 file descriptors
explanation: epoll uses an event-driven notification model where the kernel maintains a watch list and reports only ready file descriptors, achieving O(1) per event instead of O(n) scanning like select/poll for large descriptor sets.

text: What is the SPDK (Storage Performance Development Kit) approach to I/O?
options: It moves storage drivers to user space with polled-mode for speed
It provides a standard kernel-based storage driver framework
It is a hardware accelerator card for storage operations
It slows down I/O operations to improve data reliability
explanation: SPDK moves NVMe and other storage drivers into user space and uses polled-mode instead of interrupts, eliminating kernel overhead and context switches to achieve extremely low latency and high IOPS.

text: What is authentication in operating system security?
options: Formatting the disk to remove all data
Backing up files to prevent data loss
Verifying the identity of a user or process
Encrypting data stored on the hard disk drive
explanation: Authentication is the process of verifying the identity of a user or process, typically through passwords, biometrics, tokens, or certificates, to ensure only authorized entities access the system.

text: What is authorization in an operating system?
options: Encrypting network traffic between two computers
Verifying the identity of a user through a password
Determining what resources an authenticated user can access
Installing security patches on the operating system
explanation: Authorization is the process of determining what resources and operations an authenticated user or process is permitted to access, based on access control policies.

text: What is an access control list (ACL)?
options: A list of running processes in the operating system
A list of hardware devices connected to the computer
A list specifying which users have which permissions on a resource
A list of all files stored on the file system
explanation: An access control list is a data structure associated with a resource that specifies which users or groups have which types of access (read, write, execute) to that resource.

text: What is a virus in operating system security?
options: A system update that fixes security vulnerabilities
A type of firewall that protects network connections
A hardware failure that damages the CPU permanently
A malicious program that attaches to legitimate programs to spread
explanation: A virus is a malicious piece of code that attaches itself to a legitimate program and spreads when the infected program is executed, potentially damaging data or disrupting system operations.

text: What is a Trojan horse in computer security?
options: A type of encryption algorithm for securing data
A malicious program disguised as legitimate useful software
A debugging tool used by software developers
A hardware device used for network intrusion detection
explanation: A Trojan horse is a type of malware that appears to be a legitimate or useful program but secretly performs malicious actions like stealing data or creating backdoors when executed.

text: What is the principle of least privilege?
options: All users should have full administrative access to the system
Only the system administrator should use the computer
The system should grant maximum privileges by default
Users and processes should have only the minimum necessary rights
explanation: The principle of least privilege states that every user and process should operate with the minimum set of privileges necessary to complete their task, reducing the potential damage from errors or attacks.

text: What is a firewall?
options: A software tool for defragmenting the hard disk
A backup power supply for the computer system
A system that monitors and controls network traffic by rules
A device that cools the CPU to prevent overheating
explanation: A firewall is a network security system that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on predetermined security rules, acting as a barrier between trusted and untrusted networks.

text: What is encryption in operating system security?
options: Creating backup copies of important system files
Deleting sensitive files from the file system
Compressing data to save storage space on disk
Converting data into an unreadable format using a key
explanation: Encryption is the process of converting plaintext data into an unreadable ciphertext format using a cryptographic key, ensuring that only authorized parties with the decryption key can read the data.

text: What is a worm in computer security?
options: A program that requires user action to spread to others
A hardware component that protects against power surges
A tool that repairs damaged files on the system
A self-replicating program that spreads without host programs
explanation: A worm is a standalone malicious program that self-replicates and spreads across networks without needing to attach to a host program, unlike a virus which requires a host to propagate.

text: What is the purpose of password hashing?
options: Compressing passwords to save database storage space
Storing passwords as irreversible hashes instead of plaintext
Converting passwords into shorter easier-to-remember forms
Encrypting passwords so they can be decrypted when needed
explanation: Password hashing converts passwords into fixed-length irreversible hash values for storage. When a user logs in, the entered password is hashed and compared to the stored hash, preventing plaintext password exposure.

text: What is a buffer overflow attack?
options: A network attack that floods the connection buffer with packets
Allocating too many buffers causing system memory exhaustion
Filling the disk buffer with too much data slowing transfers
Writing data beyond a buffer boundary to overwrite adjacent memory
explanation: A buffer overflow attack writes data beyond the bounds of a buffer, overwriting adjacent memory such as return addresses or function pointers, allowing an attacker to redirect program execution to malicious code.

text: What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption?
options: Symmetric uses one key; asymmetric uses a public-private pair
Both encryption types use identical key mechanisms
Asymmetric uses one key; symmetric uses a public-private pair
Symmetric encryption is always slower than asymmetric
explanation: Symmetric encryption uses the same key for both encryption and decryption (e.g., AES). Asymmetric encryption uses a pair of keys: a public key for encryption and a private key for decryption (e.g., RSA).

text: What is a denial-of-service (DoS) attack?
options: Installing a backdoor for persistent unauthorized access
Encrypting user files and demanding ransom for the key
Stealing user passwords through deceptive login pages
Overwhelming a system with requests to make it unavailable
explanation: A denial-of-service attack floods a system with excessive requests or exploits vulnerabilities to consume resources, making the system or service unavailable to legitimate users.

text: What is the access matrix model in OS protection?
options: A matrix storing memory page table translations
A matrix defining access rights of subjects over objects
A matrix showing network connections between computers
A matrix of CPU scheduling priorities for processes
explanation: The access matrix is a protection model where rows represent subjects (users/processes), columns represent objects (files/resources), and each entry defines the set of access rights the subject has on the object.

text: What is sandboxing in operating system security?
options: Running code in an isolated environment with restricted access
Storing backup copies of important files in a safe location
Testing hardware components before installing in production
Scanning files for viruses before allowing execution
explanation: Sandboxing runs a program in an isolated, restricted environment that limits its access to system resources, preventing potentially malicious or untrusted code from affecting the rest of the system.

text: What is a rootkit?
options: A software kit for building the root file system on Linux
Malware that hides its presence by modifying OS internals
A legitimate tool for system administrators to manage servers
An encryption tool for protecting root user passwords
explanation: A rootkit is malicious software designed to hide its presence and maintain unauthorized access by modifying OS internals, system calls, or kernel structures to evade detection by security tools.

text: What is the difference between discretionary and mandatory access control?
options: DAC lets owners set permissions; MAC enforces system-wide policies
DAC is used only in Windows; MAC is used only in Linux
Both access control models give identical levels of security
MAC lets owners set permissions; DAC enforces system-wide policies
explanation: Discretionary Access Control (DAC) allows resource owners to set permissions. Mandatory Access Control (MAC) enforces system-wide security policies (e.g., security labels) that cannot be overridden by owners.

text: What is a man-in-the-middle attack?
options: A hardware failure that disrupts network connectivity
A user accidentally deletes important system configuration files
A process crashes due to an unhandled exception in code
An attacker secretly intercepts communication between two parties
explanation: A man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack occurs when an attacker secretly intercepts and potentially alters communication between two parties who believe they are communicating directly with each other.

text: What is the purpose of Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR)?
options: It compresses the address space to save physical memory
It assigns fixed addresses to all processes for stability
It sorts memory addresses in ascending order for efficiency
It randomizes memory layout to prevent exploitation attacks
explanation: ASLR randomizes the memory addresses used by processes (stack, heap, libraries) at each execution, making it much harder for attackers to predict target addresses needed for buffer overflow exploits.

text: What is role-based access control (RBAC)?
options: Roles are assigned based on the hardware the user accesses
Access is controlled based on the time of day only
Access is granted based on roles assigned to users in the system
Each user has a unique set of permissions unrelated to roles
explanation: RBAC assigns permissions to roles rather than individual users, and users are assigned roles. This simplifies administration since permissions are managed at the role level instead of per-user.

text: What is the Bell-LaPadula security model?
options: It allows unrestricted reading and writing at all levels
It enforces no read up and no write down for confidentiality
It applies only to network security, not operating systems
It enforces no read down and no write up for integrity
explanation: The Bell-LaPadula model enforces confidentiality through two rules: no read up (a subject cannot read above its clearance) and no write down (a subject cannot write below its clearance), preventing information leakage.

text: What is the Biba integrity model?
options: It allows all subjects to write to any integrity level
It enforces no read up and no write down for confidentiality
It is used only for availability, not integrity protection
It enforces no read down and no write up for data integrity
explanation: The Biba model is the dual of Bell-LaPadula, focusing on integrity: no read down (do not read less trustworthy data) and no write up (do not corrupt higher-integrity data), preventing data corruption.

text: What is return-oriented programming (ROP)?
options: A method of optimizing function return values for speed
A programming paradigm where all functions return void
A debugging technique for tracing function return paths
Chaining existing code snippets ending in return to execute attacks
explanation: ROP is an exploitation technique that chains together small existing code sequences (gadgets) ending in return instructions to perform arbitrary operations, bypassing non-executable stack protections (DEP/NX).

text: What is SELinux and how does it enhance system security?
options: A standard Linux file system designed for encrypted storage
A user authentication system replacing traditional passwords
A network firewall built into the Linux kernel by default
A MAC framework providing fine-grained mandatory access policies
explanation: SELinux (Security-Enhanced Linux) is a mandatory access control framework that enforces fine-grained security policies defining exactly what each process, user, and role can access, beyond standard DAC permissions.

text: What is a side-channel attack in operating system security?
options: Guessing passwords through brute-force dictionary attacks
Attacking a system through its primary network interface
Extracting information through timing, power, or cache behavior
Exploiting buffer overflows in application source code
explanation: Side-channel attacks extract secret information by observing physical characteristics like execution timing, power consumption, electromagnetic emissions, or cache access patterns rather than exploiting software vulnerabilities.

text: What is the purpose of secure boot in modern systems?
options: It encrypts the boot partition for data confidentiality
It boots the system faster by skipping hardware checks
It provides a graphical boot menu for OS selection
It verifies each boot component's signature before execution
explanation: Secure boot verifies the digital signature of each component in the boot chain (firmware, bootloader, kernel) before execution, preventing the loading of unauthorized or tampered code during startup.

text: What is control flow integrity (CFI)?
options: It controls the flow of data between disk and memory
It monitors network traffic flow for security anomalies
It restricts program execution to follow only valid control paths
It manages the integrity of file system journal entries
explanation: CFI is a security technique that ensures program execution follows only the control flow graph determined at compile time, preventing attackers from redirecting execution through code injection or ROP attacks.

text: How does kernel page table isolation (KPTI) mitigate Meltdown?
options: It removes the kernel from memory during user execution
It disables speculative execution in the CPU entirely
It encrypts all kernel memory to prevent unauthorized reading
It separates kernel and user page tables preventing data leaks
explanation: KPTI maintains separate page tables for kernel and user mode, with the user-mode page table containing only minimal kernel mappings, preventing Meltdown attacks from speculatively reading kernel memory.

text: What is a use-after-free vulnerability?
options: Freeing memory that was never allocated by the program
Using a file after it has been deleted from the disk
Using a variable before it has been initialized in code
Accessing memory after it has been freed leading to exploitation
explanation: A use-after-free vulnerability occurs when a program continues to use a pointer to memory that has been freed. An attacker can reallocate that memory with malicious data, gaining control when the dangling pointer is used.

text: What are namespaces in Linux and how do they support container security?
options: They manage DNS resolution for container network addresses
They encrypt network traffic between containers for privacy
They provide a naming convention for files in the file system
They isolate system resources giving each container its own view
explanation: Linux namespaces provide isolated views of system resources (PID, network, mount, user, etc.) for each container, ensuring that processes in one container cannot see or affect resources in another.

text: What is the primary purpose of an operating system kernel?
options: It manages core system resources and hardware
It serves as a text editor for writing code
It functions as an external peripheral device
It operates as an application software package
explanation: The kernel is the core component of an operating system responsible for managing hardware resources, memory, and process scheduling.

text: Which operating system is open-source and freely available?
options: Microsoft Windows desktop edition
Apple macOS desktop system
Linux kernel-based distributions
IBM z/OS mainframe system
explanation: Linux is an open-source operating system whose source code is freely available for modification and distribution under various licenses.

text: What does a real-time operating system (RTOS) guarantee?
options: Fastest possible throughput for batch jobs
Response within strict time deadlines
Lowest memory usage among all OS types
Automatic code compilation for programs
explanation: A real-time operating system guarantees that tasks are completed within strict time constraints, making it suitable for embedded and time-critical systems.

text: What is the role of a device driver in an operating system?
options: It translates between OS and hardware devices
It manages user login and authentication
It compresses files for disk storage space
It schedules CPU time for running processes
explanation: A device driver acts as a translator between the operating system and a specific hardware device, enabling communication and control.

text: Which of the following best describes multiprogramming?
options: Running one program at a time on the CPU
Keeping multiple programs in memory at once
Using multiple monitors for display output
Writing programs in multiple languages
explanation: Multiprogramming keeps multiple programs in memory simultaneously so the CPU can switch to another program when one is waiting for I/O.

text: What is the bootstrap program responsible for?
options: Loading the operating system at system startup
Encrypting all files on the hard disk drive
Managing network connections for the system
Compiling high-level code into machine code
explanation: The bootstrap program is the first code that runs when a computer is powered on, responsible for initializing hardware and loading the operating system.

text: Which component stores the operating system permanently?
options: Random Access Memory module
Central Processing Unit chip
Secondary storage such as a hard drive
Graphics Processing Unit card
explanation: The operating system is stored permanently on secondary storage like a hard drive or SSD and loaded into RAM during the boot process.

text: What is a distributed operating system?
options: An OS that runs on a single standalone machine
An OS that manages multiple networked computers
An OS designed exclusively for mobile phones
An OS that only handles batch job processing
explanation: A distributed operating system manages a collection of independent networked computers, making them appear as a single coherent system to users.

text: What does the term throughput refer to in operating systems?
options: The speed of a single instruction execution
The number of processes completed per time unit
The total amount of installed physical memory
The size of the operating system on the disk
explanation: Throughput refers to the number of processes or jobs that are completed per unit of time, serving as a measure of system productivity.

text: Which of the following is a function of an operating system?
options: Writing application code for end users
Managing memory allocation for processes
Designing hardware circuit board layouts
Creating graphical artwork for websites
explanation: Memory management is a core function of the operating system, which allocates and deallocates memory space to processes as needed.

text: What distinguishes a microkernel architecture from a monolithic kernel?
options: A microkernel runs all services in kernel space
A microkernel moves most services to user space
A microkernel does not support process scheduling
A microkernel eliminates the need for system calls
explanation: A microkernel architecture moves most OS services (file systems, drivers) to user space, keeping only essential functions like IPC and scheduling in the kernel.

text: In a time-sharing system, what mechanism enables fair CPU allocation?
options: Each process gets a fixed time quantum to execute
Processes are executed in order of their file size
The CPU runs only one process until it finishes
Memory is equally divided but CPU is not shared
explanation: Time-sharing systems use time quanta to give each process a fair share of CPU time, rapidly switching between processes.

text: What is the main advantage of a layered operating system design?
options: It provides faster execution than other designs
Each layer can be debugged and verified separately
It eliminates the need for hardware abstraction
It requires less memory than monolithic kernels
explanation: The layered approach allows each layer to be independently debugged and verified, as each layer only uses functions of lower layers, simplifying development.

text: How does a hypervisor differ from a traditional operating system?
options: A hypervisor manages virtual machines on hardware
A hypervisor can only run a single guest OS at once
A hypervisor replaces the need for any guest OS
A hypervisor is a type of application-level software
explanation: A hypervisor sits between hardware and guest operating systems, managing multiple virtual machines running simultaneously.

text: What problem does spooling solve in operating systems?
options: It prevents deadlocks between competing processes
It buffers data for slow devices like printers
It compresses files to save disk storage space
It encrypts data during network transmission
explanation: Spooling buffers data for slow I/O devices, allowing the CPU to continue processing while data is queued for the device.

text: What is the purpose of a system call in an operating system?
options: To directly access hardware without OS involvement
To provide a programmatic interface to OS services
To compile source code into executable binaries
To display graphical user interface components
explanation: System calls provide a controlled interface for user programs to request services from the operating system kernel.

text: Why is dual-mode operation important in an operating system?
options: It doubles the CPU processing speed for programs
It separates user execution from kernel execution
It allows two users to log in at the same time
It enables the OS to run without any hardware
explanation: Dual-mode operation protects the OS from errant user programs by restricting certain privileged instructions to kernel mode only.

text: What role does the interrupt vector table play during an interrupt?
options: It stores the addresses of interrupt service routines
It holds user application data during processing
It manages the file system directory structure
It controls the power supply to hardware devices
explanation: The interrupt vector table contains the addresses of interrupt service routines, allowing the CPU to quickly locate and execute the correct handler.

text: What is the difference between a policy and a mechanism in OS design?
options: A policy is hardware; a mechanism is software
A policy decides what to do; a mechanism decides how
A policy is for users; a mechanism is for admins
A policy is faster while a mechanism is slower
explanation: In OS design, a policy determines what should be done while a mechanism provides the means to implement it.

text: How does Direct Memory Access (DMA) improve system performance?
options: It allows devices to transfer data without CPU help
It increases the CPU clock speed during transfers
It compresses data before writing to memory chips
It encrypts data as it moves between components
explanation: DMA allows hardware devices to transfer data directly to and from memory without involving the CPU for each byte, freeing the CPU to perform other tasks.

text: In a microkernel OS, what is the primary overhead concern with message passing?
options: Excessive memory fragmentation in user space
Frequent context switches between user and kernel
Increased disk I/O from logging all messages
Higher power consumption by network interface
explanation: Microkernel architectures require frequent context switches for IPC via message passing, as services in user space must communicate through the kernel.

text: What is the main challenge of implementing an exokernel operating system?
options: Applications must manage their own resource abstractions
The kernel becomes too large to fit in memory
It cannot support more than one running application
Hardware manufacturers refuse to provide drivers
explanation: An exokernel provides minimal abstractions, exposing raw hardware resources to applications, requiring each application to implement its own resource management.

text: How does the concept of a unikernel differ from a traditional OS?
options: A unikernel links application and OS into one image
A unikernel supports multiple concurrent user logins
A unikernel uses a layered architecture exclusively
A unikernel requires a hypervisor to manage memory
explanation: A unikernel compiles a single application together with only the needed OS components into one specialized image, eliminating unnecessary OS overhead.

text: What security vulnerability does the Meltdown attack exploit in modern CPUs?
options: Buffer overflow in the operating system kernel code
Speculative execution leaking kernel memory to users
Weak encryption in the file system driver module
Improper validation of network packet checksums
explanation: Meltdown exploits speculative execution in modern CPUs to allow unprivileged user processes to read kernel memory, bypassing hardware memory isolation.

text: In capability-based operating systems, how are resources accessed?
options: Through unforgeable tokens granting specific rights
Through global access control lists for all users
Through passwords entered at each resource request
Through hardware-level encryption of resource IDs
explanation: Capability-based systems use unforgeable tokens that grant holders specific access rights to resources, providing fine-grained security.

text: What problem does the Trusted Platform Module (TPM) solve for OS security?
options: It accelerates floating-point math operations
It provides hardware-based secure key storage
It replaces the CPU for all encryption tasks
It manages virtual memory page table entries
explanation: The TPM is a hardware chip that provides secure storage for cryptographic keys and supports hardware-based integrity verification during the boot process.

text: Why is the halting problem relevant to operating system design?
options: It proves that perfect process scheduling is trivial
It shows an OS cannot always detect infinite loops
It demonstrates that memory allocation is undecidable
It indicates that file systems cannot be reliable
explanation: The halting problem proves that no general algorithm can determine whether an arbitrary program will terminate, meaning an OS cannot always detect infinite loops.

text: What is the purpose of an IOMMU in modern operating systems?
options: It schedules I/O operations across multiple CPUs
It translates device addresses to physical memory safely
It compresses data transferred between devices
It manages interrupt priority levels for devices
explanation: An IOMMU translates device virtual addresses to physical addresses, protecting memory from malicious or buggy DMA-capable devices.

text: How does address space layout randomization (ASLR) enhance OS security?
options: It encrypts all virtual memory pages in the system
It randomizes memory locations of key program areas
It prevents all buffer overflow attacks completely
It disables direct memory access for all devices
explanation: ASLR randomizes the memory addresses of key areas such as the stack, heap, and libraries, making it harder for attackers to predict target addresses.

text: What is the significance of the principle of least privilege in OS design?
options: Programs should run with the maximum available rights
Programs should be given only the minimum rights needed
Programs should share all resources without restrictions
Programs should avoid making any system calls entirely
explanation: The principle of least privilege dictates that programs and users should operate with only the minimum set of privileges needed to complete their tasks.

text: What is a system call used for in an operating system?
options: Requesting services from the operating system kernel
Connecting to a remote server over the internet
Compiling source code into an executable program
Displaying graphics on the computer monitor screen
explanation: A system call is the mechanism by which a user program requests a service from the operating system kernel.

text: Which of the following is a category of system calls?
options: Process control system calls
Graphical rendering system calls
Audio processing system calls
Database query system calls
explanation: Process control is one of the main categories of system calls, including operations like creating, terminating, and managing processes.

text: What is the purpose of an application programming interface (API)?
options: It provides functions for programmers to use OS services
It physically connects hardware components together
It formats hard drives for initial use by the OS
It manages the boot sequence of the computer system
explanation: An API provides a set of functions and protocols that programmers can use to interact with the operating system.

text: In a monolithic kernel, where do OS services execute?
options: Entirely in user space as separate processes
Entirely in kernel space as a single program
On a remote server accessed over a network
In a virtual machine managed by a hypervisor
explanation: In a monolithic kernel, all OS services execute in kernel space as part of a single large program.

text: What does the command interpreter (shell) do?
options: It reads and executes user commands
It manages physical memory allocation
It handles hardware interrupt signals
It controls network data packet routing
explanation: The command interpreter or shell reads commands from the user or a script and executes them.

text: What type of interface does a GUI provide to the user?
options: A text-based command line for typed input
A graphical visual interface with icons and windows
A voice-activated interface for spoken commands
A hardware-level interface for circuit design
explanation: A GUI provides a visual interface with icons, windows, and menus that allows users to interact using a pointing device.

text: What is a system program in operating systems?
options: A program providing a convenient environment for development
A program that runs only during the boot process
A program that replaces the kernel during execution
A program that manages only printer hardware devices
explanation: System programs provide a convenient environment for program development and execution.

text: What is the role of the linker in program execution?
options: It combines object modules into a single executable
It translates source code into assembly language
It manages process scheduling on the CPU
It allocates memory for running processes
explanation: The linker combines multiple object modules and library routines into a single executable binary file.

text: What is the function of the loader in an operating system?
options: It loads an executable program into main memory
It compiles source code into object code files
It encrypts files before storing them on disk
It formats the disk for the file system structure
explanation: The loader is responsible for loading an executable program from secondary storage into main memory.

text: Which approach separates OS functionality into distinct layers?
options: The monolithic system approach
The client-server model approach
The layered operating system approach
The virtual machine based approach
explanation: The layered approach organizes the OS into distinct layers, where each layer uses only functions of lower layers.

text: Why do operating systems use a table-driven interrupt mechanism?
options: To quickly dispatch to the correct interrupt handler
To store user data during file write operations
To manage the graphical display refresh rate
To compress kernel code for faster execution
explanation: A table-driven interrupt mechanism uses an interrupt vector table to quickly look up and jump to the correct interrupt service routine.

text: What is the advantage of loadable kernel modules over a purely layered OS?
options: They eliminate the need for any kernel at all
They allow dynamic loading without recompiling the kernel
They prevent any user from accessing kernel functions
They make the operating system completely stateless
explanation: Loadable kernel modules can be inserted and removed at runtime without recompiling the kernel.

text: How does a system call differ from a regular function call?
options: A system call triggers a mode switch to kernel mode
A system call is faster than a regular function call
A system call does not use the program stack at all
A system call can only be made from assembly code
explanation: Unlike regular function calls, system calls trigger a transition from user mode to kernel mode.

text: What is the purpose of the system call interface in an OS?
options: It acts as a lookup table mapping API calls to system calls
It directly manages hardware interrupt controllers
It replaces the need for device drivers entirely
It provides permanent storage for user applications
explanation: The system call interface serves as an intermediary that maps API function calls to the appropriate system call numbers.

text: What problem does the hybrid kernel architecture attempt to solve?
options: Combining microkernel flexibility with monolithic speed
Eliminating the need for any device driver software
Removing all user-space processes from the system
Replacing hardware interrupts with polling mechanisms
explanation: Hybrid kernels combine the modularity of microkernels with the performance of monolithic kernels.

text: What is the role of parameter passing via registers in system calls?
options: Registers pass system call parameters for fast access
Registers store the entire file system directory tree
Registers manage network socket connections directly
Registers control the display resolution and refresh
explanation: When making system calls, parameters can be passed through CPU registers for fast access.

text: How does the client-server model apply to operating system design?
options: The kernel acts as a server responding to process requests
All code runs in a single kernel without separation
The client always runs on a different physical machine
The server component only handles file operations
explanation: In client-server OS design, the kernel provides minimal services while separate server processes handle specific functions.

text: What is the benefit of using a virtual machine for OS development?
options: It provides an isolated environment for testing safely
It eliminates all bugs from the operating system code
It makes the OS run faster than on real hardware
It removes the need for any debugging tools
explanation: Virtual machines provide isolated environments where OS developers can test changes safely.

text: What is a just-in-time (JIT) compiler in the context of OS execution?
options: It compiles bytecode to native code at runtime
It compiles all programs during the boot process
It interprets machine code one instruction at a time
It converts source code to bytecode before storage
explanation: A JIT compiler translates intermediate bytecode into native machine code at runtime, improving execution speed.

text: Why is the separation of mechanism and policy important in OS structure?
options: It allows changing policies without modifying mechanisms
It makes the operating system completely stateless
It eliminates the need for any scheduling algorithm
It prevents users from executing any system commands
explanation: Separating mechanism from policy allows the OS to be more flexible, as policies can be changed without altering underlying mechanisms.

text: How does the eBPF framework extend the Linux kernel without modifying its source?
options: It runs sandboxed programs in kernel space at runtime
It replaces the entire kernel with a new binary image
It adds new hardware devices to the system dynamically
It modifies the bootloader to inject new kernel code
explanation: eBPF allows users to run sandboxed programs within the Linux kernel at runtime without changing kernel source code.

text: What challenge does the semantic gap present in virtual machine introspection?
options: The hypervisor sees only raw bytes, not OS-level meaning
The guest OS cannot access any physical hardware device
The virtual machine cannot execute any privileged code
The host OS must share all memory with the guest OS
explanation: The semantic gap refers to the difficulty of reconstructing high-level OS state from raw memory visible to the hypervisor.

text: What is the key design principle behind a library OS (libOS)?
options: The OS is linked directly as a library with each application
The OS runs entirely on a remote server via network
The OS kernel contains all possible drivers pre-compiled
The OS shares a single address space among all processes
explanation: A library OS links OS functionality directly into each application as a library.

text: How does the Mach microkernel handle memory management across address spaces?
options: Through external memory manager tasks in user space
Through a monolithic memory manager inside the kernel
Through direct hardware access bypassing all abstractions
Through shared global page tables for every process
explanation: Mach delegates memory management to external pager tasks running in user space.

text: What is the purpose of the vDSO mechanism in Linux?
options: It maps kernel functions into user space to avoid syscall overhead
It provides virtual disk storage for operating system files
It manages virtual display output for graphical applications
It creates virtual device nodes for hardware simulation
explanation: The vDSO maps certain kernel functions into user space, allowing frequently used system calls to execute without a mode switch.

text: In a formally verified OS kernel like seL4, what guarantee does verification provide?
options: The implementation correctly matches its formal specification
The kernel runs faster than any unverified kernel
The kernel is immune to all hardware failure modes
The kernel requires no memory management unit at all
explanation: Formal verification mathematically proves that the kernel implementation conforms to its formal specification.

text: What is the purpose of system call filtering with seccomp-BPF in Linux?
options: It restricts which system calls a process can make
It accelerates system call execution by caching results
It logs all system calls for performance profiling only
It redirects system calls to a remote server for execution
explanation: Seccomp-BPF allows processes to install filters that restrict which system calls they can make.

text: How does a Type-1 hypervisor differ from a Type-2 hypervisor architecturally?
options: Type-1 runs directly on hardware without a host OS
Type-1 runs as an application on top of a host OS
Type-1 cannot support more than one virtual machine
Type-1 requires a separate management operating system
explanation: A Type-1 bare-metal hypervisor runs directly on the hardware without needing a host operating system.

text: What problem does kernel address space layout randomization (KASLR) address?
options: It randomizes kernel memory locations to hinder exploits
It compresses the kernel to reduce memory consumption
It encrypts kernel code to prevent unauthorized reading
It duplicates the kernel across multiple memory regions
explanation: KASLR randomizes the base address of the kernel in memory at each boot, making it harder for attackers to target specific kernel functions.

text: What is the role of a hardware abstraction layer (HAL) in OS portability?
options: It provides a uniform interface hiding hardware differences
It replaces all device drivers with a single generic driver
It converts high-level code into hardware-specific assembly
It manages user authentication across different platforms
explanation: The HAL provides a consistent interface between the OS kernel and hardware, allowing the same kernel code to run on different platforms.

text: What is a process in operating system terminology?
options: A program in execution with its own resources
A file stored on the hard disk drive
A hardware component inside the computer
A command typed into the terminal shell
explanation: A process is a program in execution that includes the program code, current activity, stack, data section, and allocated resources.

text: Which data structure does the OS use to store process information?
options: Process Control Block (PCB)
File Allocation Table (FAT)
Binary Search Tree (BST)
Linked List Node (LLN)
explanation: The PCB stores all information about a process, including its state, program counter, registers, and resource allocation.

text: What state is a process in when it is waiting for I/O completion?
options: Running state on the CPU
Ready state in the queue
Waiting or blocked state
Terminated or exit state
explanation: A process enters the waiting state when it is waiting for an event such as I/O completion.

text: Which system call creates a new process in Unix-based systems?
options: The exec() system call
The fork() system call
The wait() system call
The exit() system call
explanation: The fork() system call creates a new child process by duplicating the calling parent process.

text: What happens when a process calls the exit() system call?
options: The process requests the OS to terminate it
The process creates a new child process
The process moves to the ready queue state
The process begins waiting for user input
explanation: When a process calls exit(), it requests the operating system to delete it and release all its allocated resources.

text: What is the difference between a program and a process?
options: A program is a passive file; a process is active
A program runs on the CPU; a process is stored on disk
A program uses memory; a process uses only the CPU
A program is temporary; a process is permanent on disk
explanation: A program is a passive entity stored on disk, while a process is an active entity with a program counter and resources.

text: What is a context switch in process management?
options: Saving one process state and loading another
Switching between two different user accounts
Changing the file system from one format to another
Converting a program from source code to binary
explanation: A context switch involves saving the state of the currently running process and restoring the state of the next process.

text: What is the ready queue in an operating system?
options: A queue of processes waiting for CPU allocation
A queue of files waiting to be printed out
A queue of users waiting to log into the system
A queue of devices waiting to be installed
explanation: The ready queue contains all processes loaded in memory and waiting to be assigned to the CPU.

text: What does the process state new indicate?
options: The process is being created by the system
The process has finished its execution fully
The process is currently running on the CPU
The process is waiting for an I/O event
explanation: The new state indicates that a process is in the process of being created.

text: What information does the program counter in a PCB hold?
options: The address of the next instruction to execute
The total memory allocated to the process
The priority level of the current process
The number of child processes it created
explanation: The program counter stores the address of the next instruction to be executed for that process.

text: What is the purpose of the exec() system call after fork()?
options: It replaces the child process image with a new program
It terminates the parent process immediately
It duplicates the child process a second time
It suspends the child until the parent finishes
explanation: After fork() creates a child process, exec() replaces the child address space with a new program.

text: What is an orphan process in Unix-like operating systems?
options: A process whose parent has terminated before it
A process that cannot access any system resources
A process that has been permanently suspended
A process waiting for a child to terminate first
explanation: An orphan process is a child process whose parent has terminated. In Unix, orphans are adopted by the init process.

text: What is the difference between short-term and long-term schedulers?
options: Short-term selects from ready queue; long-term from job pool
Short-term manages I/O; long-term manages CPU allocation
Short-term runs daily; long-term runs once at boot
Short-term handles memory; long-term handles disk access
explanation: The short-term scheduler selects which ready process gets the CPU next, while the long-term scheduler controls job admission.

text: How does interprocess communication via shared memory work?
options: Processes read and write to a common memory region
Processes send messages through the operating system
Processes share the same program counter register
Processes execute the same code at the same time
explanation: In shared memory IPC, cooperating processes establish a common memory region for fast data exchange.

text: What is the role of the medium-term scheduler?
options: It swaps processes between memory and disk for balance
It selects the next process for CPU execution directly
It creates new processes from the job submission pool
It manages file access permissions for all processes
explanation: The medium-term scheduler performs swapping to manage the degree of multiprogramming.

text: What is a zombie process and why does it exist?
options: A terminated process whose exit status has not been collected
A process that consumes excessive CPU resources constantly
A process that has been running for an extremely long time
A process that has lost its connection to the terminal
explanation: A zombie process has finished execution but still has a process table entry because its parent has not called wait().

text: How does the pipe mechanism work for interprocess communication?
options: It creates a unidirectional byte stream between processes
It allows processes to share the same virtual memory space
It sends signals between processes to control execution
It provides remote procedure calls across network nodes
explanation: A pipe creates a unidirectional communication channel where one process writes and another reads.

text: What is process cascading termination?
options: Terminating a parent causes all descendants to terminate
All processes in the system terminate simultaneously
A process terminates and restarts in an infinite loop
The OS terminates processes based on memory usage alone
explanation: Cascading termination occurs when the OS automatically terminates all child processes when a parent is terminated.

text: What is the advantage of message passing IPC over shared memory?
options: It is easier to implement in distributed systems
It provides faster data transfer for large volumes
It requires no operating system support at all
It uses less memory than shared memory always
explanation: Message passing is easier to implement in distributed systems where processes may reside on different machines.

text: What is the purpose of the wait() system call in Unix?
options: It makes a parent wait for a child to terminate
It pauses a process for a specified time duration
It causes a process to wait for user keyboard input
It blocks a process until a disk write is complete
explanation: The wait() system call causes a parent process to block until one of its child processes terminates.

text: How does copy-on-write (COW) optimize the fork() system call?
options: Parent and child share pages until one writes to them
The child receives a complete memory copy immediately
The parent memory is moved entirely to the child
Both processes are blocked until memory is duplicated
explanation: Copy-on-write delays duplicating memory pages after fork(). Pages are only copied when one process modifies them.

text: What is the vfork() system call and how does it differ from fork()?
options: The child shares parent address space and parent blocks
The child gets a full copy of the parent memory space
The parent and child both continue executing concurrently
The child is created without any address space at all
explanation: vfork() creates a child that shares the parent address space. The parent is suspended until the child calls exec() or exit().

text: What is the thundering herd problem in process management?
options: Many waiting processes wake up but only one can proceed
A single process monopolizes the CPU indefinitely
All processes terminate simultaneously due to a signal
Processes continuously create children overwhelming memory
explanation: The thundering herd problem occurs when many processes waiting for the same event all wake up but only one can proceed.

text: How does the Linux CFS handle process group fairness?
options: It uses hierarchical scheduling with cgroup-based weights
It assigns equal time slices to every process uniformly
It prioritizes system processes over all user processes
It ignores process groups and schedules individually only
explanation: Linux CFS uses a hierarchical scheduling model where cgroups define process groups with assigned weights.

text: What is a process credential and how does it affect access control?
options: It contains UIDs and GIDs determining resource permissions
It stores the process machine code in encrypted form
It records the total CPU time consumed by the process
It maintains the list of open file descriptors only
explanation: Process credentials include the real, effective, and saved UIDs/GIDs that determine resource access.

text: What is the purpose of posix_spawn() compared to fork/exec?
options: It creates a process efficiently without full fork overhead
It only works for creating daemon background processes
It creates processes that cannot make any system calls
It spawns processes only on remote networked machines
explanation: posix_spawn() combines process creation and program execution in a single call, avoiding fork overhead.

text: How does the Linux kernel handle process address space during execve()?
options: It discards the old address space and maps the new program
It copies the old address space into a backup location
It shares the old address space with the new program
It compresses the old address space to free memory
explanation: During execve(), the kernel destroys the current process address space and creates a new one from the executable.

text: What is the significance of process namespaces in Linux containers?
options: They isolate process ID spaces between containers
They encrypt all interprocess communication channels
They limit the total number of processes system-wide
They merge multiple process trees into a single tree
explanation: PID namespaces give each container its own isolated process ID space.

text: What happens during a process state transition from running to ready?
options: A timer interrupt preempts the process and saves state
The process voluntarily yields because it needs I/O
The process terminates and releases all of its resources
The process is swapped out to disk by the scheduler
explanation: When a timer interrupt occurs, the OS preempts the running process by saving its state and moving it to the ready queue.

text: How does the OOM killer in Linux decide which process to terminate?
options: It scores processes based on memory usage and priority
It always kills the most recently created process first
It terminates processes in alphabetical order by name
It randomly selects a process from the running queue
explanation: The Linux OOM killer calculates a badness score based on memory usage, runtime, and oom_score_adj.

text: What is CPU scheduling in an operating system?
options: Selecting which process gets the CPU next
Allocating disk space to stored files
Managing network bandwidth allocation
Configuring display resolution settings
explanation: CPU scheduling selects which process from the ready queue will be assigned the CPU for execution next.

text: What is a CPU burst in process execution?
options: A period when the process uses the CPU
A period when the process waits for I/O
A period when the process is being created
A period when the process is terminated
explanation: A CPU burst is a continuous period during which a process is actively using the CPU for computation.

text: Which scheduling algorithm allocates CPU in order of arrival?
options: First-Come First-Served scheduling
Shortest Job First scheduling
Round Robin scheduling method
Priority-based scheduling method
explanation: FCFS scheduling allocates the CPU to processes in the order they arrive in the ready queue.

text: What does preemptive scheduling mean?
options: The OS can forcibly remove a process from the CPU
The process runs until it voluntarily releases CPU
The CPU is shared equally by using multiple cores
The process is permanently removed from the system
explanation: In preemptive scheduling, the OS can interrupt a running process to give the CPU to another process.

text: What is turnaround time in CPU scheduling?
options: The total time from submission to completion
The time a process spends waiting in the queue
The time spent executing on the CPU only
The time between two consecutive I/O bursts
explanation: Turnaround time is the total time from when a process is submitted until it completes execution.

text: What is waiting time in the context of scheduling?
options: Time a process spends waiting in the ready queue
Time a process spends executing on the CPU
Time required to load a process into memory
Time taken to save process state during a switch
explanation: Waiting time is the total time a process spends waiting in the ready queue before getting the CPU.

text: What is a time quantum in Round Robin scheduling?
options: The fixed time slice given to each process
The total execution time of a process
The priority value assigned to a process
The number of processes in the ready queue
explanation: A time quantum is the fixed amount of CPU time each process receives before being preempted.

text: Which scheduling algorithm may cause the starvation problem?
options: Shortest Job First scheduling algorithm
Round Robin scheduling algorithm
First-Come First-Served scheduling algorithm
Lottery-based random scheduling algorithm
explanation: SJF can cause starvation because longer processes may wait indefinitely if shorter processes keep arriving.

text: What is the dispatcher component in CPU scheduling?
options: It gives control of the CPU to the selected process
It determines which process should be scheduled next
It creates new processes for the ready queue
It manages memory allocation for each process
explanation: The dispatcher gives control of the CPU to the process selected by the scheduler.

text: What is dispatch latency in scheduling?
options: Time taken to stop one process and start another
Time a process waits in the ready queue
Total execution time of a process on the CPU
Time required to load a process from disk
explanation: Dispatch latency is the time it takes for the dispatcher to stop one process and start another.

text: How does the Shortest Remaining Time First algorithm work?
options: It preempts the running process if a shorter one arrives
It runs each process for a fixed quantum then switches
It always selects the process with the highest priority
It processes all jobs in the order they were submitted
explanation: SRTF is the preemptive version of SJF, preempting when a new process has shorter remaining time.

text: What is the aging technique used to prevent starvation?
options: Gradually increasing priority of waiting processes
Decreasing the time quantum over system runtime
Removing long-waiting processes from the queue
Assigning random priorities at each scheduling event
explanation: Aging gradually increases the priority of waiting processes, ensuring they eventually get scheduled.

text: What is the effect of a very small time quantum in Round Robin?
options: High context switch overhead reducing throughput
Lower response time with no performance impact
Processes run to completion without interruption
CPU idle time increases significantly overall
explanation: A very small time quantum causes excessive context switching, reducing effective CPU throughput.

text: How does multilevel queue scheduling organize processes?
options: It separates processes into queues with different priorities
It uses a single queue with dynamically changing priorities
It schedules processes randomly from a shared pool
It executes all processes simultaneously on one CPU
explanation: Multilevel queue scheduling partitions processes into separate queues based on priority or type.

text: What distinguishes multilevel feedback queue from multilevel queue?
options: Processes can move between queues based on behavior
Processes are permanently assigned to one queue only
All queues use the same scheduling algorithm always
Processes in lower queues are never executed at all
explanation: In multilevel feedback queue scheduling, processes can move between queues based on CPU burst behavior.

text: How is response time different from turnaround time?
options: Response time measures until first output is produced
Response time measures total time including all I/O
Response time only counts time spent in blocked state
Response time equals turnaround time minus CPU burst
explanation: Response time measures time from submission until the first response, while turnaround time measures total time to completion.

text: What is the problem with using pure priority scheduling?
options: Low-priority processes may starve indefinitely
High-priority processes get too little CPU time
The scheduling algorithm runs in exponential time
It requires knowledge of future CPU burst lengths
explanation: Pure priority scheduling can lead to indefinite starvation of low-priority processes. Aging can mitigate this.

text: How does the exponential averaging formula predict next CPU burst?
options: It weights recent burst length more than historical average
It uses only the most recent CPU burst measurement
It calculates the mean of all previous burst lengths
It randomly estimates the next burst for each process
explanation: Exponential averaging gives adjustable weight to the most recent burst and historical prediction.

text: What scheduling criterion does SJF optimize?
options: It minimizes the average waiting time for processes
It maximizes the throughput of the system overall
It minimizes the response time for interactive users
It maximizes CPU utilization at all times equally
explanation: SJF is provably optimal for minimizing average waiting time among non-preemptive scheduling algorithms.

text: What is processor affinity in multiprocessor scheduling?
options: A process prefers to run on the same CPU it ran before
A process must always run on the highest-numbered CPU
A process is evenly distributed across all available CPUs
A process can only execute on a single specific CPU
explanation: Processor affinity keeps a process on the same processor to take advantage of warm caches.

text: What is the difference between hard and soft processor affinity?
options: Hard affinity forces a CPU; soft affinity is a preference
Hard affinity is faster; soft affinity is more reliable
Hard affinity uses caching; soft affinity avoids caching
Hard affinity is for I/O; soft affinity is for CPU tasks
explanation: Hard affinity binds a process to specific processors. Soft affinity is a preference the OS may override.

text: How does the Linux O(1) scheduler achieve constant-time scheduling?
options: It uses two arrays of queues indexed by priority level
It uses a balanced binary tree sorted by virtual runtime
It scans all processes linearly for highest priority
It maintains a single sorted list of all runnable processes
explanation: The O(1) scheduler uses two arrays of 140 priority-indexed queues with a bitmap for constant-time selection.

text: What is the EDF scheduling guarantee for real-time systems?
options: It can schedule any feasible task set up to 100% utilization
It guarantees all tasks meet deadlines regardless of load
It requires tasks to have identical period and deadline
It only works with a maximum of two real-time tasks
explanation: EDF is an optimal dynamic-priority algorithm that can schedule any task set with total utilization at most 100%.

text: What is priority inversion and how does priority inheritance solve it?
options: A high-priority task waits for low-priority; inheritance raises priority
A low-priority task gets highest priority by mistake
Two tasks swap priorities causing scheduling confusion
The scheduler inverts the queue order during high load
explanation: Priority inversion occurs when a high-priority task blocks on a resource held by a low-priority task. Inheritance temporarily raises the holder priority.

text: What is the Rate Monotonic Scheduling utilization bound for n tasks?
options: n(2^(1/n) - 1) which approaches ln(2) as n grows
100% utilization regardless of the number of tasks
1/n utilization decreasing linearly with task count
n squared divided by the total number of processors
explanation: RMS has a utilization bound of n(2^(1/n) - 1), converging to ln(2) approximately 0.693 as n approaches infinity.

text: How does gang scheduling improve parallel application performance?
options: It co-schedules all threads of a parallel job simultaneously
It assigns each thread to a separate dedicated processor
It prioritizes parallel jobs over all sequential jobs
It merges multiple parallel jobs into a single process
explanation: Gang scheduling simultaneously schedules all threads of a parallel application across multiple processors.

text: What distinguishes work-conserving from non-work-conserving schedulers?
options: Work-conserving never idles the CPU if work is available
Work-conserving reserves CPU time for future arrivals
Non-work-conserving always keeps the CPU fully utilized
Non-work-conserving ignores the ready queue entirely
explanation: A work-conserving scheduler never leaves the CPU idle when there are runnable processes.

text: How does NUMA-aware scheduling improve performance?
options: It schedules processes near their allocated memory nodes
It distributes all processes evenly across all NUMA nodes
It moves memory pages to match process CPU assignments
It disables remote memory access for all processes
explanation: NUMA-aware scheduling places processes on CPUs close to their allocated memory nodes, minimizing remote access latency.

text: What is the proportional share scheduling approach?
options: Each process receives CPU time proportional to its shares
Each process receives exactly equal CPU time always
Processes bid for CPU time in a real-time auction
The scheduler randomly distributes CPU time slices
explanation: Proportional share scheduling allocates CPU time proportional to the number of shares each process holds.

text: What is the problem with using SJF in practice?
options: Future CPU burst lengths cannot be known precisely
It causes excessive context switching between processes
It always produces the worst possible average wait time
It requires all processes to have identical burst lengths
explanation: SJF is impractical because the length of the next CPU burst cannot be known in advance, only estimated.

text: What is a thread in an operating system?
options: A lightweight unit of execution within a process
A complete program running independently on disk
A hardware component that stores data permanently
A network connection between two remote computers
explanation: A thread is the smallest unit of CPU execution within a process, sharing the process resources.

text: What resources do threads within the same process share?
options: Code section, data section, and open files
Stack space and program counter values
Register contents and thread-local storage
Thread ID and individual signal handlers
explanation: Threads share the code section, data section, heap, and open files, while each has its own stack and registers.

text: What is the advantage of using threads over processes?
options: Thread creation is faster than process creation
Threads provide better memory isolation than processes
Threads use separate address spaces for safety
Threads cannot share data with other threads
explanation: Thread creation and context switching are faster because threads share the same address space.

text: What is a user-level thread?
options: A thread managed by a user-space library, not the kernel
A thread created directly by the hardware CPU
A thread that can only run with root privileges
A thread that manages the graphical user interface
explanation: User-level threads are managed by a thread library in user space without kernel awareness.

text: What is a kernel-level thread?
options: A thread managed and scheduled by the OS kernel
A thread that can only execute kernel code
A thread that runs in user space exclusively
A thread created by the hardware at boot time
explanation: Kernel-level threads are managed by the OS kernel, enabling true parallelism on multiprocessors.

text: What is multithreading in operating systems?
options: A process executing multiple threads concurrently
Running multiple operating systems simultaneously
Using multiple monitors for display purposes
Connecting multiple computers over a network
explanation: Multithreading allows a process to execute multiple threads concurrently, each performing a different task.

text: Which of the following is unique to each thread?
options: Its own stack and program counter
The process code and data segment
Open file descriptors and handles
Heap memory allocated to the process
explanation: Each thread has its own stack and program counter, while sharing other process resources.

text: What is the many-to-one threading model?
options: Many user threads map to one kernel thread
Many kernel threads map to one user thread
Each user thread maps to one kernel thread
One user thread maps to many kernel threads
explanation: In the many-to-one model, multiple user threads map to a single kernel thread, limiting parallelism.

text: Why is multithreading beneficial for a web server?
options: Each client request can be handled by a separate thread
The server can only handle one request at a time
Threads eliminate the need for network connections
Each thread runs on a separate physical server
explanation: A multithreaded web server handles multiple clients concurrently without creating separate processes.

text: What is a single-threaded process?
options: A process that contains only one thread of execution
A process that cannot perform any computations
A process that is stored only on the hard disk
A process that runs across multiple computers
explanation: A single-threaded process has only one thread with one program counter, performing one task at a time.

text: What is the one-to-one threading model and its main drawback?
options: Each user thread maps to a kernel thread; high overhead from many kernel threads
Each kernel thread maps to many user threads; limited concurrency
All threads share a single kernel thread; no parallelism
One user thread runs on many CPUs; excessive context switching
explanation: The one-to-one model provides true concurrency but creating many kernel threads adds significant overhead.

text: What is the purpose of thread pools in concurrent applications?
options: Pre-created threads wait for tasks reducing creation overhead
Threads are pooled to share a single stack among them all
Thread pools prevent any new threads from being created
Thread pools merge all threads into a single execution unit
explanation: Thread pools maintain pre-created threads that wait for work assignments, avoiding repeated creation overhead.

text: How does the many-to-many threading model improve on others?
options: It maps many user threads to a smaller set of kernel threads
It eliminates the need for kernel threads completely
It restricts each process to using only one thread
It requires hardware support for thread management
explanation: The many-to-many model combines scalability of user threads with parallelism of kernel threads.

text: What happens when a user thread blocks in the many-to-one model?
options: The entire process blocks because the kernel thread blocks
Only the calling thread blocks while others continue
The system call is converted to a non-blocking call
The kernel creates a new thread to handle the call
explanation: In the many-to-one model, a blocking system call blocks the single kernel thread and all user threads.

text: What is thread-local storage (TLS) and when is it useful?
options: Per-thread data that persists across function calls
Shared memory accessible by all threads equally
A lock mechanism for synchronizing thread access
A cache for storing recently used thread contexts
explanation: TLS provides each thread with its own copy of a variable, useful when threads need private persistent data.

text: What is the difference between implicit and explicit threading?
options: Implicit threading is managed by compilers or libraries automatically
Implicit threading requires programmers to create each thread
Explicit threading uses automatic parallelization by hardware
Explicit threading hides concurrency details from developers
explanation: Implicit threading transfers thread management to compilers and runtime libraries like OpenMP.

text: How does fork() behave in a multithreaded process?
options: Behavior varies: it may duplicate only the calling thread or all
It always duplicates every thread in the parent process
It terminates all threads except the one calling fork
It creates a new process with no threads at all
explanation: POSIX specifies fork() duplicates only the calling thread, which can cause issues with locks held by other threads.

text: What is the role of the scheduler activation mechanism?
options: It communicates kernel events to the user-level thread library
It replaces the kernel scheduler with a user-level scheduler
It activates the CPU scheduler only when threads are ready
It disables scheduling for threads waiting on I/O
explanation: Scheduler activations notify the user-level thread library about kernel events like thread blocking.

text: What issue arises with signal handling in multithreaded programs?
options: It is ambiguous which thread should receive the signal
Signals are always delivered to every thread in the process
Signals cannot be used in multithreaded programs at all
Only the main thread can send signals to the kernel
explanation: Signal handling is complex because the OS must decide which thread should receive each signal.
