text: What is the advantage of lightweight processes in the two-level model?
options: They provide a bridge between user threads and kernel threads
They replace all kernel threads with faster alternatives
They eliminate context switching between threads entirely
They allow threads to access hardware registers directly
explanation: LWPs act as virtual processors between user-level and kernel-level threads.

text: What is the C10K problem and how do threads relate to it?
options: Handling 10K connections; threads per connection scales poorly
Creating 10K threads per second; context switching fails
Running 10K processes on one CPU; memory is insufficient
Transmitting 10K packets per second; bandwidth is limited
explanation: The C10K problem shows thread-per-connection models scale poorly due to memory overhead and context switching costs.

text: How do coroutines differ from threads in terms of scheduling?
options: Coroutines are cooperatively scheduled by the programmer
Coroutines are preemptively scheduled by the kernel
Coroutines cannot yield control to other coroutines
Coroutines require hardware support for context switching
explanation: Unlike preemptively scheduled threads, coroutines use cooperative scheduling where they explicitly yield control.

text: What is the purpose of the futex mechanism in Linux threading?
options: It provides fast user-space locking with kernel fallback
It replaces all POSIX thread synchronization primitives
It manages thread creation and destruction in user space
It provides hardware-level thread scheduling support
explanation: A futex allows synchronization in user space for the uncontended case, falling back to kernel only when blocking.

text: What challenge does false sharing cause in multithreaded applications?
options: Threads on different cores invalidate each other cache lines
Threads accidentally share the same virtual memory address
The OS incorrectly reports that threads share data
Thread-local storage is overwritten by neighboring threads
explanation: False sharing occurs when threads modify different variables on the same cache line, causing unnecessary invalidation.

text: How does work-stealing improve parallel task scheduling?
options: Idle threads steal tasks from busy threads local queues
The scheduler redistributes all tasks equally at intervals
Threads donate excess tasks to a global shared queue
The OS migrates threads between CPUs to balance load
explanation: In work-stealing, idle threads steal tasks from the back of other busy threads queues for good load balancing.

text: What is the memory consistency challenge in multithreaded programs?
options: Processors may reorder memory operations causing unexpected behavior
All memory writes are immediately visible to all threads
Threads always see memory updates in program order
The OS guarantees sequential consistency for all operations
explanation: Modern CPUs may reorder memory operations, requiring explicit memory barriers for correctness.

text: What is thread starvation and how can it occur with locks?
options: A thread never acquires a lock because others keep taking it
A thread consumes all available CPU time indefinitely
A thread terminates prematurely due to memory exhaustion
A thread blocks permanently because the lock was destroyed
explanation: Thread starvation occurs when a thread is perpetually unable to acquire a needed lock.

text: How does the Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) affect Python threading?
options: It prevents multiple threads from executing Python bytecode simultaneously
It makes Python threads faster than native OS threads
It allows Python to use all CPU cores for parallelism
It eliminates all race conditions in Python programs
explanation: The GIL allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time, preventing true CPU parallelism.

text: What is the advantage of M:N threading over 1:1 for high concurrency?
options: It allows more concurrent entities with less kernel overhead
It provides stronger memory isolation between threads
It guarantees that all threads run on separate CPU cores
It simplifies debugging by reducing concurrency issues
explanation: M:N threading maps many user threads to fewer kernel threads, reducing kernel overhead for highly concurrent workloads.

text: What is the thundering herd problem in thread synchronization?
options: All waiting threads wake up when a condition is signaled
Only one thread wakes up but it holds the wrong lock
Threads continuously create new threads overwhelming resources
The scheduler repeatedly switches between the same two threads
explanation: When a condition variable is broadcast, all blocked threads wake up but typically only one can proceed.

text: What is a race condition in concurrent programming?
options: When the outcome depends on the timing of thread execution
When two threads run at exactly the same speed always
When a single thread executes faster than expected
When the CPU processes instructions out of order
explanation: A race condition occurs when program behavior depends on the relative timing of multiple threads.

text: What is the critical section of a process?
options: A code segment where shared resources are accessed
The first line of code in a program main function
The section of code that handles error conditions
The code that initializes all program variables
explanation: The critical section is where a process accesses shared resources that must not be concurrently accessed.

text: What does mutual exclusion ensure?
options: Only one process executes in its critical section at a time
All processes can enter their critical sections simultaneously
No process is ever allowed to enter its critical section
Processes execute their critical sections in random order
explanation: Mutual exclusion ensures that only one process at a time can be in its critical section.

text: What is a mutex lock used for?
options: To protect critical sections from concurrent access
To speed up execution of single-threaded programs
To allocate additional memory for running processes
To schedule processes in first-come first-served order
explanation: A mutex lock allows only one thread at a time to access a critical section, preventing race conditions.

text: What is a semaphore in operating systems?
options: An integer variable used for signaling between processes
A physical device that connects two computers together
A type of memory chip used for cache storage
A scheduling algorithm for real-time operating systems
explanation: A semaphore is an integer variable accessed through atomic wait and signal operations for synchronization.

text: What is the difference between binary and counting semaphores?
options: Binary has values 0 or 1; counting can be any non-negative value
Binary counts up; counting counts down
Binary is faster; counting is slower
Binary is for threads; counting is for processes
explanation: A binary semaphore has values 0 or 1, while a counting semaphore can have any non-negative integer value.

text: What is busy waiting (spinlock) in synchronization?
options: A process continuously tests a condition in a loop
A process sleeps until a condition becomes true
A process terminates when it cannot acquire a lock
A process yields the CPU to another waiting process
explanation: Busy waiting occurs when a process continuously checks a condition in a tight loop, consuming CPU cycles.

text: What is the producer-consumer problem?
options: Coordinating processes that produce and consume shared data
Scheduling CPU time between two competing processes
Allocating memory between the kernel and user space
Managing disk space between system and user files
explanation: The producer-consumer problem involves coordinating data production and consumption using a shared buffer.

text: What does the wait() operation perform on a semaphore?
options: It decrements the semaphore and blocks if value is negative
It increments the semaphore and unblocks a waiting process
It resets the semaphore value back to its initial state
It destroys the semaphore and frees its memory resources
explanation: The wait (P) operation decrements the semaphore. If the result is negative, the process blocks.

text: What does the signal() operation perform on a semaphore?
options: It increments the semaphore and may wake a waiting process
It decrements the semaphore and blocks the calling process
It deletes the semaphore from the operating system
It checks the semaphore value without changing it at all
explanation: The signal (V) operation increments the semaphore and may wake a blocked process.

text: What are the three requirements for a valid critical section solution?
options: Mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting
Mutual exclusion, fairness, and starvation freedom
Atomicity, consistency, and isolation properties
Deadlock freedom, livelock freedom, and safety
explanation: A correct solution must satisfy mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting.

text: How does Peterson solution achieve mutual exclusion for two processes?
options: Using a turn variable and two flag variables together
Using a single shared counter variable only
Using hardware compare-and-swap instructions
Using operating system semaphore system calls
explanation: Peterson solution uses two flag variables and a turn variable to ensure mutual exclusion for two processes.

text: What is the readers-writers problem about?
options: Allowing concurrent reads but exclusive writes to shared data
Preventing all access to shared data at all times
Ensuring only one reader can access data at a time
Allowing concurrent writes but exclusive reads to data
explanation: The readers-writers problem allows multiple concurrent readers but requires exclusive writer access.

text: How does the test-and-set instruction help in synchronization?
options: It atomically tests and modifies a memory location in one step
It tests a variable and sets it in two separate instructions
It only tests the value without modifying it at all
It sets a variable to zero and then tests if it changed
explanation: Test-and-set is an atomic hardware instruction that reads and sets a value in one indivisible step.

text: What problem does the dining philosophers problem illustrate?
options: Resource allocation deadlock among competing processes
Memory fragmentation in dynamic allocation systems
Priority inversion in real-time scheduling systems
Buffer overflow in network communication protocols
explanation: The dining philosophers illustrates challenges of allocating shared resources without deadlock or starvation.

text: What is a monitor in process synchronization?
options: A high-level construct encapsulating shared data with procedures
A hardware device that displays process status information
A system program that monitors CPU temperature levels
A kernel thread that watches for suspicious activity
explanation: A monitor bundles shared data and access procedures, ensuring mutual exclusion automatically.

text: What are condition variables used for within a monitor?
options: They allow processes to wait for specific conditions to be true
They store the values of shared variables in the monitor
They count the number of processes waiting for the monitor
They provide mutual exclusion for the monitor procedures
explanation: Condition variables let processes wait until a specific condition is satisfied and signal other waiters.

text: What is the compare-and-swap (CAS) atomic instruction?
options: It atomically compares a value and swaps it if it matches
It compares two memory locations and swaps their contents
It swaps the values of two CPU registers simultaneously
It compares process priorities and swaps their positions
explanation: CAS atomically compares a memory location to an expected value and replaces it if they match.

text: Why is disabling interrupts bad for mutual exclusion on multiprocessors?
options: It only affects the local CPU, not other processors
It permanently disables all interrupts causing failure
It is too slow to be practical for any real usage
It requires recompiling the kernel for each critical section
explanation: Disabling interrupts on one CPU does not prevent other CPUs from executing the same critical section.

text: What is a spinlock and when is it preferred over a blocking lock?
options: A lock using busy waiting; preferred when wait time is short
A lock that spins process priority; preferred for I/O tasks
A lock that blocks immediately; preferred for real-time
A lock using sleep; preferred when CPU has a single core
explanation: A spinlock uses busy waiting and is preferred when expected wait time is very short.

text: What is the ABA problem in lock-free programming?
options: A value changes A to B to A, fooling CAS operations
Two threads simultaneously execute the same atomic instruction
A thread reads a value never written by any thread
The CAS succeeds but the memory is actually corrupted
explanation: The ABA problem occurs when CAS sees expected value A but another thread changed it to B and back.

text: How does Read-Copy-Update (RCU) achieve synchronization?
options: Writers create new versions; old freed after readers finish
Writers block until all readers release their locks
Readers and writers use the same mutex for access
Readers create copies of data before writers modify it
explanation: RCU lets readers access data without locks. Writers create updated copies; old versions freed after a grace period.

text: What is the difference between strong and weak memory ordering?
options: Strong guarantees sequential consistency; weak allows reordering
Strong uses more memory; weak uses less for variables
Strong is for writes only; weak is for reads only
Strong requires hardware support; weak is purely software
explanation: Strong memory ordering provides near-sequential consistency, while weak ordering allows more aggressive reordering.

text: What is a hazard pointer and how does it prevent premature reclamation?
options: It marks pointers as in-use so reclaimers skip them
It adds an extra indirection level to all pointer accesses
It copies the pointed-to data before the pointer is freed
It locks the memory region to prevent any modifications
explanation: Hazard pointers mark data as being accessed so memory reclaimers skip them.

text: What is the consensus number of a compare-and-swap operation?
options: Infinite: CAS can solve consensus for any number of threads
Two: CAS solves consensus for at most two threads
One: CAS solves consensus for a single thread only
Zero: CAS cannot solve the consensus problem at all
explanation: CAS has an infinite consensus number, making it a universal synchronization primitive.

text: What is the problem with the first readers-writers solution?
options: Writers can starve if readers continuously arrive
Readers can starve because writers get higher priority
Both get equal access causing deadlock
The solution does not provide mutual exclusion for writers
explanation: In the first readers-writers problem, readers have priority so writers may starve if readers keep arriving.

text: How does transactional memory simplify concurrent programming?
options: It allows speculative execution of critical sections like transactions
It replaces all locks with hardware memory encryption
It eliminates the need for any shared memory between threads
It guarantees all memory operations complete in constant time
explanation: Transactional memory lets programmers define atomic blocks that execute speculatively with automatic rollback on conflict.

text: What is the lock-free guarantee in concurrent data structures?
options: At least one thread makes progress in a finite number of steps
All threads make progress simultaneously at every step
No thread ever needs to wait for any other thread
The structure never uses any CPU instructions for locking
explanation: Lock-free guarantees at least one thread will complete its operation in a finite number of steps.

text: What is a sequence lock (seqlock) and when is it appropriate?
options: Writers increment counter; readers retry if counter changed
Readers acquire a sequence of locks in predetermined order
Writers wait for all readers before modifying shared data
Readers lock data in sequence to prevent writer access
explanation: A seqlock uses a sequence counter for writer-favored synchronization, good for rarely-written data.

text: What is the difference between obstruction-free and wait-free algorithms?
options: Wait-free guarantees all threads finish; obstruction-free only isolated ones
Obstruction-free is faster than wait-free algorithms
Wait-free allows starvation; obstruction-free prevents it
Both provide identical progress guarantees
explanation: Wait-free guarantees every thread completes in bounded steps. Obstruction-free only guarantees progress in isolation.

text: What is a deadlock in operating systems?
options: A state where processes are blocked waiting for each other
A state where all processes are running simultaneously
A state where the CPU is idle with no processes ready
A state where memory is completely full and unused
explanation: A deadlock occurs when processes are unable to proceed because each waits for a resource held by another.

text: How many conditions must hold for a deadlock to occur?
options: Four conditions must hold simultaneously
Two conditions must hold simultaneously
Three conditions must hold simultaneously
Only one condition needs to hold
explanation: All four conditions must hold simultaneously for a deadlock to occur.

text: What is the hold and wait condition for deadlocks?
options: A process holds resources while waiting for additional ones
A process releases all resources before requesting new ones
A process waits without holding any resources at all
A process holds resources but never waits for others
explanation: Hold and wait means a process holds at least one resource while waiting for additional resources.

text: What is the circular wait condition for deadlocks?
options: A chain of processes where each waits for the next
All processes wait for the same single resource
Processes wait in a straight line without any cycle
Only two processes are involved in the waiting chain
explanation: Circular wait means a circular chain exists where each process holds a resource the next one needs.

text: What is resource preemption in deadlock context?
options: Taking a resource away from a process to break deadlock
Giving a process additional resources to complete faster
Preventing a process from ever requesting any resources
Destroying a resource so no process can use it again
explanation: Resource preemption forcibly takes a resource from a process to break a deadlock.

text: What is a resource allocation graph used for?
options: Visualizing resource allocation and request relationships
Calculating the speed of resource access by processes
Measuring memory usage of each running process
Displaying the file system directory structure
explanation: A resource allocation graph visualizes which processes hold and request which resources.

text: What does the no preemption condition mean for deadlocks?
options: Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a process
Resources are always taken from any process immediately
Processes voluntarily give up resources when asked
The OS never allocates resources to any process
explanation: No preemption means allocated resources cannot be forcibly taken; they are only released voluntarily.

text: What is the mutual exclusion condition for deadlocks?
options: At least one resource must be held in non-sharable mode
All resources can be shared among multiple processes
Resources are only used by the operating system kernel
No process is allowed to hold any resources at all
explanation: Mutual exclusion means at least one resource is non-sharable.

text: What is deadlock prevention?
options: Ensuring at least one necessary condition can never hold
Detecting deadlocks after they occur and recovering
Ignoring deadlocks and hoping they do not happen
Allowing deadlocks but limiting them to two processes
explanation: Deadlock prevention ensures at least one of the four necessary conditions cannot be satisfied.

text: What is the simplest approach to handling deadlocks?
options: Ignoring deadlocks entirely as in the ostrich algorithm
Detecting and killing all processes in the system
Preventing all four conditions from ever holding
Using the Banker Algorithm for every allocation
explanation: The ostrich algorithm ignores deadlocks, used when they are rare and prevention cost is too high.

text: How can the hold-and-wait condition be prevented?
options: Require processes to request all resources before starting
Allow processes to hold resources indefinitely
Permit processes to request resources in any order
Let processes acquire resources one at a time as needed
explanation: Hold-and-wait can be prevented by requiring all resources be requested before execution begins.

text: How does total ordering on resources prevent circular wait?
options: Processes must request resources in increasing order only
Processes can request resources in any order they prefer
Resources are numbered but the order is not enforced
Only the highest-numbered resource can be requested first
explanation: Requiring resources to be requested in strictly increasing order prevents cycles in the request graph.

text: In a resource allocation graph, what does a cycle indicate?
options: A potential deadlock if resources have single instances
A guaranteed deadlock regardless of resource instances
No possibility of deadlock under any circumstances
An efficient allocation of resources to processes
explanation: A cycle indicates potential deadlock with single-instance resources; with multiple instances it is necessary but not sufficient.

text: What is the difference between deadlock detection and avoidance?
options: Detection finds existing deadlocks; avoidance prevents unsafe states
Detection prevents deadlocks; avoidance finds them after
Detection is proactive; avoidance is reactive to deadlocks
Detection runs at boot; avoidance runs at shutdown
explanation: Detection identifies deadlocks after occurrence; avoidance proactively prevents unsafe states.

text: What data structures does the Banker Algorithm use?
options: Available, Max, Allocation, and Need matrices
Only a single resource allocation graph structure
A priority queue and a hash table for resources
A binary tree of processes and linked list of resources
explanation: The Banker Algorithm uses Available, Max, Allocation, and Need matrices.

text: How does deadlock recovery by process termination work?
options: Abort deadlocked processes one by one until deadlock breaks
Terminate all processes including non-deadlocked ones
Restart the entire operating system from scratch
Move deadlocked processes to a different CPU
explanation: Recovery can abort all deadlocked processes or abort them one at a time until the cycle is broken.

text: What is the wait-for graph used for in deadlock detection?
options: It is a simplified RAG showing only process dependencies
It tracks how long each process has been waiting
It displays the order in which processes were created
It shows which resources are available for allocation
explanation: A wait-for graph shows only process dependencies, making cycle detection faster.

text: What is the cost of using the Banker Algorithm?
options: It requires checking safety state for each resource request
It only needs to run once during system initialization
It has no computational cost beyond simple comparisons
It requires dedicated hardware to function correctly
explanation: The Banker Algorithm must perform a safety check for each resource request, adding significant overhead.

text: How does resource preemption recover from deadlock?
options: Resources are taken from some processes and given to others
All resources in the system are destroyed and recreated
Processes voluntarily give up resources when asked
The OS doubles the number of available resource instances
explanation: Resource preemption forcibly takes resources from deadlocked processes to break the cycle.

text: What is the difference between safe and unsafe states?
options: Safe guarantees no deadlock; unsafe may lead to deadlock
Safe has no running processes; unsafe has many
Safe uses minimum memory; unsafe uses maximum
Safe is faster; unsafe is slower in execution
explanation: In a safe state, a sequence exists that avoids deadlock. An unsafe state means no safe sequence exists.

text: What is the phantom deadlock problem in distributed systems?
options: A detected deadlock that no longer exists due to message delays
A deadlock in virtual machines but not on real hardware
A deadlock that appears in resource graph but is not real
A deadlock caused by phantom processes that do not exist
explanation: Phantom deadlocks occur when communication delays cause detection of a cycle that has already been resolved.

text: How does the wound-wait scheme handle deadlock prevention?
options: Older transactions wound younger; younger wait for older
Younger transactions always preempt older transactions
All transactions are aborted and restarted simultaneously
Transactions are executed sequentially to avoid conflicts
explanation: In wound-wait, older transactions wound (rollback) younger ones holding needed resources.

text: What is the difference between wait-die and wound-wait schemes?
options: Wait-die: older waits, younger dies; wound-wait reverses this
Wait-die and wound-wait use identical strategies
Wait-die only prevents; wound-wait only detects deadlocks
Wait-die uses timestamps; wound-wait uses random selection
explanation: In wait-die, older transactions wait and younger ones die. Wound-wait reverses this.

text: How does the Chandy-Misra-Haas algorithm detect distributed deadlocks?
options: It propagates probe messages along wait-for edges across nodes
It uses a centralized coordinator to collect wait-for graphs
It periodically checkpoints all processes for analysis
It prevents deadlocks by ordering all distributed resources
explanation: The algorithm propagates probes along wait-for edges. If a probe returns to its initiator, a deadlock exists.

text: What is the complexity of deadlock detection using a wait-for graph?
options: O(n^2) where n is the number of processes
O(1) constant time regardless of process count
O(n log n) using a balanced tree approach
O(2^n) exponential in the number of processes
explanation: Cycle detection using DFS is O(n + e), which in worst case for a dense graph is O(n^2).

text: What is the problem of selecting a victim for deadlock recovery?
options: Choosing the minimum-cost process is computationally hard
Any process can be chosen with equal effectiveness
The victim must always be the most recently created
Only processes without allocated resources can be victims
explanation: Selecting an optimal victim requires balancing priority, resources held, computation done, and remaining work.

text: How does rollback work in deadlock recovery?
options: A process returns to a safe checkpointed state losing some work
A process is terminated and restarted from the beginning
The entire system is rolled back to the initial boot state
Only the last instruction of the process is undone
explanation: Rollback returns a preempted process to a previously saved checkpoint, losing computation done since then.

text: What is livelock and how does it differ from deadlock?
options: Processes keep changing state but make no progress; deadlock blocks them
Processes complete too quickly consuming all resources
One process monopolizes the CPU preventing others
All processes terminate unexpectedly from exhaustion
explanation: In a livelock, processes continuously change state without progress, unlike deadlock where they are completely blocked.

text: What is the two-phase locking protocol relationship to deadlocks?
options: It ensures serializability but does not prevent deadlocks
It prevents all deadlocks and ensures serializability
It detects deadlocks but not ensures serializability
It has no relationship to deadlocks or serializability
explanation: Two-phase locking guarantees serializability but does not prevent deadlocks.

text: Why is detection algorithm invocation frequency a design trade-off?
options: Frequent checks find deadlocks sooner but waste CPU resources
Frequent checks always improve system performance
Infrequent checks find deadlocks faster than frequent ones
The frequency has no impact on system performance
explanation: Running detection frequently finds deadlocks quickly but consumes CPU. Running infrequently wastes resources on blocked processes.

text: What is virtual memory in operating systems?
options: A technique that uses disk to extend physical memory
A type of RAM chip faster than regular memory
The total amount of cache memory in the CPU
A backup copy of all data stored in main memory
explanation: Virtual memory allows processes to use more memory than physically available by using disk space.

text: What is a page in virtual memory?
options: A fixed-size block of virtual memory
A variable-size segment of physical memory
A unit of disk storage for file systems
A section of CPU cache for instructions
explanation: A page is a fixed-size block of virtual memory that maps to physical frames.

text: What is a page frame in physical memory?
options: A fixed-size block of physical memory holding a page
A variable-size region for storing page tables
A special register in the CPU for addressing
A buffer in the disk controller for caching
explanation: A page frame is a fixed-size block of physical memory corresponding to a virtual page size.

text: What is a page fault in virtual memory?
options: An access to a page not currently in physical memory
An error in the page table structure itself
A failure of the memory hardware component
A page that has been corrupted by a process
explanation: A page fault occurs when a process accesses a page not currently loaded in physical memory.

text: What is the purpose of the page table?
options: It maps virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers
It stores the contents of each virtual memory page
It lists all processes currently using memory
It records the total free memory in the system
explanation: The page table maps each virtual page number to its corresponding physical frame number.

text: What is internal fragmentation?
options: Wasted space within allocated memory blocks
Wasted space between allocated memory blocks
Memory corruption inside a running process
Disk space wasted by the file system structure
explanation: Internal fragmentation is wasted space within allocated blocks that are larger than needed.

text: What is external fragmentation?
options: Free memory scattered in small non-contiguous blocks
Memory inside allocated blocks is wasted and unused
The disk has too many fragments from deleted files
Cache memory becomes fragmented reducing CPU speed
explanation: External fragmentation occurs when free memory is scattered, preventing large contiguous allocations.

text: What is the base register used for in memory management?
options: It holds the starting physical address of a process
It stores the total size of the physical memory
It contains the page table pointer for the process
It records the number of page faults that occurred
explanation: The base register holds the smallest legal physical memory address for a process.

text: What is logical address space?
options: The set of all addresses generated by a program
The set of all physical memory locations in RAM
The total disk space available for virtual memory
The addresses used by the CPU cache subsystem
explanation: The logical address space is the set of all addresses generated by a program during execution.

text: What does the MMU do?
options: It translates logical addresses to physical addresses
It manages the CPU scheduling of processes
It controls the input and output of devices
It stores the operating system kernel code
explanation: The MMU translates virtual addresses generated by the CPU to physical addresses in main memory.

text: How does LRU page replacement decide which page to evict?
options: It replaces the page not used for the longest time
It replaces the most recently loaded page
It replaces a randomly selected page
It replaces the page with smallest page number
explanation: LRU replaces the page that has not been accessed for the longest time.

text: What is Belady anomaly in page replacement?
options: More frames can lead to more page faults with FIFO
Fewer frames always lead to more page faults
More frames always lead to fewer page faults
Page faults are independent of available frames
explanation: Belady anomaly is the counterintuitive phenomenon where more frames can increase page faults with FIFO.

text: What is the dirty bit in a page table entry?
options: It indicates whether the page has been modified
It marks the page as corrupted needing replacement
It shows whether the page is in physical memory
It records how many times the page was accessed
explanation: The dirty bit indicates whether a page was modified since loading, determining if it needs write-back.

text: How does a two-level page table reduce memory overhead?
options: It only allocates entries for used address regions
It stores all entries in a single contiguous block
It compresses entries to use fewer bits each
It shares one page table among all processes
explanation: A two-level page table only allocates second-level tables for address regions actually in use.

text: What is demand paging and how does it differ from pre-paging?
options: Demand paging loads pages when accessed; pre-paging loads in advance
Demand paging loads all at startup; pre-paging loads on demand
Demand paging uses more disk I/O; pre-paging uses none
Demand paging is for kernel; pre-paging is for user pages
explanation: Demand paging loads pages only when first accessed; pre-paging speculatively loads pages in advance.

text: What is the clock (second-chance) page replacement algorithm?
options: It uses a circular list and reference bit for second chances
It replaces the earliest loaded page without any check
It replaces the most recently accessed page
It uses a timer to replace pages after a fixed period
explanation: The clock algorithm checks the reference bit: if set, clears it (second chance); if unset, replaces.

text: What is the purpose of segmentation in memory management?
options: It divides memory into variable-size logical segments
It divides memory into fixed-size pages for uniformity
It encrypts memory contents for process security
It compresses memory to increase available space
explanation: Segmentation divides a process memory into variable-sized segments based on logical divisions.

text: What is the effective access time formula with page faults?
options: EAT = (1-p) * memory_access + p * page_fault_time
EAT = p * memory_access + (1-p) * page_fault_time
EAT = memory_access + page_fault_time divided by two
EAT = memory_access multiplied by page_fault_time
explanation: EAT accounts for page fault probability: even small fault rates significantly impact performance.

text: What is memory-mapped I/O?
options: Mapping device registers to addresses in memory space
Copying all I/O data into RAM before processing
Using DMA to bypass the CPU for all I/O operations
Encrypting I/O data before writing it to memory
explanation: Memory-mapped I/O maps device registers to memory addresses, allowing standard load/store instructions.

text: What is the difference between first-fit and best-fit allocation?
options: First-fit allocates first adequate block; best-fit the smallest
First-fit always wastes more memory than best-fit
Best-fit is always faster than first-fit in practice
First-fit finds the largest; best-fit finds the first
explanation: First-fit takes the first adequate block; best-fit searches for the smallest sufficient block.

text: How does the inverted page table reduce memory usage?
options: One entry per physical frame instead of per virtual page
It compresses virtual page numbers using hash functions
It stores only dirty pages discarding clean entries
It shares one table among all processes without index
explanation: An inverted page table has one entry per physical frame, dramatically reducing table size.

text: What is copy-on-write in memory management?
options: Shared pages are copied only when a process writes to them
All pages are immediately copied when a new process is created
Pages are copied from disk during every read access
The OS creates backup copies of all pages periodically
explanation: COW allows forked processes to share physical pages until one modifies a page, triggering a copy.

text: What is the TLB reach?
options: Amount of memory accessible through TLB entries times page size
Physical distance between the TLB and CPU core
Maximum page faults the TLB can handle per second
Time for the TLB to complete a single address lookup
explanation: TLB reach is the number of TLB entries multiplied by the page size.

text: What is the purpose of page coloring?
options: It maps virtual pages to frames that avoid cache conflicts
It marks pages with colors to indicate access frequency
It prioritizes pages based on the owning process
It groups pages by size for efficient disk swapping
explanation: Page coloring distributes cache line usage evenly, minimizing cache conflicts.

text: How does kernel same-page merging (KSM) optimize virtualization memory?
options: It identifies identical pages across VMs and shares them with COW
It compresses all kernel pages to half their size
It merges kernel code of multiple VMs into one copy
It deduplicates only file system pages across the system
explanation: KSM scans for identical pages across VMs and merges them using copy-on-write.

text: What is the working set window parameter effect on performance?
options: It defines time interval for active pages; too small causes thrashing
It sets the maximum number of processes in memory
It determines the page replacement algorithm to use
It controls disk I/O bandwidth for swap operations
explanation: The working set window defines the interval for counting active pages. Too small causes thrashing.

text: What is the buddy system for memory allocation?
options: It splits and merges power-of-two sized blocks
It pairs each block with a backup block for reliability
It assigns two processes to share the same memory block
It uses two separate allocators for kernel and user memory
explanation: The buddy system allocates power-of-two blocks that can be merged with adjacent buddies when freed.

text: What is memory ballooning in VM memory management?
options: A balloon driver in the guest reclaims memory for the hypervisor
The hypervisor inflates VM memory beyond physical limits
Guest memory grows automatically without host involvement
Physical memory is compressed to appear larger
explanation: Memory ballooning uses a guest driver that inflates to return physical pages to the hypervisor.

text: What is the global vs local page replacement trade-off?
options: Global steals frames from any process; local only from the faulting one
Global is always faster; local is always more fair
Global uses FIFO only; local uses LRU only
Global replaces kernel pages; local replaces user pages
explanation: Global replacement can steal from any process; local limits replacement to the faulting process.

text: How do transparent huge pages (THP) improve performance?
options: They promote regular pages to huge pages reducing TLB misses
They make all allocations invisible to the process
They hide page fault handling from the application
They encrypt page table entries transparently for security
explanation: THP automatically merges contiguous regular pages into huge pages, reducing TLB misses.

text: What is a file system in an operating system?
options: A method for organizing and storing data on storage devices
A tool for editing text documents on the computer
A hardware component connecting the CPU to memory
A network protocol for transferring files between computers
explanation: A file system provides the structure for organizing, storing, naming, and retrieving data on storage devices.

text: What is a directory in a file system?
options: A container that organizes files and other directories
A special type of executable program file on disk
A hardware partition on the physical disk drive
A network location where remote files are stored
explanation: A directory contains references to files and other directories, providing hierarchical organization.

text: What does the file extension indicate?
options: The type or format of the file contents
The physical size of the file on the disk
The date when the file was last modified
The owner of the file in the system
explanation: A file extension indicates the type or format of a file.

text: What is the root directory in a file system?
options: The top-level directory from which all paths start
The directory where user home folders are stored
The directory containing only temporary system files
The directory that stores the operating system kernel
explanation: The root directory is the topmost directory from which all other directories branch out.

text: What is the difference between relative and absolute paths?
options: Absolute starts from root; relative from current directory
Absolute is shorter; relative is longer in length
Absolute is for files; relative is only for directories
Absolute uses letters; relative uses numbers
explanation: An absolute path starts from root; a relative path starts from the current working directory.

text: What are file access permissions used for?
options: Controlling who can read, write, or execute a file
Determining the physical location of the file on disk
Measuring how fast a file can be accessed
Setting the maximum size a file can grow to
explanation: File access permissions control which users can read, write, or execute a file.

text: What is the purpose of mounting a file system?
options: Making a storage device file system accessible at a mount point
Physically installing a hard drive inside the case
Creating a new file system on unformatted storage
Removing a storage device safely from the system
explanation: Mounting attaches a file system on a storage device to a directory in the existing hierarchy.

text: What is a file metadata?
options: Information about the file such as size dates and permissions
The actual data content stored within the file itself
The file extension at the end of the file name
The physical sectors on disk where the file is located
explanation: File metadata includes name, size, dates, permissions, and owner, stored separately from content.

text: What is sequential file access?
options: Reading file data in order from beginning to end
Reading file data from any position at random
Reading only the last record in the file first
Reading file data in reverse from end to start
explanation: Sequential access reads or writes data in order from beginning to end.

text: What does formatting a disk do?
options: It creates a file system structure on the storage device
It increases the physical storage capacity of the disk
It repairs damaged sectors on the disk surface
It encrypts all data currently stored on the disk
explanation: Formatting creates the necessary file system data structures, preparing the disk for file storage.

text: How does indexed allocation store file blocks?
options: An index block contains pointers to all data blocks
Data blocks are stored in a contiguous sequence
Each data block contains a pointer to the next
A bitmap tracks which blocks belong to each file
explanation: Indexed allocation uses an index block containing pointers to all data blocks of a file.

text: What is the purpose of a file allocation table (FAT)?
options: It tracks which disk blocks are allocated to each file
It stores the contents of all files in a single table
It manages user permissions for all files on disk
It compresses files to save storage space
explanation: The FAT maps file data to disk blocks using linked allocation.

text: What is an inode in Unix-like file systems?
options: A data structure storing file metadata and block pointers
A directory entry containing only the file name
A special file type for interprocess communication
A kernel structure for managing open files only
explanation: An inode stores file metadata and pointers to data blocks on disk.

text: How do hard links differ from symbolic links?
options: Hard links point to the inode; symbolic links to the path
Hard links can cross file systems; symbolic links cannot
Hard links work with directories; symbolic links do not
Hard links consume more disk space than symbolic links
explanation: A hard link points to the same inode; a symbolic link is a file containing the path to the target.

text: What is a journaling file system?
options: It logs changes before applying to prevent corruption on crashes
It keeps a journal of user activities for auditing
It compresses old files into a journal to save space
It records network transfers for performance analysis
explanation: A journaling file system records pending changes in a log before writing them.

text: What is the difference between contiguous and linked allocation?
options: Contiguous places blocks together; linked chains with pointers
Contiguous uses more space; linked uses less always
Contiguous is for small files; linked for large only
Contiguous is faster for sequential only; linked is always faster
explanation: Contiguous allocation places blocks adjacent; linked allocation chains them with pointers.

text: What does the VFS layer provide?
options: A common interface to different file system implementations
Direct access to hardware disk controllers for speed
Encryption of all files regardless of file system type
Automatic backup of files to remote cloud storage
explanation: The VFS provides a uniform interface for interacting with different file system types.

text: What is the purpose of the open file table?
options: It tracks all currently open files and their states
It lists all files stored on every mounted disk
It stores file permissions for all system users
It records which files have been recently deleted
explanation: The open file table maintains entries for all currently open files in the system.

text: What is free space management using a bitmap?
options: Each bit represents a block: 1 for free, 0 for allocated
A linked list of all free blocks on the disk
A table mapping file names to their free blocks
A counter tracking the total number of free blocks
explanation: A bitmap uses one bit per block to indicate allocation status.

text: What is direct access (random access) to a file?
options: Reading or writing any block without sequential traversal
Reading the file only from beginning to end
Writing data only at the end of the file
Accessing the file through a network remotely
explanation: Direct access allows reading or writing any block without traversing preceding records.

text: How does ext4 use extents instead of block pointers?
options: Extents describe contiguous ranges reducing metadata overhead
Extents compress file data to use fewer blocks
Extents encrypt block addresses for security
Extents duplicate blocks across disks for redundancy
explanation: Ext4 extents describe contiguous block ranges, reducing metadata for large files.

text: What is the log-structured file system design principle?
options: All writes are appended sequentially to a log on disk
Files are stored in a balanced tree by modification time
Data is written to random locations for wear leveling
Files are split into fixed chunks across multiple disks
explanation: LFS writes all data and metadata sequentially, converting random writes to sequential writes.

text: What is copy-on-write in file systems like Btrfs and ZFS?
options: Modified data is written to new locations keeping originals intact
Data is copied to all disks before any modification
Read operations create copies of data in memory cache
Files are duplicated on every access to prevent loss
explanation: COW file systems write modified blocks to new locations, enabling snapshots and atomic updates.

text: How does a B-tree file system improve directory lookup?
options: It provides O(log n) lookup compared to O(n) for linear directories
It stores entries in a hash table for O(1) access
It caches the entire directory in memory
It sorts entries alphabetically using binary search on disk
explanation: B-tree directories provide O(log n) lookup compared to O(n) for linear listings.

text: What is the purpose of the superblock in Unix file systems?
options: It stores metadata about the file system such as size and status
It contains the data of the largest file on the disk
It holds the root user password and security credentials
It caches frequently accessed file data for fast retrieval
explanation: The superblock contains critical file system metadata.

text: How does deduplication reduce storage usage?
options: It identifies and eliminates duplicate data blocks across files
It compresses each file independently with different algorithms
It moves infrequently accessed files to slower tiers
It reduces metadata size by sharing inodes between files
explanation: Deduplication detects duplicate blocks using hashing and replaces copies with references.

text: What is the purpose of the intent log in NTFS?
options: It records planned operations for recovery after crashes
It logs user access attempts for security auditing
It stores the file system compression dictionary
It records disk hardware errors for maintenance
explanation: The NTFS intent log records metadata changes before committing for crash recovery.

text: How does RAID-5 distribute parity for fault tolerance?
options: Parity is distributed across all disks in a rotating pattern
Parity is stored on a single dedicated parity disk
Parity is not used; data is simply mirrored
Parity is calculated but never stored to save space
explanation: RAID-5 distributes parity across all disks, avoiding a dedicated parity disk bottleneck.

text: What is the TRIM command and why is it important for SSDs?
options: It informs the SSD which blocks are no longer in use
It reduces the physical size of the SSD to save power
It trims file metadata to reduce table size
It compresses stored data to extend usable capacity
explanation: TRIM tells the SSD controller which blocks are unused, enabling efficient garbage collection.

text: What is the consistency checker (fsck) and when is it needed?
options: It checks and repairs file system inconsistencies after unclean shutdowns
It runs continuously to monitor performance metrics
It encrypts the file system for unauthorized access protection
It compresses the file system to reclaim wasted space
explanation: Fsck scans for structural inconsistencies from unclean shutdowns and repairs them.

text: What is secondary storage in a computer system?
options: Non-volatile storage that retains data without power
Volatile memory that loses data when power is off
The CPU cache used for fast instruction access
The main memory used by running processes
explanation: Secondary storage permanently retains data even when the computer is powered off.

text: What is the main advantage of secondary storage over primary?
options: It provides larger capacity at lower cost per byte
It provides faster access speed than RAM memory
It is directly accessible by the CPU for execution
It uses less physical space than RAM modules
explanation: Secondary storage offers much larger capacity at significantly lower cost per byte.

text: What is the function of a disk controller?
options: It manages communication between the disk and computer
It stores the operating system boot code permanently
It cools the disk drive to prevent overheating
It compresses data before writing to the disk
explanation: A disk controller manages communication between the system bus and the disk drive.

text: What is seek time on a hard disk drive?
options: Time for the head to move to the correct track
Time for the desired sector to rotate under the head
Time to transfer data from disk to memory
Time to boot the operating system from disk
explanation: Seek time is the time for the read/write head to move to the track where data is located.

text: What is rotational latency on a hard disk?
options: Time for the desired sector to rotate under the head
Time for the disk head to move to correct track
Time to transfer data from disk to controller
Total time to read an entire disk track
explanation: Rotational latency is the time waiting for the desired sector to rotate under the head.

text: What does SSD stand for in storage technology?
options: Solid State Drive using flash memory
Secondary Storage Device using spinning platters
System Storage Disk using magnetic technology
Serial Signal Drive using optical media
explanation: SSD stands for Solid State Drive, using flash memory without moving parts.

text: What is the FCFS disk scheduling algorithm?
options: It services disk requests in the order they arrive
It services the closest request to the head first
It services requests from outermost track inward
It services requests based on their priority levels
explanation: FCFS services disk I/O requests in the exact order they arrive in the queue.

text: What is disk formatting used for?
options: Preparing a disk by creating file system structures
Increasing the rotational speed of disk platters
Adding more storage capacity to an existing drive
Changing the physical size of the disk sectors
explanation: Disk formatting creates the necessary file system data structures on the disk surface.

text: What is the difference between HDD and SSD?
options: HDD uses spinning platters; SSD uses flash memory chips
HDD is faster than SSD in all access patterns
HDD is more expensive per gigabyte than SSD
HDD is smaller in physical size than SSD
explanation: HDD stores data on spinning magnetic platters; SSD stores data on flash memory chips.

text: What is a disk partition?
options: A logical division of a physical disk into sections
A physical crack in the disk surface causing loss
A backup copy of the disk on another device
A connection between two disk drives in the system
explanation: A disk partition is a logical division that can have its own file system.

text: How does SSTF disk scheduling select the next request?
options: It selects the request closest to current head position
It selects the request furthest from head position
It alternates between innermost and outermost requests
It selects requests randomly from the pending queue
explanation: SSTF selects the pending request with minimum seek distance from the current head position.

text: How does the SCAN disk scheduling algorithm work?
options: Head moves in one direction servicing requests then reverses
Head jumps to random tracks to service requests
Head stays on one track until all requests done
Head moves only outward and resets to innermost track
explanation: SCAN moves the head in one direction servicing requests, then reverses, like an elevator.

text: What improvement does C-SCAN offer over regular SCAN?
options: More uniform wait time by only servicing in one direction
It services requests in both directions for throughput
It eliminates all seek time by caching in memory
It prioritizes larger requests over smaller ones
explanation: C-SCAN services requests only while moving in one direction, providing more uniform wait times.

text: What is the LOOK variant of disk scheduling?
options: Head reverses at the last request not at the disk edge
Head checks ahead before moving to reduce travel
Head looks at all requests and picks optimal order
Head moves in a circular pattern around disk center
explanation: LOOK reverses at the last request rather than traveling to the disk edge.

text: What is wear leveling in SSD storage?
options: Distributing writes evenly across all cells to extend life
Reducing write speed to prevent physical wear on chips
Using only a portion of the SSD to avoid wearing cells
Monitoring flash cell temperature during operations
explanation: Wear leveling distributes writes evenly across all flash cells, extending drive lifespan.

text: What is disk striping in RAID?
options: Splitting data across multiple disks for parallel access
Duplicating data on every disk for maximum redundancy
Compressing data before writing to reduce usage
Encrypting data across disks for security purposes
explanation: Disk striping distributes data across multiple disks for parallel read and write operations.

text: What is the difference between RAID 0 and RAID 1?
options: RAID 0 stripes for speed; RAID 1 mirrors for redundancy
RAID 0 provides redundancy; RAID 1 provides speed
RAID 0 uses parity; RAID 1 uses striping
RAID 0 is slower; RAID 1 is faster in all operations
explanation: RAID 0 stripes for performance without redundancy. RAID 1 mirrors for fault tolerance.

text: What is the boot block on a storage device?
options: A reserved area containing code to load the OS
A block storing user data for the first application
A region tracking bad sectors on the disk surface
A section holding the file system free space bitmap
explanation: The boot block contains bootstrap code needed to load the operating system during startup.

text: What is logical block addressing (LBA)?
options: A scheme addressing disk blocks as linear sequence of numbers
A method for physically positioning the disk head
A technique for compressing block addresses
A protocol for transferring blocks between disk and memory
explanation: LBA addresses disk blocks as a linear sequence, abstracting away physical geometry.

text: What is the purpose of disk caching?
options: Storing recently accessed blocks in memory for faster reaccess
Permanently moving disk data into RAM to free space
Compressing blocks before storing on disk surface
Creating backup copies on a separate device
explanation: Disk caching stores recently accessed blocks in memory for fast subsequent access.

text: What is the FTL in SSDs?
options: It maps logical addresses to physical flash locations managing wear
It translates file system calls into controller commands
It converts data between different file system formats
It manages the protocol between SSD and motherboard
explanation: The FTL maps logical block addresses to physical flash locations, handling wear leveling and garbage collection.

text: What is write amplification in SSD storage?
options: Physical data written exceeds logical data requested
Writes are amplified to increase speed on flash
Data is written multiple times for redundancy
The controller increases voltage to speed up writes
explanation: Write amplification occurs when physical writes exceed logical writes due to garbage collection and flash characteristics.

text: How does the deadline I/O scheduler prevent starvation?
options: It assigns expiration deadlines to each request
It always services the oldest request first
It prioritizes write requests over all reads
It batches similar requests and processes together
explanation: The deadline scheduler uses expiration deadlines to ensure long-waiting requests eventually get served.

text: What is over-provisioning in SSDs?
options: Reserved spare capacity for garbage collection and wear leveling
Extra capacity for backup copies of SSD firmware
Additional space for error correction code storage
Spare blocks as temporary cache for read operations
explanation: Over-provisioning reserves hidden capacity for garbage collection, wear leveling, and bad block replacement.

text: How does NVMe improve performance over AHCI?
options: It supports 64K queues with 64K entries using parallel paths
It uses a single deep queue with sequential processing
It bypasses file system to write directly to flash
It compresses all data before writing to reduce size
explanation: NVMe supports up to 65,535 I/O queues with 65,536 entries each, leveraging PCIe directly.

text: What is shingled magnetic recording (SMR) in HDDs?
options: Tracks overlap like shingles to increase storage density
Head reads multiple tracks simultaneously for speed
Data is written in a spiral pattern like vinyl record
Magnetic particles are aligned vertically for density
explanation: SMR overlaps write tracks to increase density, requiring special write management.

text: What is the zoned storage model?
options: Storage divided into zones requiring sequential writes within each
Priority zones assigned to different I/O request types
Isolated storage areas for different user accounts
Temperature zones for SSD thermal throttling
explanation: Zoned storage requires sequential writes within each zone, mapping well to SMR HDDs and QLC SSDs.

text: How does CFQ I/O scheduler work?
options: It assigns a time slice to each process I/O queue for fairness
It merges all requests into one queue sorted by deadline
It prioritizes sequential reads over random writes
It batches requests by disk zone for throughput
explanation: CFQ maintains per-process I/O queues serviced in round-robin with time slices.

text: What is the impact of 4K native vs 512-byte emulated sectors?
options: 4Kn matches modern file systems but may break legacy software
4Kn sectors are slower due to larger transfer size
512e provides better performance than 4Kn always
4Kn and 512e have identical performance
explanation: 4K native sectors match modern OS page sizes better but legacy software may need 512e emulation.

text: What is persistent memory (PMEM)?
options: Byte-addressable non-volatile storage at near-DRAM speed
A faster form of traditional SSD using flash chips
It replaces all CPU cache levels with non-volatile memory
It provides volatile storage cheaper than regular RAM
explanation: Persistent memory offers byte-addressable, non-volatile storage with near-DRAM latency.

text: What is an I/O device in a computer system?
options: Hardware that transfers data to or from the computer
Software that manages the operating system kernel
A type of memory used exclusively by the CPU
A network protocol for internet communication
explanation: An I/O device allows data transfer between the computer and the external world.

text: What is the purpose of a device driver?
options: It provides a software interface between OS and device
It physically powers the hardware device
It stores device data permanently on disk
It connects two hardware devices physically
explanation: A device driver provides a standardized interface between the OS and a specific hardware device.

text: What is an interrupt in the context of I/O?
options: A signal from a device to the CPU indicating an event
A command from the CPU to permanently stop a device
A method of physically disconnecting a device
A way to reset the entire computer system
explanation: An interrupt is a hardware signal from an I/O device notifying the CPU of an event.

text: What is polling in I/O communication?
options: The CPU repeatedly checks a device status register
The device sends an interrupt to CPU when ready
Data transfers directly between device and memory
The OS disables the device until data is needed
explanation: Polling has the CPU repeatedly reading a device status register to check readiness.

text: What does DMA stand for in I/O systems?
options: Direct Memory Access for data transfers
Device Management Architecture for control
Disk Memory Allocation for storage
Dynamic Memory Addressing for caching
explanation: DMA allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to and from memory without continuous CPU involvement.

text: What is a bus in a computer system?
options: A communication pathway connecting hardware components
A software program for managing device drivers
A type of memory for temporary data storage
A network cable connecting computers together
explanation: A bus is a shared communication pathway connecting the CPU, memory, and I/O devices.

text: What is the difference between block and character devices?
options: Block devices transfer in fixed blocks; character devices byte by byte
Block devices are faster; character devices more reliable
Block devices are for input; character devices for output
Block devices are wireless; character devices use cables
explanation: Block devices transfer data in fixed-size blocks; character devices transfer data one character at a time.

text: What is a port in I/O systems?
options: A connection point where a device communicates with the system
A software application that manages network traffic
A type of memory address used by CPU cache
A protocol for encrypting data during transmission
explanation: A port is a connection point through which an I/O device communicates with the system.

text: What is the I/O request lifecycle?
options: Application to driver to controller to device and back
Device sends data directly to application without OS
The CPU handles all I/O without involving the kernel
Data travels from memory to cache to CPU to device
explanation: An I/O request flows from application through kernel to driver to controller to device and back.

text: What is buffering in I/O operations?
options: Temporarily storing data in memory during transfers
Permanently storing data on the hard disk drive
Deleting data after processing by the CPU
Encrypting data before sending to a device
explanation: Buffering temporarily stores data in memory during I/O transfers to handle speed differences.

text: What is the purpose of double buffering in I/O?
options: One buffer fills while the other is processed or emptied
Two buffers store identical copies for redundancy
Both buffers double the transfer speed simultaneously
One buffer for reads and the other for writes only
explanation: Double buffering overlaps I/O and computation by filling one buffer while processing the other.

text: How does interrupt-driven I/O improve over polling?
options: The CPU can do useful work while waiting for device
The CPU constantly checks device status via interrupts
Interrupts eliminate the need for device driver software
Interrupt-driven I/O never uses any CPU cycles
explanation: Interrupt-driven I/O allows the CPU to perform useful work instead of busy-waiting.

text: What is the role of the I/O scheduler?
options: It reorders I/O requests to optimize device access
It creates new I/O devices when first connected
It converts digital signals to analog for output
It encrypts all data before writing to devices
explanation: The I/O scheduler reorders pending requests to minimize seek time and maximize throughput.

text: What is spooling and which device commonly uses it?
options: Buffering output for slow devices; commonly printers
Encrypting data for network devices; commonly routers
Compressing input from fast devices; commonly SSDs
Caching video for displays; commonly monitors
explanation: Spooling buffers data for slow devices. Printers commonly use spooling.

text: What is a scatter-gather DMA operation?
options: DMA transfers data to or from multiple non-contiguous regions
DMA scatters data randomly across entire memory
DMA gathers all memory into a single contiguous block
DMA combines multiple device requests into one transfer
explanation: Scatter-gather DMA transfers data between a device and multiple non-contiguous memory buffers.

text: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous I/O?
options: Synchronous blocks until complete; asynchronous returns immediately
Synchronous is faster; asynchronous is slower always
Synchronous uses polling; asynchronous uses only DMA
Synchronous is for reads; asynchronous for writes only
explanation: Synchronous I/O blocks the process until completion; asynchronous I/O returns control immediately.

text: What is the purpose of device-independent I/O software?
options: It provides uniform interfaces for different device types
It replaces all device drivers with a single generic one
It directly controls hardware without abstraction
It manages only network devices, not storage or input
explanation: Device-independent I/O software provides uniform interfaces abstracting device-specific details.

text: How does I/O completion work with callback functions?
options: The OS calls a registered function when I/O finishes
The application repeatedly checks a status variable
The driver terminates the application when I/O completes
The CPU suspends all operations until I/O is done
explanation: The OS invokes a registered callback function when an asynchronous I/O operation completes.

text: What is the purpose of an ioctl command?
options: It sends device-specific commands not fitting standard interfaces
It initializes all I/O devices during boot
It controls CPU clock speed for I/O operations
It schedules all pending I/O requests in the queue
explanation: Ioctl sends device-specific control commands that do not fit the standard read/write interface.

text: What is the role of an interrupt controller?
options: It manages multiple interrupt signals and prioritizes them
It generates interrupt signals for software programs
It stores interrupt handler code for all devices
It prevents any interrupts from reaching the CPU
explanation: An interrupt controller manages multiple interrupt lines and delivers the highest-priority interrupt to the CPU.

text: What is memory-mapped I/O versus port-mapped I/O?
options: Memory-mapped uses load/store; port-mapped uses special instructions
Memory-mapped is slower; port-mapped provides faster access
Memory-mapped is for input only; port-mapped for output only
Memory-mapped requires DMA; port-mapped uses interrupts only
explanation: Memory-mapped I/O maps device registers to memory addresses; port-mapped uses special CPU instructions.

text: How does io_uring improve Linux I/O performance?
options: It uses shared ring buffers between user and kernel reducing syscalls
It replaces all device drivers with a unified framework
It bypasses the kernel entirely for all I/O
It compresses all I/O data automatically before transfer
explanation: io_uring uses shared submission and completion ring buffers for minimal system call overhead.

text: What is an IOMMU and how does it protect against rogue DMA?
options: It translates device addresses and restricts DMA to allowed regions
It encrypts all data transferred by DMA devices
It disables DMA for all devices except trusted ones
It monitors DMA transfers and logs for auditing
explanation: An IOMMU translates device addresses and restricts DMA to approved memory regions.

text: What is interrupt coalescing and when is it beneficial?
options: Combining multiple interrupts into one to reduce CPU overhead
Splitting one interrupt into many for finer control
Permanently disabling interrupts for high-speed devices
Routing all interrupts to a single CPU core
explanation: Interrupt coalescing batches multiple events into one interrupt, beneficial for high-throughput devices.

text: How does SPDK achieve high I/O performance?
options: It uses user-space drivers with polling instead of kernel interrupts
It increases interrupt frequency to reduce latency
It uses larger DMA buffers for better throughput
It compresses data before writing to reduce I/O
explanation: SPDK moves drivers to user space and uses polling, avoiding kernel context switches.

text: What is the MSI-X interrupt mechanism in PCIe devices?
options: It allows each device many distinct interrupt vectors without sharing
It uses a single shared interrupt line for all PCIe devices
It disables interrupts relying on polling for PCIe
It converts PCIe interrupts into legacy ISA signals
explanation: MSI-X allows PCIe devices up to 2048 distinct interrupt vectors for efficient per-queue handling.

text: What is zero-copy I/O and why is it important?
options: Data moves between device and user buffer without kernel copies
The CPU processes I/O data without loading into registers
I/O operations consume zero CPU time using dedicated hardware
Data is written to device without ever being stored in memory
explanation: Zero-copy I/O eliminates unnecessary copies between kernel and user space buffers.

text: What is device virtualization in I/O systems?
options: Presenting virtual device instances to VMs from shared hardware
Creating software emulations of nonexistent devices
Encrypting device communications for virtual channels
Duplicating physical devices for redundancy and failover
explanation: Device virtualization presents multiple virtual instances from one physical device to VMs.

text: How does the Linux multi-queue block layer improve performance?
options: Per-CPU submission queues mapping to hardware dispatch queues
A single global queue serializing all I/O requests
Eliminating queuing and sending requests directly to devices
One queue per file system mounted on the system
explanation: The multi-queue block layer provides per-CPU queues, eliminating lock contention and scaling with CPU count.

text: What is RDMA and its advantage for I/O?
options: Direct memory access between networked machines without CPU
Remote access to computer disk storage over network
Duplicating all memory to a remote server for backup
Encrypting memory transfers between CPU and RAM
explanation: RDMA enables direct memory-to-memory transfer between networked computers without CPU involvement.

text: What is authentication in computer security?
options: Verifying the identity of a user or system
Encrypting data stored on the hard disk drive
Backing up files to prevent data loss
Monitoring network traffic for performance
explanation: Authentication verifies the identity of a user, process, or device.

text: What is authorization in computer security?
options: Determining what actions an authenticated entity can perform
Verifying the identity of a user through passwords
Encrypting network traffic between two computers
Installing antivirus software on the computer
explanation: Authorization determines what resources and operations an authenticated entity can access.

text: What is a password used for in operating systems?
options: Authenticating a user identity at system login
Encrypting all files stored on the hard drive
Determining the user access level to resources
Monitoring the user activities on the system
explanation: A password is a credential used during authentication to verify a user identity.

text: What is an access control list (ACL)?
options: A list specifying which users have what permissions on a resource
A list of all files currently stored on the system
A log of all system errors that have occurred
A record of all network connections to the server
explanation: An ACL specifies which users or groups have what access permissions to a resource.

text: What is a virus in computer security?
options: Malicious code that attaches to programs and replicates
A hardware malfunction that causes system crashes
A legitimate program monitoring system performance
A network protocol used for secure file transfers
explanation: A computer virus attaches to legitimate programs and replicates by modifying other programs.

text: What is encryption used for in operating systems?
options: Converting data into unreadable format for protection
Compressing files to save disk storage space
Speeding up file access times on the hard drive
Organizing files into directories on the disk
explanation: Encryption converts plaintext into ciphertext, protecting confidentiality of sensitive information.

text: What is a firewall in computer security?
options: A system that monitors and controls network traffic
A program that defragments the hard disk drive
A device that increases internet connection speed
A tool that compresses files for email attachments
explanation: A firewall monitors traffic and decides whether to allow or block it based on security rules.

text: What is the principle of least privilege?
options: Users should have only the minimum permissions needed
Users should be given maximum permissions for convenience
All users should have the same level of system access
Only administrators should have any permissions at all
explanation: Least privilege means every user and process operates with only the minimum permissions needed.

text: What is a Trojan horse in computer security?
options: Malware disguised as legitimate software
A hardware device for network monitoring
A type of encryption algorithm for files
A protocol for secure remote access
explanation: A Trojan horse appears legitimate but secretly performs malicious activities.

text: What is a security patch in operating systems?
options: A software update fixing known security vulnerabilities
A physical lock placed on the computer hardware
A backup copy of the operating system on disk
A network cable providing a secure connection
explanation: A security patch fixes known security vulnerabilities to protect the system from exploitation.

text: What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption?
options: Symmetric uses one key; asymmetric uses public and private pair
Symmetric is always weaker; asymmetric is always stronger
Symmetric is for files; asymmetric for network only
Symmetric is slower; asymmetric is faster for large data
explanation: Symmetric uses one key for both operations; asymmetric uses a public/private key pair.

text: What is a buffer overflow attack?
options: Writing data beyond buffer boundary overwriting adjacent memory
Reading data from buffer faster than it can be filled
Allocating too many buffers causing memory exhaustion
Encrypting buffer contents to prevent unauthorized reading
explanation: A buffer overflow writes beyond allocated size, potentially allowing malicious code injection.

text: How does role-based access control (RBAC) work?
options: Permissions assigned to roles; users assigned to roles
Each user defines their own permissions for all resources
Permissions based solely on time of day and location
All users share the same role with identical permissions
explanation: RBAC assigns permissions to roles rather than individual users; users inherit permissions through roles.

text: What is a denial-of-service (DoS) attack?
options: Overwhelming a system with requests making it unavailable
Stealing user passwords through social engineering
Encrypting user files and demanding ransom
Installing a backdoor for persistent unauthorized access
explanation: A DoS attack floods a system to consume resources, making it unavailable to legitimate users.

text: What is sandboxing in OS security?
options: Running untrusted code in isolated environment with restricted access
Storing encrypted keys in a secure hardware module
Monitoring all network traffic for suspicious patterns
Backing up critical system files to external storage
explanation: Sandboxing runs untrusted programs in a restricted environment with limited resource access.

text: What is the purpose of a digital signature?
options: Verifying authenticity and integrity of a message or document
Encrypting entire contents of a message for privacy
Compressing data to reduce storage and transmission size
Creating a backup copy of important digital documents
explanation: A digital signature verifies that a message was sent by the claimed sender and was not altered.

text: What is a rootkit and why is it dangerous?
options: Malware that hides by modifying the OS to avoid detection
A legitimate tool for administrators to manage servers
A type of antivirus software running with root privileges
A network monitoring tool capturing all traffic data
explanation: A rootkit modifies the OS to hide its presence, making detection extremely difficult.

text: What is two-factor authentication (2FA)?
options: Requiring two different credential types to verify identity
