Using two passwords instead of one for stronger security
Logging in from two different devices simultaneously
Having two administrators approve every login attempt
explanation: 2FA requires two different types of credentials, adding an extra security layer.

text: What is the purpose of an intrusion detection system?
options: Monitoring system activity to detect unauthorized access
Preventing all network traffic from entering the system
Encrypting all data transmitted over the network
Automatically repairing files that have been corrupted
explanation: An IDS monitors for signs of unauthorized access or malicious activity.

text: What is privilege escalation in security attacks?
options: Gaining higher access rights than originally authorized
Reducing a user permissions to restrict their access
Transferring privileges from one account to another
Revoking all administrative privileges from the system
explanation: Privilege escalation gains elevated access beyond initial authorization.

text: How does return-oriented programming (ROP) bypass non-executable stack?
options: It chains existing code gadgets ending in return instructions
It injects new executable code directly onto the stack
It disables the NX bit in page table entries directly
It encrypts the payload to avoid detection by the OS
explanation: ROP chains existing code snippets to perform operations without injecting new code.

text: What is the confused deputy problem in security?
options: A privileged program is tricked into misusing its authority
A user is confused about which password to use
The CPU confuses kernel and user mode during execution
Two security modules conflict causing neither to function
explanation: The confused deputy problem occurs when a privileged program is tricked into unauthorized actions.

text: How does mandatory access control differ from discretionary?
options: MAC enforces system-wide policies; DAC lets owners set permissions
MAC gives all users equal access; DAC restricts completely
MAC is for network resources; DAC for local files only
MAC uses encryption; DAC uses access control lists only
explanation: MAC enforces system-wide policies overriding user preferences; DAC lets owners set permissions.

text: What is a side-channel attack in OS security?
options: Extracting information through physical implementation characteristics
Directly breaking encryption with brute force
Exploiting a known vulnerability in authentication
Intercepting network packets to read unencrypted data
explanation: Side-channel attacks extract information through timing, power consumption, or cache behavior.

text: How does control flow integrity protect against code reuse attacks?
options: It validates execution follows the intended control flow graph
It encrypts all function return addresses on the stack
It prevents any indirect branches from being executed
It randomizes the entire code section on each execution
explanation: CFI validates that control transfers target only valid destinations in the control flow graph.

text: What is the Spectre vulnerability class?
options: It exploits speculative execution to leak data across security boundaries
It exploits buffer overflows in the OS kernel code
It exploits weak encryption in the file system
It exploits race conditions in the scheduler
explanation: Spectre exploits speculative execution to leak data through cache side-channels.

text: What is the Bell-LaPadula model?
options: It enforces confidentiality with no-read-up and no-write-down
It enforces integrity preventing unauthorized modification
It enforces availability ensuring resources are accessible
It enforces authentication requiring multi-factor credentials
explanation: Bell-LaPadula enforces confidentiality: no-read-up and no-write-down.

text: How does Intel SGX provide trusted execution?
options: It creates encrypted memory enclaves the OS cannot access
It runs a trusted OS inside a hardware-managed VM
It encrypts all CPU registers to prevent inspection
It disables all system calls during sensitive execution
explanation: SGX creates hardware-protected enclaves whose contents are encrypted and inaccessible to the OS.

text: What is the Biba integrity model?
options: It enforces integrity with no-write-up and no-read-down
It enforces the same rules as Bell-LaPadula
It combines confidentiality and integrity in one model
It provides availability guarantees Bell-LaPadula lacks
explanation: Biba enforces integrity: no-write-up and no-read-down, complementing Bell-LaPadula confidentiality.

text: What is a Trusted Computing Base (TCB)?
options: All components critical to security; smaller is easier to verify
The base OS installation without any applications
The hardware foundation for compiling all software
The encryption key store protecting all credentials
explanation: The TCB includes all components critical to security. Minimizing it reduces attack surface.
